title: The Cartography of Puddles
text_zh: 雨后的城市总会短暂地变成另一个版本的自己。人行道上的水洼像是大地睁开的眼睛，倒映着被我们习以为常的天空与建筑。我有一个奇怪的习惯——每次雨后散步，我都会刻意寻找那些形状各异的水洼，蹲下来，从它们的视角重新审视这座城市。老城区的水洼最有意思，因为地面的砖石凹凸不平，积水的形状完全取决于百年前铺路工人的手艺。有些水洼浅而宽阔，像一面从容的镜子；有些则狭长幽深，只映出一道窄窄的天光。我曾在一个十字路口的水洼里看到四栋不同年代的建筑同时出现——清末的砖楼、民国的石库门、八十年代的筒子楼、以及刚落成的玻璃幕墙写字楼。它们在现实中互不相邻，却在这一小片积水里亲密无间地挤在一起，像是时间轴被折叠后的产物。一个骑自行车的老人碾过水洼，画面瞬间碎裂成无数涟漪，等水面重新平静，那些建筑又完好无损地拼了回来。
text_pinyin: Yǔ hòu de chéngshì zǒng huì duǎnzàn de biànchéng lìng yī gè bǎnběn de zìjǐ. Rénxíngdào shàng de shuǐwā xiàng shì dàdì zhēng kāi de yǎnjīng, dàoyìng zhe bèi wǒmen xíyǐwéicháng de tiānkōng yǔ jiànzhù. Wǒ yǒu yī gè qíguài de xíguàn——měi cì yǔ hòu sànbù, wǒ dōu huì kèyì xúnzhǎo nàxiē xíngzhuàng gèyì de shuǐwā, dūn xiàlái, cóng tāmen de shìjiǎo chóngxīn shěnshì zhè zuò chéngshì. Lǎo chéngqū de shuǐwā zuì yǒu yìsi, yīnwèi dìmiàn de zhuānshí āotū bùpíng, jīshuǐ de xíngzhuàng wánquán qǔjué yú bǎi nián qián pū lù gōngrén de shǒuyì. Yǒuxiē shuǐwā qiǎn ér kuānkuò, xiàng yī miàn cóngróng de jìngzi; yǒuxiē zé xiácháng yōushēn, zhǐ yìng chū yī dào zhǎi zhǎi de tiānguāng. Wǒ céng zài yī gè shízì lùkǒu de shuǐwā lǐ kàn dào sì dòng bùtóng niándài de jiànzhù tóngshí chūxiàn——Qīng mò de zhuānlóu, Mínguó de shíkùmén, bāshí niándài de tǒngzilóu, yǐjí gāng luòchéng de bōli mùqiáng xiězìlóu. Tāmen zài xiànshí zhōng hù bù xiānglín, què zài zhè yī xiǎo piàn jīshuǐ lǐ qīnmì wújiān de jǐ zài yīqǐ, xiàng shì shíjiān zhóu bèi zhédié hòu de chǎnwù. Yī gè qí zìxíngchē de lǎorén niǎn guò shuǐwā, huàmiàn shùnjiān suìliè chéng wúshù liányī, děng shuǐmiàn chóngxīn píngjìng, nàxiē jiànzhù yòu wánhǎo wúsǔn de pīn le huílái. Wǒ hūrán yìshí dào, shuǐwā shì zhè zuò chéngshì zuì chéngshí de xiàoxiàng huàjiā——tā bù tiāoxuǎn, bù měihuà, zhǐ shì zhōngshí de jìlù tóudǐng shàngfāng qiàhǎo cúnzài de yīqiè. Ér wǒmen dàduōshù rén, zhōng qí yīshēng dōu zài yǎngwàng, què hěn shǎo dītóu qù kàn jiǎo xià zhèxiē zhuǎnshùn jí shì de jìngzi lǐ, cáng zhe zěnyàng yī gè bèi diāndǎo guòlái de shìjiè.
text_en: A city after rain always briefly becomes another version of itself. Puddles on the sidewalk are like eyes the earth has opened, reflecting the sky and buildings we've long taken for granted. I have a strange habit—after every rain, on my walks, I deliberately seek out puddles of various shapes, crouch down, and re-examine the city from their perspective. The puddles in the old district are the most interesting, because the brick and stone surfaces are uneven, and the shape of each pool depends entirely on the craftsmanship of road workers from a century ago. Some puddles are shallow and broad, like an unhurried mirror; others are narrow and deep, reflecting only a slender strip of sky-light. Once, in a puddle at a crossroads, I saw four buildings from different eras appear simultaneously—a late-Qing brick building, a Republican-era shikumen, an eighties-style tube apartment, and a newly completed glass-curtain office tower. In reality they are nowhere near each other, yet in this small patch of standing water they pressed together intimately, like a product of the timeline being folded. An old man on a bicycle rolled through the puddle, the image instantly shattering into countless ripples, and when the surface calmed again those buildings reassembled themselves intact.

questions.q_zh: 作者在十字路口的水洼里看到了什么特别的景象？
questions.q_en: What special sight did the author see in the puddle at the crossroads?

options.text: 四栋不同年代的建筑同时倒映在一片积水中
options.pinyin: sì dòng bùtóng niándài de jiànzhù tóngshí dàoyìng zài yī piàn jīshuǐ zhōng
options.text_en: Four buildings from different eras reflected simultaneously in one pool of water

options.text: 一栋正在拆除的老建筑
options.pinyin: yī dòng zhèngzài chāichú de lǎo jiànzhù
options.text_en: An old building being demolished

options.text: 天空中出现了彩虹
options.pinyin: tiānkōng zhōng chūxiàn le cǎihóng
options.text_en: A rainbow appeared in the sky

questions.explanation: A crossroads puddle reflecting four buildings from different decades simultaneously in a single pool of water.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为水洼是「最诚实的肖像画家」，这个比喻的核心含义是什么？
questions.q_en: The author calls puddles 'the most honest portrait painters'—what is the core meaning of this metaphor?

options.text: 水洼的倒影比照片更清晰
options.pinyin: shuǐwā de dàoyǐng bǐ zhàopiàn gèng qīngxī
options.text_en: Puddle reflections are clearer than photographs

options.text: 水洼不加筛选和修饰地忠实记录头顶的一切
options.pinyin: shuǐwā bù jiā shāixuǎn hé xiūshì de zhōngshí jìlù tóudǐng de yīqiè
options.text_en: Puddles faithfully record everything overhead without selection or embellishment

options.text: 水洼反映了城市最美的一面
options.pinyin: shuǐwā fǎnyìng le chéngshì zuì měi de yī miàn
options.text_en: Puddles reflect the most beautiful side of the city

questions.explanation: Puddles reflect everything above them without selection or embellishment, making them faithful, unedited mirrors of reality.

title: The Night Shift Florist
text_zh: 我家楼下有一间二十四小时营业的花店，老板是个五十多岁的女人，大家都叫她梅姐。白天的花店和别处没什么两样，真正有意思的是深夜以后。凌晨来买花的人，每一个都带着故事。梅姐说她见过最多的是刚下夜班的护士，总是挑一小束淡色的花，说是「给自己的奖励」。还有凌晨三点来的出租车司机，买一支向日葵插在方向盘旁边的水杯里，说这样跑夜班不觉得孤独。最让梅姐动容的是一位老先生，每个月十五号准时在凌晨一点出现，买一束白色的满天星。梅姐曾经忍不住问他为什么总是这个时间来。老先生说，十五号是亡妻的忌日，他习惯在月圆的夜晚走到江边把花放进水里。「白天人太多，」他说，「我不想让别人看到我哭。凌晨的江边只有我和月亮，正好。」梅姐听完沉默了很久，后来每个月十五号都会提前包好一束白色满天星放在柜台角落，不标价格。老先生来了就拿走，从不问多少钱，只是走之前会朝梅姐轻轻点一下头。这个无声的默契持续了三年多。梅姐说她从未觉得自己是在卖花，她觉得自己更像是一个深夜情绪的中转站——那些在白天无处安放的柔软，都在花瓣里找到了暂时的容身之所。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià yǒu yī jiān èrshísì xiǎoshí yíngyè de huādiàn, lǎobǎn shì gè wǔshí duō suì de nǚrén, dàjiā dōu jiào tā Méi jiě. Báitiān de huādiàn hé biéchù méi shénme liǎngyàng, zhēnzhèng yǒu yìsi de shì shēnyè yǐhòu. Língchén lái mǎi huā de rén, měi yī gè dōu dài zhe gùshi. Méi jiě shuō tā jiàn guò zuì duō de shì gāng xià yèbān de hùshi, zǒng shì tiāo yī xiǎo shù dànsè de huā, shuō shì「gěi zìjǐ de jiǎnglì」. Hái yǒu língchén sān diǎn lái de chūzūchē sījī, mǎi yī zhī xiàngrìkuí chā zài fāngxiàngpán pángbiān de shuǐbēi lǐ, shuō zhèyàng pǎo yèbān bú juéde gūdú. Zuì ràng Méi jiě dòngróng de shì yī wèi lǎo xiānshēng, měi gè yuè shíwǔ hào zhǔnshí zài língchén yī diǎn chūxiàn, mǎi yī shù báisè de mǎntiānxīng. Méi jiě céngjīng rěn bú zhù wèn tā wèishéme zǒng shì zhège shíjiān lái. Lǎo xiānshēng shuō, shíwǔ hào shì wáng qī de jìrì, tā xíguàn zài yuèyuán de yèwǎn zǒu dào jiāng biān bǎ huā fàng jìn shuǐ lǐ. 「Báitiān rén tài duō,」tā shuō, 「wǒ bù xiǎng ràng biérén kàn dào wǒ kū. Língchén de jiāng biān zhǐ yǒu wǒ hé yuèliàng, zhènghǎo.」Méi jiě tīng wán chénmò le hěn jiǔ, hòulái měi gè yuè shíwǔ hào dōu huì tíqián bāo hǎo yī shù báisè mǎntiānxīng fàng zài guìtái jiǎoluò, bù biāo jiàgé. Lǎo xiānshēng lái le jiù ná zǒu, cóng bú wèn duōshǎo qián, zhǐ shì zǒu zhīqián huì cháo Méi jiě qīngqīng diǎn yīxià tóu. Zhège wúshēng de mòqì chíxù le sān nián duō. Méi jiě shuō tā cóng wèi juéde zìjǐ shì zài mài huā, tā juéde zìjǐ gèng xiàng shì yī gè shēnyè qíngxù de zhōngzhuǎn zhàn——nàxiē zài báitiān wúchù ānfàng de róuruǎn, dōu zài huābàn lǐ zhǎo dào le zànshí de róngshen zhī suǒ.
text_en: Downstairs from my apartment is a twenty-four-hour flower shop. The owner, Sister Mei, is in her fifties. During the day the shop is ordinary, but after midnight it gets interesting. Every late-night buyer carries a story. Nurses just off shift pick pale flowers—‘a reward for myself.’ A taxi driver comes at three a.m. For a single sunflower to stick in his steering-wheel cup holder, saying it makes the night shift less lonely. What moves Sister Mei most is an elderly gentleman who appears at one a.m. On the fifteenth of every month to buy white baby’s breath. She once asked why always at that hour. The fifteenth was the anniversary of his wife’s death, he said. He walks to the river on full-moon nights to set the flowers on the water. ‘Too many people during the day. I don’t want anyone to see me cry. At dawn by the river it’s just me and the moon—just right.’ Sister Mei was silent a long time. From then on, every fifteenth she wraps a bouquet in advance and leaves it on the counter, unpriced. The old man takes it, never asking how much, only giving her a nod before leaving. This wordless understanding has lasted over three years.

questions.q_zh: 老先生为什么选择凌晨一点来买花？
questions.q_en: Why does the old gentleman choose to buy flowers at one a.m.?

options.text: 因为凌晨花店打折
options.pinyin: yīnwèi língchén huādiàn dǎzhé
options.text_en: Because the flower shop offers discounts at dawn

options.text: 因为他不想让别人看到他在江边哭
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā bù xiǎng ràng biérén kàn dào tā zài jiāng biān kū
options.text_en: Because he doesn't want others to see him crying by the river

options.text: 因为他白天工作太忙
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā báitiān gōngzuò tài máng
options.text_en: Because he's too busy with work during the day

questions.explanation: The old gentleman buys flowers at one a.m. because he 'doesn't want others to see him crying by the river,' choosing privacy for his grief.

questions.q_zh: 梅姐说自己更像是「深夜情绪的中转站」，这句话表达了什么含义？
questions.q_en: What does Sister Mei mean when she says she is more like 'a late-night transfer station for emotions'?

options.text: 她的花店是一个让人在深夜释放白天无法表达的柔软情感的地方
options.pinyin: tā de huādiàn shì yī gè ràng rén zài shēnyè shìfàng báitiān wúfǎ biǎodá de róuruǎn qínggǎn de dìfāng
options.text_en: Her flower shop is a place where people release the tender emotions they cannot express during the day

options.text: 她的花店是深夜唯一营业的商店
options.pinyin: tā de huādiàn shì shēnyè wéiyī yíngyè de shāngdiàn
options.text_en: Her flower shop is the only store open at night

options.text: 她帮深夜的客人传递消息
options.pinyin: tā bāng shēnyè de kèrén chuándì xiāoxi
options.text_en: She helps late-night customers deliver messages

questions.explanation: Sister Mei describes her role as receiving the tender, vulnerable emotions people suppress during daytime hours, making the shop an emotional way station.

title: The Geometry of Clotheslines
text_zh: 在这座城市最老的几个居民区里，阳台之间依然拉着晾衣绳。从楼下抬头望去，那些横跨巷道的绳索构成了一种独特的城市天际线——不是由钢筋水泥勾勒的，而是由棉布、涤纶和阳光共同编织的。我的邻居王婶说，一根晾衣绳能告诉你一户人家的全部秘密。「你看，那家总是晒小孩的衣服，说明家里有老人帮忙带孙子。那家晒的全是衬衫，而且永远熨得笔挺，那是个独居的退休干部。」她指着对面三楼一条绳子上孤零零挂着的一件旧毛衣说：「那个老太太已经走了两个月了，毛衣还在那儿。她儿子大概还没来得及收拾遗物。每次刮风，那件毛衣就晃来晃去的，像是在跟我们打招呼。」我注意到王婶说这话的时候，语气并不伤感，倒更像是在陈述一个社区内部心照不宣的事实。在高层住宅和烘干机普及的今天，晾衣绳正在从城市的视觉词汇里消失。但在这些老巷子里，它们仍然是邻里之间最坦诚的沟通方式——不需要敲门，不需要寒暄，只要抬头看一眼彼此的晾衣绳，就知道对方过得怎么样。
text_pinyin: Zài zhè zuò chéngshì zuì lǎo de jǐ gè jūmínqū lǐ, yángtái zhījiān yīrán lā zhe liàng yī shéng. Cóng lóu xià táitóu wàng qù, nàxiē héngkuà xiàngdào de shéngsuǒ gòuchéng le yī zhǒng dútè de chéngshì tiānjì xiàn——bú shì yóu gāngjīn shuǐní gōulè de, ér shì yóu miánbù, dílún hé yángguāng gòngtóng biānzhī de. Wǒ de línjū Wáng shěn shuō, yī gēn liàng yī shéng néng gàosu nǐ yī hù rénjiā de quánbù mìmì. 「Nǐ kàn, nà jiā zǒng shì shài xiǎohái de yīfu, shuōmíng jiā lǐ yǒu lǎorén bāngmáng dài sūnzi. Nà jiā shài de quán shì chènshān, érqiě yǒngyuǎn yùn de bǐtǐng, nà shì gè dújū de tuìxiū gànbù.」Tā zhǐ zhe duìmiàn sān lóu yī tiáo shéngzi shàng gūlínglíng guà zhe de yī jiàn jiù máoyī shuō: 「Nàge lǎotàitai yǐjīng zǒu le liǎng gè yuè le, máoyī hái zài nàr. Tā érzi dàgài hái méi láidejí shōushi yíwù. Měi cì guā fēng, nà jiàn máoyī jiù huàng lái huàng qù de, xiàng shì zài gēn wǒmen dǎ zhāohu.」Wǒ zhùyì dào Wáng shěn shuō zhè huà de shíhou, yǔqì bìng bù shānggǎn, dào gèng xiàng shì zài chénshù yī gè shèqū nèibù xīnzhào bú xuān de shìshí. Zài gāocéng zhùzhái hé hōnggānjī pǔjí de jīntiān, liàng yī shéng zhèngzài cóng chéngshì de shìjué cíhuì lǐ xiāoshī. Dàn zài zhèxiē lǎo xiàngzi lǐ, tāmen réngrán shì línjū zhījiān zuì tǎnchéng de gōutōng fāngshì——bù xūyào qiāo mén, bù xūyào hánxuān, zhǐyào táitóu kàn yīyǎn bǐcǐ de liàng yī shéng, jiù zhīdào duìfāng guò de zěnmeyàng. Zhè zhǒng yǐ yīwù wéi méijiè de wúshēng duìhuà, huòxǔ shì chéngshìhuà jìnchéng zhōng zuì xiān bèi xīshēng de qīnmì gǎn zhī yī.
text_en: In the oldest residential neighborhoods of this city, clotheslines still stretch between balconies. Looking up from below, the ropes spanning the alleyways form a distinctive urban skyline—not one sketched in steel and concrete, but woven together from cotton, polyester, and sunlight. My neighbor Auntie Wang says a single clothesline can tell you all of a household's secrets. 'Look—that family is always drying children's clothes, which means there are grandparents at home helping with the grandchildren. That one dries nothing but dress shirts, always pressed perfectly crisp—that's a retired cadre living alone.' She pointed to a solitary old sweater hanging on a line on the third floor across the way and said: 'That old lady passed away two months ago, but the sweater is still there. Her son probably hasn't gotten around to sorting through her belongings. Every time the wind blows, that sweater sways back and forth as if waving hello to us.' I noticed that when Auntie Wang said this, her tone wasn't sorrowful—it was more like stating a fact tacitly understood within the community. In today's world of high-rise apartments and widespread dryers, clotheslines are vanishing from the city's visual vocabulary. But in these old alleyways, they remain the most candid form of communication between neighbors—no need to knock on doors, no need for small talk, just glance up at each other's clotheslines and you know how the other is doing.

questions.q_zh: 王婶提到对面三楼那件旧毛衣时，她想表达什么？
questions.q_en: What was Auntie Wang conveying when she mentioned the old sweater on the third floor across the way?

options.text: 那家人太懒，不收衣服
options.pinyin: nà jiā rén tài lǎn, bù shōu yīfu
options.text_en: That family is too lazy to take in their laundry

options.text: 老太太去世后遗物尚未清理，毛衣成了一种无声的告别
options.pinyin: lǎotàitai qùshì hòu yíwù shàng wèi qīnglǐ, máoyī chéng le yī zhǒng wúshēng de gàobié
options.text_en: After the old lady's death her belongings remain uncollected, and the sweater has become a silent farewell

options.text: 毛衣质量太差没人要
options.pinyin: máoyī zhìliàng tài chà méi rén yào
options.text_en: The sweater is too poor quality for anyone to want

questions.explanation: An old sweater still hanging after its owner died, with belongings uncollected, making the garment a silent marker of her absence.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为晾衣绳正在消失的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: According to the author, why are clotheslines disappearing?

options.text: 政府禁止在阳台晾晒衣物
options.pinyin: zhèngfǔ jìnzhǐ zài yángtái liàngshài yīwù
options.text_en: The government has banned drying clothes on balconies

options.text: 高层住宅和烘干机的普及使晾衣绳从城市的视觉词汇中消失
options.pinyin: gāocéng zhùzhái hé hōnggānjī de pǔjí shǐ liàng yī shéng cóng chéngshì de shìjué cíhuì zhōng xiāoshī
options.text_en: The spread of high-rise housing and dryers is erasing clotheslines from the city's visual vocabulary

options.text: 年轻人不再穿需要晾晒的衣服
options.pinyin: niánqīngrén bú zài chuān xūyào liàngshài de yīfu
options.text_en: Young people no longer wear clothes that need line-drying

questions.explanation: Cites high-rise construction and dryer adoption as eliminating clotheslines from urban visual culture, erasing this form of domestic display.

title: The Sound Archive of an Overpass
text_zh: 城南那座老天桥建于一九八七年，三十多年来经历过无数次翻修，但骨架从未变过。每天清晨五点半，环卫工人的扫帚最先唤醒它——竹枝扫过水泥台阶的声音，干燥而均匀，像是一种清晨特有的仪式。六点以后，早市的小贩开始在桥下摆摊，油锅滋滋作响的声音混着蒸笼掀盖时腾起的热汽声，构成了这座天桥一天中最温暖的声音层。到了早高峰，一切都被汽车喇叭和电动车铃铛淹没，天桥本身的声音反而被压到了最底层——只有当你站在桥面正中央，双脚踏在某一块微微翘起的铁板上，才能感受到整座桥体随着车流的节奏在轻微地颤动。下午两三点是天桥最安静的时刻，偶尔有退休老人在桥上靠着栏杆发呆，风穿过栏杆的缝隙发出轻柔的呜咽声，像是一种被城市遗忘的低语。黄昏时分，放学的孩子们蹦蹦跳跳地跑过，书包里的文具盒叮当作响，那是天桥一天中最清脆的声音。夜深以后，偶尔有流浪歌手在桥头弹吉他，旋律飘散在夜空里，和远处最后一班公交车的刹车声遥相呼应。我有时候想，如果一座天桥有记忆，它记住的一定不是自己的外观如何变化，而是三十年来从它身上流过的这些声音——它们比任何建筑材料都更能定义一个地方的灵魂。
text_pinyin: Chéng nán nà zuò lǎo tiānqiáo jiàn yú yī jiǔ bā qī nián, sānshí duō nián lái jīnglì guò wúshù cì fānxiū, dàn gǔjià cóng wèi biàn guò. Měi tiān qīngchén wǔ diǎn bàn, huánwèi gōngrén de sàozhou zuì xiān huànxǐng tā——zhúzhī sǎo guò shuǐní táijiē de shēngyīn, gānzào ér jūnyún, xiàng shì yī zhǒng qīngchén tèyǒu de yíshì. Liù diǎn yǐhòu, zǎoshì de xiǎofàn kāishǐ zài qiáo xià bǎi tān, yóuguō zīzī zuòxiǎng de shēngyīn hùn zhe zhēnglóng xiān gài shí téng qǐ de rèqì shēng, gòuchéng le zhè zuò tiānqiáo yī tiān zhōng zuì wēnnuǎn de shēngyīn céng. Dào le zǎo gāofēng, yīqiè dōu bèi qìchē lǎba hé diàndòngchē língdang yānmò, tiānqiáo běnshēn de shēngyīn fǎn'ér bèi yā dào le zuì dǐcéng——zhǐ yǒu dāng nǐ zhàn zài qiáomiàn zhèng zhōngyāng, shuāng jiǎo tà zài mǒu yī kuài wēiwēi qiào qǐ de tiěbǎn shàng, cái néng gǎnshòu dào zhěng zuò qiáo tǐ suí zhe chēliú de jiézòu zài qīngwēi de chàndòng. Xiàwǔ liǎng sān diǎn shì tiānqiáo zuì ānjìng de shíkè, ǒu'ěr yǒu tuìxiū lǎorén zài qiáo shàng kào zhe lángān fādāi, fēng chuān guò lángān de fèngxì fāchū qīngróu de wūyè shēng, xiàng shì yī zhǒng bèi chéngshì yíwàng de dīyǔ. Huánghūn shífēn, fàngxué de háizimen bèngbèngtiàotiào de pǎo guò, shūbāo lǐ de wénjùhé dīngdāng zuòxiǎng, nà shì tiānqiáo yī tiān zhōng zuì qīngcuì de shēngyīn. Yè shēn yǐhòu, ǒu'ěr yǒu liúlàng gēshǒu zài qiáo tóu tán jítā, xuánlǜ piāosàn zài yèkōng lǐ, hé yuǎnchù zuìhòu yī bān gōngjiāo chē de shāchē shēng yáoxiāng hūyìng. Wǒ yǒu shíhou xiǎng, rúguǒ yī zuò tiānqiáo yǒu jìyì, tā jì zhù de yīdìng bú shì zìjǐ de wàiguān rúhé biànhuà, ér shì sānshí nián lái cóng tā shēnshàng liú guò de zhèxiē shēngyīn——tāmen bǐ rènhé jiànzhù cáiliào dōu gèng néng dìngyì yī gè dìfāng de línghún.
text_en: The old pedestrian overpass in the south of the city was built in 1987. Every morning at five-thirty, sanitation workers’ brooms wake it first—bamboo bristles sweeping concrete steps, dry and even. After six, morning-market vendors set up beneath the bridge: the sizzle of oil pans, the rush of steam escaping a lifted steamer lid—the warmest sonic layer of the overpass’s day. By rush hour, car horns and scooter bells drown everything. Only standing at dead center, both feet on a warped iron plate, can you feel the structure trembling to the rhythm of traffic. Two or three in the afternoon is the quietest moment; a retiree leans on the railing, wind threading through the gaps emits a soft keening, like a whisper the city forgot. At dusk, schoolchildren bounce across, pencil cases clattering in backpacks—the crispest sound of the day. Late at night, a busker strums guitar at the bridgehead, the melody echoing with the last bus’s braking. If an overpass had memory, it would remember not how its appearance changed but these sounds flowing over it for thirty years—they define a place’s soul more than any building material.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为天桥一天中最温暖的声音层出现在什么时候？
questions.q_en: When does the warmest sonic layer of the overpass's day appear, according to the author?

options.text: 黄昏时分孩子们放学经过的时候
options.pinyin: huánghūn shífēn háizimen fàngxué jīngguò de shíhou
options.text_en: At dusk when schoolchildren pass by after school

options.text: 早市小贩在桥下摆摊的时候
options.pinyin: zǎoshì xiǎofàn zài qiáo xià bǎi tān de shíhou
options.text_en: When morning-market vendors set up stalls beneath the bridge

options.text: 深夜流浪歌手弹吉他的时候
options.pinyin: shēnyè liúlàng gēshǒu tán jítā de shíhou
options.text_en: When buskers play guitar late at night

questions.explanation: Morning-market sounds (sizzling oil, escaping steam) as 'the warmest sonic layer,' occurring after sweeping but before rush-hour traffic.

questions.q_zh: 作者最后表达的核心观点是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the author's core idea expressed at the end?

options.text: 老天桥应该被拆除重建
options.pinyin: lǎo tiānqiáo yīnggāi bèi chāichú chóngjiàn
options.text_en: The old overpass should be demolished and rebuilt

options.text: 城市的噪音污染越来越严重
options.pinyin: chéngshì de zàoyīn wūrǎn yuèláiyuè yánzhòng
options.text_en: Urban noise pollution is getting worse

options.text: 声音比建筑材料更能定义一个地方的灵魂
options.pinyin: shēngyīn bǐ jiànzhù cáiliào gèng néng dìngyì yī gè dìfāng de línghún
options.text_en: Sounds define a place's soul more than building materials do

questions.explanation: Concludes that accumulated sounds define a place's character more fundamentally than its physical construction materials.

title: The Philosophy of a Corner Store
text_zh: 我家附近有一间开了二十年的杂货店。过道窄得两个人错身要侧着走。老板姓孙，沉默寡言，从不主动搭话，但他对商品摆放有一种近乎偏执的讲究。他的经营哲学用一句话概括就是：「好的杂货店不是超市的缩小版，是一个社区的生活索引。」他会根据季节调货架——入秋把驱蚊液挪到角落，换上暖水袋和姜茶。梅雨前一周，除湿盒准时出现在最显眼的位置。但真正让我佩服的是他的时间策略。这条街有三家早餐店，所以他早上不急着开门。他的黄金时段是晚上十点以后——饭馆打烊，便利店人满为患，他的小店恰好填补这个缝隙。「开杂货店不是做生意，」他有一次罕见地主动开口，「是做翻译。把这条街上每个人说不出口的需求翻译成货架上的商品。」他指着门口一个不起眼的小篮子——里面放着免费的创可贴和一次性雨衣。「赚不了钱，但下次他们要买东西时会想起这里。」我问他怎么跟隔壁新开的连锁便利店竞争。他看了一眼那家二十四小时营业、灯火通明、门口摆着三个促销展架的便利店说：「他们卖的是商品。我卖的是'孙老板记得你家孩子牛奶过敏所以从来不给你推荐牛奶'这件事。」那家便利店后来开了八个月就关了。孙老板的店还在。门口那个篮子里的创可贴还是免费的。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn yǒu yì jiān kāi le èrshí nián de záhuò diàn. Guòdào zhǎi de liǎng ge rén cuò shēn yào cè zhe zǒu. Lǎobǎn xìng Sūn, chénmò guǎyán, cóng bù zhǔdòng dāhuà, dàn tā duì shāngpǐn bǎifàng yǒu yì zhǒng jìnhū piānzhí de jiǎngjiu. Tā de jīngyíng zhéxué yòng yí jù huà gàikuò jiùshì: "Hǎo de záhuò diàn bú shì chāoshì de suōxiǎo bǎn, shì yí ge shèqū de shēnghuó suǒyǐn." Tā huì gēnjù jìjié diào huòjià — rù qiū bǎ qūwényè nuó dào jiǎoluò, huàn shàng nuǎnshuǐdài hé jiāngchá. Méiyǔ qián yì zhōu, chúshī hé zhǔnshí chūxiàn zài zuì xiǎnyǎn de wèizhi. Dàn zhēnzhèng ràng wǒ pèifú de shì tā de shíjiān cèlüè. Zhè tiáo jiē yǒu sān jiā zǎocān diàn, suǒyǐ tā zǎoshang bù jí zhe kāi mén. Tā de huángjīn shíduàn shì wǎnshang shí diǎn yǐhòu — fànguǎn dǎyàng, biànlìdiàn rén mǎn wéi huàn, tā de xiǎo diàn qiàhǎo tiánbǔ zhège fèngxì. "Kāi záhuò diàn bú shì zuò shēngyi," tā yǒu yí cì hǎnjiàn de zhǔdòng kāikǒu, "shì zuò fānyì. Bǎ zhè tiáo jiē shàng měi ge rén shuō bu chūkǒu de xūqiú fānyì chéng huòjià shàng de shāngpǐn." Tā zhǐ zhe ménkǒu yí ge bù qǐyǎn de xiǎo lánzi — lǐmiàn fàng zhe miǎnfèi de chuāngkētiē hé yícìxìng yǔyī. "Zhuàn bù liǎo qián, dàn xià cì tāmen yào mǎi dōngxi shí huì xiǎng qǐ zhèlǐ." Wǒ wèn tā zěnme gēn gébì xīn kāi de liánsuǒ biànlìdiàn jìngzhēng. Tā kàn le yì yǎn nà jiā èrshísì xiǎoshí yíngyè, dēnghuǒ tōngmíng, ménkǒu bǎi zhe sān ge cùxiāo zhǎnjià de biànlìdiàn shuō: "Tāmen mài de shì shāngpǐn. Wǒ mài de shì 'Sūn lǎobǎn jìde nǐ jiā háizi niúnǎi guòmǐn suǒyǐ cónglái bù gěi nǐ tuījiàn niúnǎi' zhè jiàn shì." Nà jiā biànlìdiàn hòulái kāi le bā ge yuè jiù guān le. Sūn lǎobǎn de diàn hái zài. Ménkǒu nàge lánzi lǐ de chuāngkētiē háishi miǎnfèi de.
text_en: Near my home there's a general store that's been open twenty years. The aisles are so narrow two people have to turn sideways to pass. The owner, surnamed Sun, is taciturn, never initiates conversation, but has an almost obsessive meticulousness about product placement. His business philosophy in one sentence: 'A good general store isn't a miniature supermarket—it's a community's life index.' He adjusts shelves by season—after autumn, mosquito repellent moves to the corner, replaced by hot-water bottles and ginger tea. A week before plum rains, dehumidifier boxes appear in the most prominent spot. But what truly impresses me is his timing strategy. This street has three breakfast shops, so he's in no hurry to open mornings. His golden hours are after ten PM—when restaurants close, convenience stores overflow, and his little shop fills exactly that gap. 'Running a general store isn't doing business,' he once said, on a rare occasion volunteering speech. 'It's doing translation. Translating the needs everyone on this street can't articulate into products on the shelves.' He pointed to an inconspicuous basket by the door—free band-aids and disposable rain ponchos. 'No money in it, but next time they need to buy something they'll think of here.' I asked how he competes with the chain convenience store that just opened next door. He glanced at that twenty-four-hour, brightly lit store with three promotional displays out front and said: 'They sell products. I sell that Boss Sun remembers your kid is allergic to milk so he never recommends milk to you.' That convenience store closed after eight months. Boss Sun's shop is still here. The band-aids in the basket by the door are still free.

questions.q_zh: 孙老板说开杂货店是「做翻译」，他的意思是什么？
questions.q_en: What does Boss Sun mean when he says running a general store is 'doing translation'?

options.text: 他经常帮外国顾客翻译商品说明
options.pinyin: tā jīngcháng bāng wàiguó gùkè fānyì shāngpǐn shuōmíng
options.text_en: He often helps foreign customers translate product descriptions

options.text: 把居民难以表达的生活需求转化为货架上具体的商品
options.pinyin: bǎ jūmín nányǐ biǎodá de shēnghuó xūqiú zhuǎnhuà wéi huòjià shàng jùtǐ de shāngpǐn
options.text_en: Translating residents' hard-to-articulate living needs into concrete products on the shelves

options.text: 他的店同时卖中文和外文书
options.pinyin: tā de diàn tóngshí mài zhōngwén hé wàiwén shū
options.text_en: His store sells both Chinese and foreign-language books

questions.explanation: Boss Sun describes translating residents' unarticulated needs into stocked products—inferring what people want before they explicitly ask for it.

questions.q_zh: 门口小篮子里放免费创可贴和雨衣的做法体现了什么经营理念？
questions.q_en: What business philosophy does the basket of free band-aids and rain ponchos embody?

options.text: 通过免费赠品吸引大量新顾客
options.pinyin: tōngguò miǎnfèi zèngpǐn xīyǐn dàliàng xīn gùkè
options.text_en: Attracting large numbers of new customers through free gifts

options.text: 以不求回报的细微善意建立长期信任和归属感
options.pinyin: yǐ bù qiú huíbào de xìwēi shànyì jiànlì chángqī xìnrèn hé guīshǔ gǎn
options.text_en: Building long-term trust and belonging through small acts of goodwill without expecting return

options.text: 清理库存中卖不出去的商品
options.pinyin: qīnglǐ kùcún zhōng mài bù chūqù de shāngpǐn
options.text_en: Clearing unsellable inventory

questions.explanation: Free band-aids and rain ponchos build goodwill without transactional expectation, cultivating trust and belonging that keeps customers returning long-term.

title: The Patience of Traffic Lights
text_zh: 这座城市有三千多个红绿灯路口，其中大部分已经实现了智能联网，能够根据实时车流量动态调整信号配时。但在老城区的边缘地带，仍然保留着十几个旧式的固定周期信号灯，它们按照多年前设定的节奏，日复一日地重复着同样的循环——红灯四十五秒，绿灯三十秒，黄灯三秒。交通管理部门多次提议更换，但周围居民联名反对。一位在这个路口住了三十年的老太太说得最直接：「我们不需要聪明的红绿灯，我们需要靠得住的红绿灯。我出门买菜，走到路口，心里默数到四十五就知道可以过了。这种确定性是智能系统给不了的。」她的话让我想起了一个更深层的问题——在一个越来越强调「智能」和「优化」的时代，确定性本身是否也是一种正在消失的公共资源？那些被精密算法控制的信号灯确实提高了通行效率，但也制造了一种微妙的不安——你永远不知道这次红灯会持续多久，你只能被动等待。而这个老路口的红绿灯，虽然效率低下，却给了行人一种罕见的主动权：你可以预判、可以规划、可以在自己的节奏里从容地与这座城市协商通行的权利。
text_pinyin: Zhè zuò chéngshì yǒu sānqiān duō gè hónglǜdēng lùkǒu, qízhōng dàbùfèn yǐjīng shíxiàn le zhìnéng liánwǎng, nénggòu gēnjù shíshí chēliúliàng dòngtài tiáozhěng xìnhào pèishí. Dàn zài lǎo chéngqū de biānyuán dìdài, réngrán bǎoliú zhe shí jǐ gè jiùshì de gùdìng zhōuqī xìnhào dēng, tāmen ànzhào duō nián qián shèdìng de jiézòu, rì fù yī rì de chóngfù zhe tóngyàng de xúnhuán——hóng dēng sìshíwǔ miǎo, lǜ dēng sānshí miǎo, huáng dēng sān miǎo. Jiāotōng guǎnlǐ bùmén duō cì tíyì gēnghuàn, dàn zhōuwéi jūmín liánmíng fǎnduì. Yī wèi zài zhège lùkǒu zhù le sānshí nián de lǎotàitai shuō de zuì zhíjiē: 「Wǒmen bù xūyào cōngming de hónglǜdēng, wǒmen xūyào kào de zhù de hónglǜdēng. Wǒ chūmén mǎi cài, zǒu dào lùkǒu, xīnlǐ mò shǔ dào sìshíwǔ jiù zhīdào kěyǐ guò le. Zhè zhǒng quèdìngxìng shì zhìnéng xìtǒng gěi bù liǎo de.」Tā de huà ràng wǒ xiǎng qǐ le yī gè gèng shēncéng de wèntí——zài yī gè yuèláiyuè qiángdiào「zhìnéng」hé「yōuhuà」de shídài, quèdìngxìng běnshēn shìfǒu yě shì yī zhǒng zhèngzài xiāoshī de gōnggòng zīyuán? Nàxiē bèi jīngmì suànfǎ kòngzhì de xìnhào dēng quèshí tígāo le tōngxíng xiàolǜ, dàn yě zhìzào le yī zhǒng wēimiào de bù'ān——nǐ yǒngyuǎn bù zhīdào zhè cì hóng dēng huì chíxù duō jiǔ, nǐ zhǐ néng bèidòng děngdài. Ér zhège lǎo lùkǒu de hónglǜdēng, suīrán xiàolǜ dīxià, què gěi le xíngrén yī zhǒng hǎnjiàn de zhǔdòng quán: nǐ kěyǐ yùpàn, kěyǐ guīhuà, kěyǐ zài zìjǐ de jiézòu lǐ cóngróng de yǔ zhè zuò chéngshì xiéshāng tōngxíng de quánlì. Huòxǔ suǒwèi de zhìhuì chéngshì, bù yīnggāi jǐnjǐn zhuīqiú sùdù yǔ xiàolǜ de zuìdàhuà, ér shì zài mǒuxiē jiǎoluò kèyì bǎoliú yīxiē bènzhuō de, kě yùcè de, shǔyú rén de jiézòu.
text_en: This city has over three thousand signalized intersections, most of which are now smart-connected and can dynamically adjust signal timing based on real-time traffic volume. But on the fringes of the old district, a dozen or so old fixed-cycle traffic lights remain, repeating the same loop day after day according to rhythms set years ago—forty-five seconds of red, thirty seconds of green, three seconds of yellow. The traffic management department has proposed replacing them multiple times, but surrounding residents have collectively opposed it. An old woman who has lived at this intersection for thirty years put it most bluntly: 'We don't need clever traffic lights—we need reliable ones. When I go out to buy groceries and reach the intersection, I count to forty-five in my head and know I can cross. That kind of certainty is something a smart system can't give you.' Her words made me think of a deeper question—in an era that increasingly emphasizes 'intelligence' and 'optimization,' is certainty itself a vanishing public resource? Those signals controlled by precise algorithms do improve throughput, but they also create a subtle unease—you never know how long this red light will last; you can only wait passively. The old intersection's traffic lights, though inefficient, give pedestrians a rare form of agency: you can anticipate, you can plan, you can negotiate your right of passage with the city at your own pace, with composure.

questions.q_zh: 老太太反对更换智能信号灯的核心理由是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the old woman's core reason for opposing the replacement of traffic lights with smart ones?

options.text: 智能信号灯耗电量太大
options.pinyin: zhìnéng xìnhào dēng hào diànliàng tài dà
options.text_en: Smart traffic lights consume too much electricity

options.text: 固定周期信号灯提供了一种智能系统无法替代的确定性
options.pinyin: gùdìng zhōuqī xìnhào dēng tígōng le yī zhǒng zhìnéng xìtǒng wúfǎ tìdài de quèdìngxìng
options.text_en: Fixed-cycle lights provide a certainty that smart systems cannot replace

options.text: 她看不懂智能信号灯的操作界面
options.pinyin: tā kàn bù dǒng zhìnéng xìnhào dēng de cāozuò jièmiàn
options.text_en: She can't understand the smart signal interface

questions.explanation: The old woman values the fixed-cycle light's predictability: knowing exactly how long to wait provides certainty that adaptive smart systems cannot guarantee.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为「智慧城市」应该包含什么要素？
questions.q_en: What element does the author believe a 'wise city' should include?

options.text: 全面淘汰旧设备以追求最高效率
options.pinyin: quánmiàn táotài jiù shèbèi yǐ zhuīqiú zuìgāo xiàolǜ
options.text_en: Completely replacing old equipment to pursue maximum efficiency

options.text: 在某些角落保留笨拙的、可预测的、属于人的节奏
options.pinyin: zài mǒuxiē jiǎoluò bǎoliú bènzhuō de, kě yùcè de, shǔyú rén de jiézòu
options.text_en: Deliberately preserving in certain corners something clumsy, predictable, and human in its rhythm

options.text: 让居民投票决定所有城市基础设施的更新方案
options.pinyin: ràng jūmín tóupiào juédìng suǒyǒu chéngshì jīchǔ shèshī de gēngxīn fāng'àn
options.text_en: Letting residents vote on all urban infrastructure renewal plans

questions.explanation: Wise cities should deliberately preserve some clumsy, predictable, human-scale rhythms rather than optimizing everything for efficiency.

title: The Archaeology of Mailboxes
text_zh: 我所住的这栋楼建于一九九三年，一楼大厅的墙壁上镶嵌着六十个信箱，每个信箱上都有一个小小的铜质门牌号。三十年来，这些信箱经历了从满负荷运转到彻底废弃的完整生命周期。上世纪九十年代，信箱是每天的必经之地——订阅的报纸杂志、远方亲友的来信、偶尔夹杂着一两封让人心跳加速的情书。进入二十一世纪，信箱里开始堆满广告传单和信用卡账单，纸质信件渐渐退场。到了二零一零年代末，快递柜和电子邮件彻底取代了信箱的功能，大部分住户不再清理自己的信箱，任由传单从缝隙里溢出来，像是一个个被遗忘的小型档案馆。但有趣的是，并非所有信箱都被遗弃了。三楼的张老师至今每天都会来开信箱，虽然里面十有八九什么都没有。我有一次好奇地问她为什么，她说那是三十年前搬进来时养成的习惯，每天下楼开信箱已经成了她生活节奏的一部分。「就像有些人起床后一定要先喝一杯水，」她说，「我起床后一定要先去看看信箱。里面有没有信不重要，重要的是这个动作本身让我觉得自己还在期待什么。一个人一旦不再期待，日子就真的只剩下重复了。」我听完之后在想，也许信箱从来就不只是一个接收邮件的容器，它更是一种关于等待和期盼的隐喻——那个每天弯腰查看的姿态，本身就是一种朝向未知敞开的姿势。
text_pinyin: Wǒ suǒ zhù de zhè dòng lóu jiàn yú yī jiǔ jiǔ sān nián, yī lóu dàtīng de qiángbì shàng xiāngqiàn zhe liùshí gè xìnxiāng, měi gè xìnxiāng shàng dōu yǒu yī gè xiǎo xiǎo de tóngzhì ménpái hào. Sānshí nián lái, zhèxiē xìnxiāng jīnglì le cóng mǎn fùhè yùnzhuǎn dào chèdǐ fèiqì de wánzhěng shēngmìng zhōuqī. Shàng shìjì jiǔshí niándài, xìnxiāng shì měi tiān de bì jīng zhī dì——dìngyuè de bàozhǐ zázhì, yuǎnfāng qīnyǒu de láixìn, ǒu'ěr jiāzá zhe yī liǎng fēng ràng rén xīntiào jiāsù de qíngshū. Jìnrù èrshíyī shìjì, xìnxiāng lǐ kāishǐ duī mǎn guǎnggào chuándān hé xìnyòngkǎ zhàngdān, zhǐzhì xìnjiàn jiànjiàn tuìchǎng. Dào le èr líng yī líng niándài mò, kuàidì guì hé diànzǐ yóujiàn chèdǐ qǔdài le xìnxiāng de gōngnéng, dàbùfèn zhùhù bú zài qīnglǐ zìjǐ de xìnxiāng, rènyóu chuándān cóng fèngxì lǐ yìchū lái, xiàng shì yī gè gè bèi yíwàng de xiǎoxíng dàng'ànguǎn. Dàn yǒuqù de shì, bìngfēi suǒyǒu xìnxiāng dōu bèi yíqì le. Sān lóu de Zhāng lǎoshī zhìjīn měi tiān dōu huì lái kāi xìnxiāng, suīrán lǐmiàn shí yǒu bājiǔ shénme dōu méiyǒu. Wǒ yǒu yī cì hàoqí de wèn tā wèishéme, tā shuō nà shì sānshí nián qián bān jìnlái shí yǎng chéng de xíguàn, měi tiān xià lóu kāi xìnxiāng yǐjīng chéng le tā shēnghuó jiézòu de yī bùfèn. 「Jiù xiàng yǒuxiē rén qǐchuáng hòu yīdìng yào xiān hē yī bēi shuǐ,」tā shuō, 「wǒ qǐchuáng hòu yīdìng yào xiān qù kànkan xìnxiāng. Lǐmiàn yǒu méiyǒu xìn bú zhòngyào, zhòngyào de shì zhège dòngzuò běnshēn ràng wǒ juéde zìjǐ hái zài qīdài shénme. Yī gè rén yīdàn bú zài qīdài, rìzi jiù zhēn de zhǐ shèng xià chóngfù le.」Wǒ tīng wán zhīhòu zài xiǎng, yěxǔ xìnxiāng cónglái jiù bù zhǐ shì yī gè jiēshōu yóujiàn de róngqì, tā gèng shì yī zhǒng guānyú děngdài hé qīpàn de yǐnyù——nàge měi tiān wānyāo chákàn de zītài, běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng cháo xiàng wèizhī chǎng kāi de zīshì.
text_en: My building was built in 1993. Sixty mailboxes line the lobby wall, each with a small brass unit number. Over thirty years, these mailboxes have lived a complete cycle from full operation to abandonment. In the nineties, the mailbox was a daily destination—newspapers, letters from relatives, the occasional heart-racing envelope. Into the 2000s, mailboxes filled with advertising flyers and credit-card statements as paper correspondence left the stage. By the late 2010s, parcel lockers and email had replaced the mailbox entirely; most residents stopped clearing theirs, letting flyers overflow like forgotten miniature archives. But not all were abandoned. Teacher Zhang on the third floor still opens her mailbox every day, though nine times out of ten it’s empty. I asked why. She said it was a habit formed thirty years ago. ‘Just as some people must drink water first thing after waking, I must check my mailbox. Whether there’s a letter doesn’t matter—what matters is the act makes me feel I’m still expecting something. Once a person stops expecting, days become nothing but repetition.’

questions.q_zh: 张老师为什么每天还去开信箱？
questions.q_en: Why does Teacher Zhang still open her mailbox every day?

options.text: 她在等一封重要的信
options.pinyin: tā zài děng yī fēng zhòngyào de xìn
options.text_en: She is waiting for an important letter

options.text: 开信箱的动作让她保持对生活的期待感
options.pinyin: kāi xìnxiāng de dòngzuò ràng tā bǎochí duì shēnghuó de qīdài gǎn
options.text_en: The act of opening the mailbox maintains her sense of anticipation for life

options.text: 她需要每天取出广告传单
options.pinyin: tā xūyào měi tiān qǔ chū guǎnggào chuándān
options.text_en: She needs to remove advertising flyers every day

questions.explanation: Teacher Zhang opens her mailbox daily despite knowing it will be empty, because the act itself preserves her sense of anticipation for life.

questions.q_zh: 作者把信箱比作什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author compare the mailbox to?

options.text: 一个被遗忘的小型档案馆
options.pinyin: yī gè bèi yíwàng de xiǎoxíng dàng'ànguǎn
options.text_en: A forgotten miniature archive

options.text: 一种关于等待和期盼的隐喻
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng guānyú děngdài hé qīpàn de yǐnyù
options.text_en: A metaphor about waiting and anticipation

options.text: 一件过时的通信工具
options.pinyin: yī jiàn guòshí de tōngxìn gōngjù
options.text_en: An outdated communication tool

questions.explanation: Frames the mailbox as a metaphor for the human habit of waiting and anticipating, even when the era of letters has passed.

title: The Dialect of Rain Gutters
text_zh: 不同年代的建筑有不同的雨水管，而不同的雨水管在雨天会发出截然不同的声音。这是我住在这个老小区十年后才意识到的一件事。五十年代苏式建筑的铸铁雨水管，管壁厚实，雨水撞击时发出低沉的「咚咚」声，像是大提琴的拨弦，浑厚而有余韵。七八十年代的水泥预制管则完全是另一种质感——雨水在粗糙的管壁内反复弹跳，发出一种沙沙的摩擦声，像是有人在用砂纸打磨什么。九十年代以后普遍使用的PVC塑料管声音最为尖锐清脆，大雨时几乎像是一架失控的爵士鼓，密集而刺耳。最有趣的是那些年久失修、接口处已经松动的老管道——雨水会从缝隙中渗出来，沿着外壁流下，在管道表面形成一层薄薄的水膜，而这层水膜在风的作用下会产生一种极其微弱的「嗡嗡」声，像是管道在低声哼唱一首只有它自己知道的歌。我的朋友是一位声音艺术家，她曾经花了整整一个雨季在这个小区录制雨水管的声音。她说每一根管道都像是一个独特的乐器，它的音色由材质、口径、长度、锈蚀程度和安装角度共同决定。「城市有自己的方言，」她说，「而雨水管是最容易被忽略的那一种。当这些老房子被拆掉的时候，消失的不只是建筑，还有一整套无法复制的声音词汇。」
text_pinyin: Bùtóng niándài de jiànzhù yǒu bùtóng de yǔshuǐ guǎn, ér bùtóng de yǔshuǐ guǎn zài yǔtiān huì fāchū jiérán bùtóng de shēngyīn. Zhè shì wǒ zhù zài zhège lǎo xiǎoqū shí nián hòu cái yìshí dào de yī jiàn shì. Wǔshí niándài Sū shì jiànzhù de zhùtiě yǔshuǐ guǎn, guǎn bì hòushi, yǔshuǐ zhuàngjī shí fāchū dīchén de「dōngdōng」shēng, xiàng shì dàtíqín de bōxián, húnhòu ér yǒu yúyùn. Qī bāshí niándài de shuǐní yùzhì guǎn zé wánquán shì lìng yī zhǒng zhìgǎn——yǔshuǐ zài cūcāo de guǎn bì nèi fǎnfù tántiào, fāchū yī zhǒng shāshā de mócā shēng, xiàng shì yǒu rén zài yòng shāzhǐ dǎmó shénme. Jiǔshí niándài yǐhòu pǔbiàn shǐyòng de PVC sùliào guǎn shēngyīn zuìwéi jiānruì qīngcuì, dà yǔ shí jīhū xiàng shì yī jià shīkòng de juéshì gǔ, mìjí ér cì'ěr. Zuì yǒuqù de shì nàxiē nián jiǔ shī xiū, jiēkǒu chù yǐjīng sōngdòng de lǎo guǎndào——yǔshuǐ huì cóng fèngxì zhōng shèn chūlái, yán zhe wài bì liú xià, zài guǎndào biǎomiàn xíngchéng yī céng báo báo de shuǐ mó, ér zhè céng shuǐ mó zài fēng de zuòyòng xià huì chǎnshēng yī zhǒng jíqí wēiruò de「wēngwēng」shēng, xiàng shì guǎndào zài dīshēng hēngchàng yī shǒu zhǐ yǒu tā zìjǐ zhīdào de gē. Wǒ de péngyǒu shì yī wèi shēngyīn yìshùjiā, tā céngjīng huā le zhěngzhěng yī gè yǔjì zài zhège xiǎoqū lùzhì yǔshuǐ guǎn de shēngyīn. Tā shuō měi yī gēn guǎndào dōu xiàng shì yī gè dútè de yuèqì, tā de yīnsè yóu cáizhì, kǒujìng, chángdù, xiùshí chéngdù hé ānzhuāng jiǎodù gòngtóng juédìng. 「Chéngshì yǒu zìjǐ de fāngyán,」tā shuō, 「ér yǔshuǐ guǎn shì zuì róngyì bèi hūlüè de nà yī zhǒng. Dāng zhèxiē lǎo fángzi bèi chāi diào de shíhou, xiāoshī de bù zhǐ shì jiànzhù, hái yǒu yī zhěng tào wúfǎ fùzhì de shēngyīn cíhuì.」
text_en: Buildings from different eras have different rain gutters, and different gutters produce entirely different sounds in the rain. This is something I only realized after living in this old residential compound for ten years. The cast-iron downpipes on fifties-era Soviet-style buildings have thick walls; when rain strikes them they produce a deep 'dong dong' sound, like the pizzicato of a cello—rich and resonant. The precast concrete pipes of the seventies and eighties have an entirely different texture—rain bounces repeatedly off the rough inner walls, producing a raspy friction sound, as if someone were sanding something with sandpaper. The PVC plastic pipes that became standard after the nineties have the sharpest, crispest sound; in heavy rain they're almost like a runaway jazz drum kit, dense and piercing. Most interesting are the old pipes that have fallen into disrepair, their joints already loose—rain seeps through the gaps, flows down the outer wall, forming a thin film of water on the pipe's surface, and this film, acted upon by the wind, produces an extremely faint humming sound, as if the pipe were softly singing a song only it knows. My friend is a sound artist who once spent an entire rainy season recording rain-gutter sounds in this compound. She said every pipe is like a unique instrument, its timbre determined jointly by material, diameter, length, degree of corrosion, and angle of installation. 'A city has its own dialects,' she said, 'and rain gutters are the most easily overlooked one. When these old buildings are torn down, what disappears is not just architecture but an entire irreproducible vocabulary of sound.'

questions.q_zh: 五十年代苏式建筑的铸铁雨水管发出的声音有什么特点？
questions.q_en: What characterizes the sound of cast-iron rain gutters on 1950s Soviet-style buildings?

options.text: 尖锐刺耳，像爵士鼓
options.pinyin: jiānruì cì'ěr, xiàng juéshì gǔ
options.text_en: Sharp and piercing, like a jazz drum

options.text: 低沉浑厚，像大提琴的拨弦
options.pinyin: dīchén húnhòu, xiàng dàtíqín de bōxián
options.text_en: Deep and rich, like the pizzicato of a cello

options.text: 沙沙的摩擦声，像砂纸打磨
options.pinyin: shāshā de mócā shēng, xiàng shāzhǐ dǎmó
options.text_en: A raspy friction sound, like sandpaper

questions.explanation: Cast-iron pipes on 1950s buildings producing a 'deep dong dong sound, like the pizzicato of a cello—rich and warm.'

questions.q_zh: 声音艺术家朋友说「城市有自己的方言」，她想表达的核心观点是什么？
questions.q_en: What core point does the sound-artist friend express when she says 'a city has its own dialects'?

options.text: 每座城市的居民说不同的方言
options.pinyin: měi zuò chéngshì de jūmín shuō bùtóng de fāngyán
options.text_en: Residents of each city speak different dialects

options.text: 老建筑拆除时不仅失去建筑本身，还失去了独特的、无法复制的声音特征
options.pinyin: lǎo jiànzhù chāichú shí bùjǐn shīqù jiànzhù běnshēn, hái shīqù le dútè de, wúfǎ fùzhì de shēngyīn tèzhēng
options.text_en: When old buildings are demolished, what's lost is not just the architecture but unique, irreproducible sonic characteristics

options.text: 城市应该安装更好的隔音材料
options.pinyin: chéngshì yīnggāi ānzhuāng gèng hǎo de géyīn cáiliào
options.text_en: Cities should install better soundproofing materials

questions.explanation: The sound artist argues each building era's gutters produce unique, irreproducible sounds; demolishing old buildings eliminates these acoustic characteristics forever.

title: The Memory of Sidewalk Trees
text_zh: 这条路上的法国梧桐是一九五六年种的，如今每一棵的树干都粗得需要两个人才能合抱。城市绿化部门的档案里记载着它们的编号、品种和种植日期，但没有记载的是它们各自经历过的那些微小的人间故事。三号树根部的水泥地面有一道被反复修补过的裂缝，那是九十年代一辆失控的自行车撞上来留下的痕迹——骑车的是个赶着去考试的大学生，据说他爬起来之后第一件事不是检查自己有没有受伤，而是确认书包里的准考证还在不在。十七号树的树干上有一处凹陷，那是二零零三年非典期间，社区工作人员在树上钉宣传栏留下的钉孔，十多年过去，树皮已经几乎把它包裹住了，只剩下一个浅浅的酒窝。最让我感触的是四十二号树下的一条长椅。这条长椅是一对老夫妻捐赠的，椅背上嵌着一块小铜牌，刻着「献给在这棵树下相识的五十年」。我后来打听到，老先生已经去世了，老太太每天傍晚还是会来这条长椅上坐一会儿，有时候带一本书，有时候什么都不带，就看着来来往往的行人发呆。管理这条路的园林工人跟我说，他每次修剪四十二号树的时候都会格外小心，尽量不让树枝的阴影偏离那条长椅。「我不知道她在等什么，」他说，「但我能做的就是让那片树荫一直在。」
text_pinyin: Zhè tiáo lù shàng de Fǎguó wútóng shì yī jiǔ wǔ liù nián zhòng de, rújīn měi yī kē de shùgàn dōu cū de xūyào liǎng gè rén cái néng hébào. Chéngshì lǜhuà bùmén de dàng'àn lǐ jìzǎi zhe tāmen de biānhào, pǐnzhǒng hé zhòngzhí rìqī, dàn méiyǒu jìzǎi de shì tāmen gèzì jīnglì guò de nàxiē wēixiǎo de rénjiān gùshi. Sān hào shù gēnbù de shuǐní dìmiàn yǒu yī dào bèi fǎnfù xiūbǔ guò de lièfèng, nà shì jiǔshí niándài yī liàng shīkòng de zìxíngchē zhuàng shànglái liú xià de hénjì——qí chē de shì gè gǎn zhe qù kǎoshì de dàxuéshēng, jùshuō tā pá qǐlái zhīhòu dì yī jiàn shì bú shì jiǎnchá zìjǐ yǒu méiyǒu shòushāng, ér shì quèrèn shūbāo lǐ de zhǔnkǎozhèng hái zài bú zài. Shíqī hào shù de shùgàn shàng yǒu yī chù āoxiàn, nà shì èr líng líng sān nián fēidiǎn qījiān, shèqū gōngzuò rényuán zài shù shàng dīng xuānchuánlán liú xià de dīngkǒng, shí duō nián guòqù, shùpí yǐjīng jīhū bǎ tā bāoguǒ zhù le, zhǐ shèng xià yī gè qiǎnqiǎn de jiǔwō. Zuì ràng wǒ gǎnchù de shì sìshí'èr hào shù xià de yī tiáo chángyǐ. Zhè tiáo chángyǐ shì yī duì lǎo fūqī juānzèng de, yǐbèi shàng qiàn zhe yī kuài xiǎo tóngpái, kè zhe「xiàn gěi zài zhè kē shù xià xiāngshí de wǔshí nián」. Wǒ hòulái dǎting dào, lǎo xiānshēng yǐjīng qùshì le, lǎotàitai měi tiān bàngwǎn háishi huì lái zhè tiáo chángyǐ shàng zuò yīhuìr, yǒu shíhou dài yī běn shū, yǒu shíhou shénme dōu bú dài, jiù kàn zhe láilái wǎngwǎng de xíngrén fādāi. Guǎnlǐ zhè tiáo lù de yuánlín gōngrén gēn wǒ shuō, tā měi cì xiūjiǎn sìshí'èr hào shù de shíhou dōu huì géwài xiǎoxīn, jǐnliàng bú ràng shùzhī de yīnyǐng piānlí nà tiáo chángyǐ. 「Wǒ bù zhīdào tā zài děng shénme,」tā shuō, 「dàn wǒ néng zuò de jiù shì ràng nà piàn shùyīn yīzhí zài.」
text_en: The plane trees along this road were planted in 1956; every trunk now takes two people to wrap their arms around. City records list numbers, species, planting dates, but not the human stories each tree has witnessed. At the base of tree three, a crack repaired repeatedly—a nineties bicycle crash. The rider, a college student rushing to an exam, got up and checked his admission ticket before checking himself. Tree seventeen has a depression—a nail hole from 2003, when community workers pinned a SARS bulletin to the trunk. The bark has nearly swallowed it, leaving a shallow dimple. What touches me most is the bench beneath tree forty-two, donated by an elderly couple. A brass plaque reads: ‘Dedicated to the fifty years since we met under this tree.’ The old gentleman has since passed. His wife still comes every evening—sometimes with a book, sometimes with nothing, just watching passersby. The landscaping worker who manages this stretch told me he is extra careful pruning tree forty-two, keeping the shadow of its branches over that bench. ‘I don’t know what she’s waiting for,’ he said, ‘but what I can do is make sure that shade is always there.’

questions.q_zh: 四十二号树下长椅上的铜牌写了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the brass plaque on the bench under tree forty-two say?

options.text: 「纪念这棵树的种植五十周年」
options.pinyin: 「jìniàn zhè kē shù de zhòngzhí wǔshí zhōunián」
options.text_en: 'Commemorating the fiftieth anniversary of this tree's planting'

options.text: 「献给在这棵树下相识的五十年」
options.pinyin: 「xiàn gěi zài zhè kē shù xià xiāngshí de wǔshí nián」
options.text_en: 'Dedicated to the fifty years since we met under this tree'

title: The Etiquette of Elevator Silence
text_zh: 搬进三十二层高楼以后，我成了一名电梯沉默学的业余研究者。电梯里的沉默至少分四个品种。最常见的是「礼貌性沉默」：进门点头微笑，然后各自盯着楼层数字，全程不说话。这是城市人际关系中最精巧的发明——用零点五秒的面部肌肉运动同时完成「我看到你了」和「我不会打扰你」两个信息的传递。然后是「尴尬性沉默」，出现在刚吵过架的邻居之间。空气中的紧张感可以用手摸到。两个人恨不得把自己缩进电梯的对角线两端，如果电梯是圆形的他们一定会站到地球背面去。第三种是「默契性沉默」，存在于住了很多年的老邻居之间。不说话不是因为没话说，而是因为不需要了——一个眼神就能传达「今天累了」或者「最近还好吧」。有一次停电，电梯困住了我和六楼的陈叔。黑暗里我们被迫打破了多年的沉默。他说他其实一直想告诉我，他挺喜欢我阳台上种的薄荷，每次电梯门开的时候能闻到清香。我说我也一直想告诉他，他每天早上在楼道练太极拳的声音很好听。电来了，门开了，我们相视一笑。然后继续回到各自的沉默里。但那个沉默的配方已经变了：从「不想说」变成了「不用说」。这是第四种——「被温暖填充过的沉默」。最稀有的品种。
text_pinyin: Bān jìn sānshí'èr céng gāolóu yǐhòu, wǒ chéng le yì míng diàntī chénmòxué de yèyú yánjiūzhě. Diàntī lǐ de chénmò zhìshǎo fēn sì ge pǐnzhǒng. Zuì chángjiàn de shì "lǐmàoxìng chénmò": jìn mén diǎntóu wēixiào, ránhòu gèzì dīng zhe lóucéng shùzì, quánchéng bù shuōhuà. Zhè shì chéngshì rénjì guānxì zhōng zuì jīngqiǎo de fāmíng — yòng líng diǎn wǔ miǎo de miànbù jīròu yùndòng tóngshí wánchéng "wǒ kàn dào nǐ le" hé "wǒ bú huì dǎrǎo nǐ" liǎng ge xìnxī de chuándì. Ránhòu shì "gāngàxìng chénmò", chūxiàn zài gāng chǎo guò jià de línjū zhījiān. Kōngqì zhōng de jǐnzhānggǎn kěyǐ yòng shǒu mō dào. Liǎng ge rén hèn bu de bǎ zìjǐ suō jìn diàntī de duìjiǎoxiàn liǎng duān, rúguǒ diàntī shì yuánxíng de tāmen yídìng huì zhàn dào dìqiú bèimiàn qù. Dì sān zhǒng shì "mòqìxìng chénmò", cúnzài yú zhù le hěn duō nián de lǎo línjū zhījiān. Bù shuōhuà bú shì yīnwèi méi huà shuō, ér shì yīnwèi bù xūyào le — yí ge yǎnshén jiù néng chuándá "jīntiān lèi le" huòzhě "zuìjìn hái hǎo ba". Yǒu yí cì tíngdiàn, diàntī kùn zhù le wǒ hé liù lóu de Chén shū. Hēi'àn lǐ wǒmen bèi pò dǎpò le duō nián de chénmò. Tā shuō tā qíshí yìzhí xiǎng gàosu wǒ, tā tǐng xǐhuan wǒ yángtái shàng zhǒng de bòhe, měi cì diàntī mén kāi de shíhou néng wén dào qīngxiāng. Wǒ shuō wǒ yě yìzhí xiǎng gàosu tā, tā měi tiān zǎoshang zài lóudào liàn tàijíquán de shēngyīn hěn hǎotīng. Diàn lái le, mén kāi le, wǒmen xiāngshì yí xiào. Ránhòu jìxù huí dào gèzì de chénmò lǐ. Dàn nàge chénmò de pèifāng yǐjīng biàn le: cóng "bù xiǎng shuō" biàn chéng le "bú yòng shuō". Zhè shì dì sì zhǒng — "bèi wēnnuǎn tiánchōng guò de chénmò". Zuì xīyǒu de pǐnzhǒng.
text_en: After moving into a thirty-two-story tower, I became an amateur researcher in elevator silence studies. Elevator silence has at least four varieties. Most common is 'polite silence': enter, nod, smile, then stare at the floor numbers, zero words the entire ride. This is urban interpersonal relations' most ingenious invention—using zero-point-five seconds of facial muscle movement to simultaneously transmit 'I see you' and 'I won't bother you.' Then there's 'awkward silence,' appearing between neighbors who just argued. The tension in the air is physically tangible. Both parties wish they could shrink into opposite diagonal corners; if the elevator were circular they'd stand on opposite sides of the earth. Third is 'tacit silence,' existing between old neighbors who've lived here for years. They don't speak not because there's nothing to say, but because they no longer need to—one glance conveys 'tired today' or 'doing okay lately.' Once a power outage trapped me in the elevator with Uncle Chen from the sixth floor. In the darkness we were forced to break years of silence. He said he'd actually been wanting to tell me he quite likes the mint on my balcony—every time the elevator doors open he catches a whiff. I said I'd also been meaning to tell him his morning tai chi sounds in the hallway are lovely. Power returned, doors opened, we exchanged a smile. Then went back to our respective silences. But the recipe for that silence had changed: from 'don't want to speak' to 'don't need to speak.' That's the fourth variety—'silence filled with warmth.' The rarest kind.

questions.q_zh: 作者把电梯里的沉默分为哪几种类型？
questions.q_en: What types of elevator silence does the author identify?

options.text: 礼貌性沉默、尴尬性沉默和默契性沉默
options.pinyin: lǐmàoxìng chénmò, gāngàxìng chénmò hé mòqìxìng chénmò
options.text_en: Polite silence, awkward silence, and tacit silence

options.text: 害羞性沉默、恐惧性沉默和习惯性沉默
options.pinyin: hàixiūxìng chénmò, kǒngjùxìng chénmò hé xíguànxìng chénmò
options.text_en: Shy silence, fearful silence, and habitual silence

options.text: 早晨的沉默、中午的沉默和晚上的沉默
options.pinyin: zǎochén de chénmò, zhōngwǔ de chénmò hé wǎnshàng de chénmò
options.text_en: Morning silence, noon silence, and evening silence

questions.explanation: Identifies polite silence (nodding then looking away), awkward silence (between acquaintances with nothing to say), and tacit silence (comfortable shared quiet).

questions.q_zh: 停电后作者和陈叔的沉默发生了什么变化？
questions.q_en: How did the silence between the author and Uncle Chen change after the power outage?

options.text: 他们从此每天在电梯里聊天
options.pinyin: tāmen cóngcǐ měi tiān zài diàntī lǐ liáotiān
options.text_en: They chatted in the elevator every day from then on

options.text: 沉默依然存在，但变成了一种被温暖填充过的安静
options.pinyin: chénmò yīrán cúnzài, dàn biànchéng le yī zhǒng bèi wēnnuǎn tiánchōng guò de ānjìng
options.text_en: The silence remained, but became a quietness filled with warmth

options.text: 他们觉得很尴尬，开始刻意避开对方
options.pinyin: tāmen juéde hěn gāngà, kāishǐ kèyì bìkāi duìfāng
options.text_en: They felt awkward and started deliberately avoiding each other

questions.explanation: After being trapped together during a power outage, their silence transformed from empty politeness to a warm, knowing quietness.

title: The Autobiography of a Bench
text_zh: 公园里有一条被坐得发亮的木质长椅，管理员说它是一九九八年安装的，至今更换过两次椅面木条、三次扶手、一次椅腿螺栓。如果把它比作一个人，那它大约相当于做过两次骨科手术、换过三副假牙、还打了一次石膏。但它的「灵魂」——那个焊接在地面上的铸铁底座——从未被替换过。我常常想象，如果这条长椅会说话，它会讲述怎样的故事。清晨五点，第一个坐上来的总是练完功的老人，他们带着保温杯和收音机，在椅面上留下一层薄薄的茶渍。上午九点左右，年轻的妈妈带着孩子来晒太阳，婴儿车的轮子总会碾过长椅前面同一片泥地，久而久之形成了两道浅浅的车辙。午后最安静的时候，偶尔有人在这里午睡，头枕着扶手，脸上盖着报纸，鞋子脱了放在椅子底下——那是对一个公共空间能给予的最大信任。傍晚是长椅最忙碌的时段，情侣、下棋的老人、刷手机的年轻人轮番使用同一个座位，就像城市生活的流水席。最让我触动的是那些独自来坐的人。他们不看手机，不看书，不和任何人说话，就那样安静地坐着，眼睛望着远处或者什么都不望。我觉得长椅最重要的功能不是供人坐下来做什么，而是允许人坐下来什么都不做——在一个以效率衡量一切的社会里，这种无所事事的权利本身就是一种奢侈。
text_pinyin: Gōngyuán lǐ yǒu yī tiáo bèi zuò de fā liàng de mùzhì chángyǐ, guǎnlǐyuán shuō tā shì yī jiǔ jiǔ bā nián ānzhuāng de, zhìjīn gēnghuàn guò liǎng cì yǐmiàn mùtiáo, sān cì fúshǒu, yī cì yǐtuǐ luóshuān. Rúguǒ bǎ tā bǐzuò yī gè rén, nà tā dàyuē xiāngdāng yú zuò guò liǎng cì gǔkē shǒushù, huàn guò sān fù jiǎyá, hái dǎ le yī cì shígāo. Dàn tā de「línghún」——nàge hànjiē zài dìmiàn shàng de zhùtiě dǐzuò——cóng wèi bèi tìhuàn guò. Wǒ chángcháng xiǎngxiàng, rúguǒ zhè tiáo chángyǐ huì shuōhuà, tā huì jiǎngshù zěnyàng de gùshi. Qīngchén wǔ diǎn, dì yī gè zuò shànglái de zǒng shì liàn wán gōng de lǎorén, tāmen dài zhe bǎowēnbēi hé shōuyīnjī, zài yǐmiàn shàng liú xià yī céng báo báo de chá zì. Shàngwǔ jiǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, niánqīng de māma dài zhe háizi lái shài tàiyáng, yīng'ér chē de lúnzi zǒng huì niǎn guò chángyǐ qiánmiàn tóngyī piàn ní dì, jiǔ ér jiǔ zhī xíngchéng le liǎng dào qiǎnqiǎn de chēzhé. Wǔhòu zuì ānjìng de shíhou, ǒu'ěr yǒu rén zài zhèlǐ wǔshuì, tóu zhěn zhe fúshǒu, liǎn shàng gài zhe bàozhǐ, xiézi tuō le fàng zài yǐzi dǐxia——nà shì duì yī gè gōnggòng kōngjiān néng jǐyǔ de zuìdà xìnrèn. Bàngwǎn shì chángyǐ zuì mánglù de shíduàn, qínglǚ, xià qí de lǎorén, shuā shǒujī de niánqīngrén lúnfān shǐyòng tóngyī gè zuòwèi, jiù xiàng chéngshì shēnghuó de liúshuǐxí. Zuì ràng wǒ chùdòng de shì nàxiē dúzì lái zuò de rén. Tāmen bú kàn shǒujī, bú kàn shū, bù hé rènhé rén shuōhuà, jiù nàyàng ānjìng de zuò zhe, yǎnjīng wàng zhe yuǎnchù huòzhě shénme dōu bú wàng. Wǒ juéde chángyǐ zuì zhòngyào de gōngnéng bú shì gōng rén zuò xiàlái zuò shénme, ér shì yǔnxǔ rén zuò xiàlái shénme dōu bú zuò——zài yī gè yǐ xiàolǜ héngliáng yīqiè de shèhuì lǐ, zhè zhǒng wú suǒ shì shì de quánlì běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng shēchǐ.
text_en: In the park there is a wooden bench polished to a sheen from years of sitting. The groundskeeper says it was installed in 1998: seat slats replaced twice, armrests three times, leg bolts once. But its ‘soul’—the cast-iron base welded into the ground—has never changed. At five a.m., the first to sit are elders who’ve finished morning exercises, carrying thermoses and radios, leaving tea stains on the seat. Around nine, young mothers bring children to bask in the sun, stroller wheels rolling over the same patch of dirt until two shallow ruts form. In the quietest part of the afternoon, someone naps here—newspaper over the face, shoes under the seat—the greatest trust one can give a public space. Evening is the bench’s busiest period: couples, chess-playing elders, phone-scrolling young people take turns, like a buffet of city life. What moves me most are those who sit alone. They don’t look at phones, don’t read, don’t talk—just sit quietly, eyes gazing into the distance or at nothing.

questions.q_en: What does the author consider the bench's most important function?

options.pinyin: yǔnxǔ rén zuò xiàlái shénme dōu bú zuò
options.text_en: Permitting people to sit down and do nothing

options.text: 供人们社交和下棋
options.pinyin: gōng rénmen shèjiāo hé xià qí
options.text_en: For people to socialize and play chess

questions.explanation: Frames the bench's greatest function as 'permitting people to sit down and do nothing'—providing a space for purposeless rest.

questions.q_zh: 作者说午睡的人把鞋脱了放在椅子底下，这说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author mean by saying the napping person takes off their shoes and puts them under the bench?

options.text: 这个人很不讲卫生
options.pinyin: zhège rén hěn bù jiǎng wèishēng
options.text_en: This person has poor hygiene

options.text: 这是对公共空间能给予的最大信任
options.pinyin: zhè shì duì gōnggòng kōngjiān néng jǐyǔ de zuìdà xìnrèn
options.text_en: This is the greatest trust one can give a public space

options.text: 公园里不允许穿鞋
options.pinyin: gōngyuán lǐ bù yǔnxǔ chuān xié
options.text_en: The park doesn't allow wearing shoes

questions.explanation: Someone removing shoes and placing them under the bench as an act of complete trust in the safety and hospitality of the public space.

title: The Taxonomy of Window Light
text_zh: 深夜从对面的高楼望过去，每一扇亮着灯的窗户都是一个微型剧场。我曾经花了整整一个冬天，在自己的阳台上观察对面那栋楼的窗灯变化，像一个不带望远镜的业余天文学家，用肉眼辨认着属于人间的星座。暖黄色的灯光最为常见，那通常是客厅的吸顶灯或台灯，意味着有人还在活动——看电视、做家务、或者只是等一个晚归的家人。冷白色的荧光灯光几乎可以确定是在加班——那种灯光有一种不带感情的均匀明亮，像是把黑夜强行切割出一块白天的碎片。最温柔的是那种若有若无的蓝色微光，通常来自手机或平板电脑的屏幕，说明主人已经上床但还没有入睡，正在黑暗中刷着最后几条消息。偶尔会出现一种快速闪烁的彩色光——那是有人在深夜看电影或打游戏。最让我着迷的是那些在凌晨两三点突然亮起的灯。它们总是很突兀，像夜空中划过的流星，让人忍不住猜测背后的原因——是被噩梦惊醒？是婴儿的哭声？还是忽然想起了一件放不下的事？这些灯通常在十几分钟后熄灭，仿佛那个短暂的清醒只是梦境的延伸。我后来意识到，我在做的事情本质上是一种城市版的观星——每一扇窗户都是一颗恒星，每一盏灯都是它此刻的光谱。你无法知道那颗星球上发生了什么具体的故事，但光的颜色、亮度和持续时间已经足以勾勒出一个生活的轮廓。
text_pinyin: Shēnyè cóng duìmiàn de gāolóu wàng guòqù, měi yī shàn liàng zhe dēng de chuānghù dōu shì yī gè wēixíng jùchǎng. Wǒ céngjīng huā le zhěngzhěng yī gè dōngtiān, zài zìjǐ de yángtái shàng guānchá duìmiàn nà dòng lóu de chuāng dēng biànhuà, xiàng yī gè bú dài wàngyuǎnjìng de yèyú tiānwénxuéjiā, yòng ròuyǎn biànrèn zhe shǔyú rénjiān de xīngzuò. Nuǎn huángsè de dēngguāng zuìwéi chángjiàn, nà tōngcháng shì kètīng de xīdǐng dēng huò táidēng, yìwèi zhe yǒu rén hái zài huódòng——kàn diànshì, zuò jiāwù, huòzhě zhǐ shì děng yī gè wǎn guī de jiārén. Lěng báisè de yíngguāng dēngguāng jīhū kěyǐ quèdìng shì zài jiābān——nà zhǒng dēngguāng yǒu yī zhǒng bú dài gǎnqíng de jūnyún míngliàng, xiàng shì bǎ hēiyè qiángxíng qiēgē chū yī kuài báitiān de suìpiàn. Zuì wēnróu de shì nà zhǒng ruòyǒuruòwú de lánsè wēiguāng, tōngcháng láizì shǒujī huò píngbǎn diànnǎo de píngmù, shuōmíng zhǔrén yǐjīng shàng chuáng dàn hái méiyǒu rùshuì, zhèngzài hēi'àn zhōng shuā zhe zuìhòu jǐ tiáo xiāoxi. Ǒu'ěr huì chūxiàn yī zhǒng kuàisù shǎnshuò de cǎisè guāng——nà shì yǒu rén zài shēnyè kàn diànyǐng huò dǎ yóuxì. Zuì ràng wǒ zháomí de shì nàxiē zài língchén liǎng sān diǎn tūrán liàng qǐ de dēng. Tāmen zǒng shì hěn tūwù, xiàng yèkōng zhōng huà guò de liúxīng, ràng rén rěn bú zhù cāicè bèihòu de yuányīn——shì bèi èmèng jīngxǐng? Shì yīng'ér de kū shēng? Háishi hūrán xiǎng qǐ le yī jiàn fàng bú xià de shì? Zhèxiē dēng tōngcháng zài shí jǐ fēnzhōng hòu xīmiè, fǎngfú nàge duǎnzàn de qīngxǐng zhǐ shì mèngjìng de yánshēn. Wǒ hòulái yìshí dào, wǒ zài zuò de shìqíng běnzhì shàng shì yī zhǒng chéngshì bǎn de guānxīng——měi yī shàn chuānghù dōu shì yī kē héngxīng, měi yī zhǎn dēng dōu shì tā cǐkè de guāngpǔ. Nǐ wúfǎ zhīdào nà kē xīngqiú shàng fāshēng le shénme jùtǐ de gùshi, dàn guāng de yánsè, liàngdù hé chíxù shíjiān yǐjīng zúyǐ gōulè chū yī gè shēnghuó de lúnkuò.
text_en: Looking across to the high-rise opposite late at night, every lit window is a miniature theater. I spent one winter on my balcony observing window-light changes in the building across the way, like an amateur astronomer discerning constellations of the human world. Warm yellow light is most common—a ceiling lamp or table lamp, meaning someone is still up: watching TV, doing housework, or waiting for a family member late coming home. Cool white fluorescent light means someone working overtime—emotionless, uniform brightness, as if forcing a shard of daytime from the night. The gentlest is a faint blue glow from a phone or tablet—the occupant in bed but not yet asleep, scrolling through last messages in the dark. Occasionally a rapidly flickering colored light—someone watching a movie or gaming. What fascinates me most are lights that suddenly come on at two or three a.m. They are always abrupt, making you guess: nightmare? A baby’s cry? Something remembered that won’t let go? These lights usually go out after ten minutes, as if the wakefulness were an extension of a dream. Every window is a star, every lamp its spectrum. You can’t know the specific story on that planet, but color, brightness, and duration sketch the outline of a life.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为冷白色的荧光灯光通常意味着什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author believe cool white fluorescent light usually means?

options.text: 有人在厨房做饭
options.pinyin: yǒu rén zài chúfáng zuòfàn
options.text_en: Someone is cooking in the kitchen

options.text: 有人在加班
options.pinyin: yǒu rén zài jiābān
options.text_en: Someone is working overtime

options.text: 有人刚起床
options.pinyin: yǒu rén gāng qǐchuáng
options.text_en: Someone just woke up

questions.explanation: Cool white fluorescent light 'means someone is working overtime,' associating its harsh quality with labor extending past normal hours.

questions.q_zh: 作者把观察窗灯比作什么活动？
questions.q_en: What activity does the author compare watching window lights to?

options.text: 看电影
options.pinyin: kàn diànyǐng
options.text_en: Watching movies

options.text: 城市版的观星
options.pinyin: chéngshì bǎn de guānxīng
options.text_en: A city version of stargazing

options.text: 侦探破案
options.pinyin: zhēntàn pò àn
options.text_en: A detective solving cases

questions.explanation: Compares observing lit windows across the way to stargazing—reading individual human stories from points of light in the darkness.

title: The Tenderness of Repair Shops
text_zh: 在快递驿站和外卖平台统治了街面的时代，城南那条小巷里还活着三家修理铺——修鞋的老周、修伞的林师傅、以及修钟表的何老太。他们像是城市新陈代谢系统里最后的抵抗者，坚守着一个正在消亡的信念：东西坏了应该修，而不是扔。老周的修鞋铺只有两平方米大小，工具挂满了墙壁，空气里弥漫着皮革和胶水的味道。他说现在来修鞋的人越来越少了，年轻人穿坏一双鞋的第一反应是打开购物App重新下单。「他们不是不珍惜东西，」老周说得很平静，「是这个时代教会了他们，新的永远比修过的便宜。」但老周的生意并没有因此消失，反而在最近几年出现了一种微妙的变化——来找他的人开始带着情感而不仅仅是鞋子。一个年轻女孩抱着一双已经磨得面目全非的帆布鞋来，说那是大学时男朋友送的，分手了但鞋还想留着。一个中年男人带来一双老式的手工布鞋，是他过世母亲做的最后一双。老周接过鞋的时候手特别轻，像是在接一件瓷器。他说他不知道从什么时候起，自己修的不再是鞋，而是别人的回忆。「鞋可以买新的，但缝在鞋底上的那些路是买不到的。我修的是那些路。」林师傅的修伞铺也有类似的故事，何老太修的钟表更是如此——每一块被送来的旧表背后，都有一段关于时间的私人叙事。这三家修理铺，在某种意义上，是这条小巷里最后的情感考古学家。
text_pinyin: Zài kuàidì yìzhàn hé wàimài píngtái tǒngzhì le jiēmiàn de shídài, chéng nán nà tiáo xiǎo xiàng lǐ hái huó zhe sān jiā xiūlǐ pù——xiū xié de Lǎo Zhōu, xiū sǎn de Lín shīfu, yǐjí xiū zhōngbiǎo de Hé lǎotài. Tāmen xiàng shì chéngshì xīnchén dàixiè xìtǒng lǐ zuìhòu de dǐkàng zhě, jiānshǒu zhe yī gè zhèngzài xiāowáng de xìnniàn: dōngxi huài le yīnggāi xiū, ér bú shì rēng. Lǎo Zhōu de xiū xié pù zhǐ yǒu liǎng píngfāng mǐ dàxiǎo, gōngjù guà mǎn le qiángbì, kōngqì lǐ mímàn zhe pígé hé jiāoshuǐ de wèidào. Tā shuō xiànzài lái xiū xié de rén yuèláiyuè shǎo le, niánqīngrén chuān huài yī shuāng xié de dì yī fǎnyìng shì dǎkāi gòuwù App chóngxīn xià dān. 「Tāmen bú shì bù zhēnxī dōngxi,」Lǎo Zhōu shuō de hěn píngjìng, 「shì zhège shídài jiàohuì le tāmen, xīn de yǒngyuǎn bǐ xiū guò de piányi.」Dàn Lǎo Zhōu de shēngyì bìng méiyǒu yīncǐ xiāoshī, fǎn'ér zài zuìjìn jǐ nián chūxiàn le yī zhǒng wēimiào de biànhuà——lái zhǎo tā de rén kāishǐ dài zhe qínggǎn ér bù jǐnjǐn shì xiézi. Yī gè niánqīng nǚhái bào zhe yī shuāng yǐjīng mó de miànmù quánfēi de fānbù xié lái, shuō nà shì dàxué shí nánpéngyǒu sòng de, fēnshǒu le dàn xié hái xiǎng liú zhe. Yī gè zhōngnián nánrén dài lái yī shuāng lǎoshì de shǒugōng bù xié, shì tā guòshì mǔqīn zuò de zuìhòu yī shuāng. Lǎo Zhōu jiē guò xié de shíhou shǒu tèbié qīng, xiàng shì zài jiē yī jiàn cíqì. Tā shuō tā bù zhīdào cóng shénme shíhou qǐ, zìjǐ xiū de bú zài shì xié, ér shì biérén de huíyì. 「Xié kěyǐ mǎi xīn de, dàn féng zài xiédǐ shàng de nàxiē lù shì mǎi bú dào de. Wǒ xiū de shì nàxiē lù.」Lín shīfu de xiū sǎn pù yě yǒu lèisì de gùshi, Hé lǎotài xiū de zhōngbiǎo gèng shì rúcǐ——měi yī kuài bèi sòng lái de jiù biǎo bèihòu, dōu yǒu yī duàn guānyú shíjiān de sīrén xùshì. Zhè sān jiā xiūlǐ pù, zài mǒu zhǒng yìyì shàng, shì zhè tiáo xiǎo xiàng lǐ zuìhòu de qínggǎn kǎogǔ xuéjiā.
text_en: In an era of delivery platforms, three repair shops survive in that southern alley—Old Zhou the cobbler, Master Lin the umbrella mender, Granny He the watchmaker. They cling to a dying belief: when something breaks, fix it, don’t throw it away. Old Zhou’s shop is two square meters, tools covering every wall, the air thick with leather and glue. Fewer people come now; a young person’s first reaction to worn shoes is opening a shopping app. ‘It’s not that they don’t value things,’ Old Zhou says. ‘This era taught them new is always cheaper than repaired.’ But his business has shifted. The people who come now bring emotions, not just shoes. A young woman brought canvas shoes worn beyond recognition—her college boyfriend gave them to her; they’d broken up but she wanted to keep the shoes. A middle-aged man brought handmade cloth shoes, the last pair his late mother made. Old Zhou took them with unusual gentleness, as if receiving porcelain. What he repairs is no longer shoes—it’s memories. ‘You can buy new shoes, but you can’t buy the roads stitched into the soles. I’m repairing those roads.’ Master Lin’s umbrella shop has similar stories, Granny He’s watches even more—behind every old timepiece lies a private narrative about time.

questions.q_zh: 老周说「我修的是那些路」，这句话是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does Old Zhou mean when he says 'I'm repairing those roads'?

options.text: 他同时也做道路维修的工作
options.pinyin: tā tóngshí yě zuò dàolù wéixiū de gōngzuò
options.text_en: He also does road maintenance work

options.text: 他修复的是鞋子承载的记忆和经历
