options.text_en: He is restoring the memories and experiences the shoes carry

options.text: 他专门修复走坏路磨损的鞋底
options.pinyin: tā zhuānmén xiūfù zǒu huài lù mósǔn de xiédǐ
options.text_en: He specializes in repairing soles worn by rough roads

questions.explanation: Old Zhou states that shoes carry their owner's journeys; repairing them means restoring those accumulated memories and experiences, not just fixing leather.

questions.q_zh: 作者把三家修理铺称为「情感考古学家」，这个比喻强调了什么？
questions.q_en: The author calls the three repair shops 'emotional archaeologists'—what does this metaphor emphasize?

options.text: 他们的修理技术非常古老
options.pinyin: tāmen de xiūlǐ jìshù fēicháng gǔlǎo
options.text_en: Their repair techniques are very ancient

options.text: 他们通过修复旧物挖掘和保存人们寄托在物品上的情感记忆
options.pinyin: tāmen tōngguò xiūfù jiùwù wājué hé bǎocún rénmen jìtuō zài wùpǐn shàng de qínggǎn jìyì
options.text_en: Through repairing old objects they excavate and preserve the emotional memories people invest in their belongings

options.text: 他们的店铺里陈列着很多古董
options.pinyin: tāmen de diànpù lǐ chénliè zhe hěn duō gǔdǒng
options.text_en: Their shops display many antiques

questions.explanation: The metaphor emphasizes that repair work excavates and preserves the emotional investments people have made in their belongings over time.

title: The Grammar of Alleyway Cats
text_zh: 这个老城区的流浪猫形成了一套精密的社会体系，而这套体系的运作逻辑竟然与人类社区的治理结构有着惊人的相似。巷口杂货店门前的那只橘猫是这片区域公认的「片区长」，它拥有整条巷子的优先觅食权和最好的午睡位置——杂货店老板娘放在门口的那把旧藤椅。其他猫想要穿过它的领地，必须遵循一套不成文的通行礼仪：低头、放慢脚步、不直视它的眼睛。如果有外来的猫闯入，橘猫并不会立刻发动攻击，而是会先在对方身边慢慢绕圈走，用体态语言完成一次「身份审查」。大多数外来者在这种无声的压力下会自行离开，极少数坚持留下的则需要经历几天的「观察期」——在此期间，它们只能在巷子边缘的几个指定位置活动。最让我惊叹的是猫群内部的互助机制。有一只瘸了一条腿的黑猫，其他猫吃完之后总会给它留一些食物，不是出于同情，更像是一种自然而然的默契。一位长年投喂流浪猫的邻居告诉我，猫的社会比人类想象的要复杂得多。「它们有自己的外交、自己的边境、自己的福利体系，」她说，「唯一跟人类不同的是，它们从不开会。」我有时候在想，也许我们在城市治理上遇到的很多难题，这些巷弄里的猫早就用自己的方式解决了——不需要条文，不需要投票，只需要在日复一日的相处中，磨合出一套所有成员都心照不宣的规则。
text_pinyin: Zhège lǎo chéngqū de liúlàng māo xíngchéng le yī tào jīngmì de shèhuì tǐxì, ér zhè tào tǐxì de yùnzuò luójí jìngrán yǔ rénlèi shèqū de zhìlǐ jiégòu yǒu zhe jīngrén de xiāngsì. Xiàng kǒu záhuòdiàn mén qián de nà zhī júmāo shì zhè piàn qūyù gōngrèn de「piànqūzhǎng」, tā yōngyǒu zhěng tiáo xiàngzi de yōuxiān mìshí quán hé zuìhǎo de wǔshuì wèizhì——záhuòdiàn lǎobǎnniáng fàng zài ménkǒu de nà bǎ jiù téngyǐ. Qítā māo xiǎng yào chuān guò tā de lǐngdì, bìxū zūnxún yī tào bù chéngwén de tōngxíng lǐyí: dītóu, fàng màn jiǎobù, bù zhíshì tā de yǎnjīng. Rúguǒ yǒu wàilái de māo chuǎngrù, júmāo bìng bú huì lìkè fādòng gōngjī, ér shì huì xiān zài duìfāng shēnbiān mànmàn rào quān zǒu, yòng tǐtài yǔyán wánchéng yī cì「shēnfèn shěnchá」. Dàduōshù wàilái zhě zài zhè zhǒng wúshēng de yālì xià huì zìxíng líkāi, jí shǎoshù jiānchí liú xià de zé xūyào jīnglì jǐ tiān de「guānchá qī」——zài cǐ qījiān, tāmen zhǐ néng zài xiàngzi biānyuán de jǐ gè zhǐdìng wèizhì huódòng. Zuì ràng wǒ jīngtàn de shì māo qún nèibù de hùzhù jīzhì. Yǒu yī zhī qué le yī tiáo tuǐ de hēi māo, qítā māo chī wán zhīhòu zǒng huì gěi tā liú yīxiē shíwù, bú shì chūyú tóngqíng, gèng xiàng shì yī zhǒng zìrán ér rán de mòqì. Yī wèi cháng nián tóuwèi liúlàng māo de línjū gàosu wǒ, māo de shèhuì bǐ rénlèi xiǎngxiàng de yào fùzá de duō. 「Tāmen yǒu zìjǐ de wàijiāo, zìjǐ de biānjìng, zìjǐ de fúlì tǐxì,」tā shuō, 「wéiyī gēn rénlèi bùtóng de shì, tāmen cóng bù kāihuì.」Wǒ yǒu shíhou zài xiǎng, yěxǔ wǒmen zài chéngshì zhìlǐ shàng yù dào de hěn duō nántí, zhèxiē xiàngnòng lǐ de māo zǎo jiù yòng zìjǐ de fāngshì jiějué le——bù xūyào tiáowén, bù xūyào tóupiào, zhǐ xūyào zài rì fù yī rì de xiāngchǔ zhōng, móhé chū yī tào suǒyǒu chéngyuán dōu xīnzhào bú xuān de guīzé.
text_en: The stray cats of this old district have formed an intricate social system whose logic resembles human governance. The orange tabby at the corner shop is the recognized ‘district chief,’ enjoying priority foraging and the best napping spot—the rattan chair the shopkeeper’s wife leaves by the door. Cats passing through its territory must observe unwritten transit etiquette: lower the head, slow the pace, avoid eye contact. When an outsider intrudes, the orange tabby doesn’t attack but circles the newcomer slowly, completing an ‘identity inspection’ through body language. Most outsiders leave under this silent pressure; the rare few who stay must endure a days-long ‘observation period,’ confined to marginal spots at the alley’s edges. What astonishes me most is the mutual-aid mechanism. A black cat with a lame leg always finds food left by the others after eating—not pity, but a natural, unspoken understanding. A neighbor who feeds the strays told me cat society is far more complex than humans imagine. ‘They have their own diplomacy, borders, welfare system,’ she said. ‘The only difference from humans is they never hold meetings.’

questions.q_zh: 橘猫在面对外来猫入侵时是如何反应的？
questions.q_en: How does the orange tabby react when an outside cat intrudes?

options.text: 立刻发动攻击
options.pinyin: lìkè fādòng gōngjī
options.text_en: Immediately attacks

options.text: 在对方身边慢慢绕圈走，用体态语言进行身份审查
options.pinyin: zài duìfāng shēnbiān mànmàn rào quān zǒu, yòng tǐtài yǔyán jìnxíng shēnfèn shěnchá
options.text_en: Slowly circles the newcomer, conducting an identity inspection through body language

options.text: 躲起来不敢出面
options.pinyin: duǒ qǐlái bù gǎn chūmiàn
options.text_en: Hides and doesn't dare come out

questions.explanation: The orange tabby slowly circling newcomers, using body language to assess identity before allowing or denying passage through its territory.

questions.q_zh: 邻居说猫的社会与人类社会「唯一不同的是」什么？
questions.q_en: According to the neighbor, what is the 'only difference' between cat society and human society?

options.text: 猫不会使用工具
options.pinyin: māo bú huì shǐyòng gōngjù
options.text_en: Cats don't use tools

options.text: 猫从不开会
options.pinyin: māo cóng bù kāihuì
options.text_en: Cats never hold meetings

options.text: 猫没有语言
options.pinyin: māo méiyǒu yǔyán
options.text_en: Cats have no language

questions.explanation: The neighbor's observation highlights that cats achieve comparable social organization without bureaucratic overhead—the 'only difference' being the absence of meetings.

title: The Retirement of a Phone Booth
text_zh: 市政府去年宣布将分批拆除全市剩余的两百三十个公共电话亭，理由是维护成本远超使用率。消息传出后，一个叫「电话亭守护者联盟」的民间团体在社交媒体上发起了一场保留运动，收集了四千多条关于电话亭的个人记忆。其中有一条来自一位中学语文老师的投稿让我久久不能忘怀。她说她上大学的时候，每个周末都会去学校门口的电话亭给远在农村的父母打电话。那时候手机还没有普及，打长途要用磁卡，一张五十元的磁卡大约能通话十五分钟。为了在有限的时间里说完所有想说的话，她每次打电话之前都会在纸上列一个提纲——先汇报学习成绩，再问家里的庄稼收成，最后用剩下的两三分钟聊一些无关紧要的家常。她说正是那种时间的紧迫感，让每一句话都变得慎重而珍贵。「现在微信聊天可以说一天，反而什么都没说，」她写道，「那时候十五分钟要把全部的想念压缩进去，每一个字都是经过筛选的。那种密度的交流，在即时通讯的时代已经不存在了。」另一位投稿者是个出租车司机，他说二十年前他第一次从车站出来到一座陌生的城市闯荡，身上只有一百块钱。他在火车站广场的电话亭里给老家的朋友打了一个电话，那通电话帮他找到了第一份工作。「那个电话亭是我在这座城市扎根的起点，」他说，「拆了它，就像拆了我人生的第一块地基。」
text_pinyin: Shì zhèngfǔ qùnián xuānbù jiāng fēn pī chāichú quán shì shèngyú de liǎng bǎi sānshí gè gōnggòng diànhuàtíng, lǐyóu shì wéihù chéngběn yuǎn chāo shǐyònglǜ. Xiāoxi chuánchū hòu, yī gè jiào「Diànhuàtíng Shǒuhù Zhě Liánméng」de mínjiān tuántǐ zài shèjiāo méitǐ shàng fāqǐ le yī chǎng bǎoliú yùndòng, shōují le sìqiān duō tiáo guānyú diànhuàtíng de gèrén jìyì. Qízhōng yǒu yī tiáo láizì yī wèi zhōngxué yǔwén lǎoshī de tóugǎo ràng wǒ jiǔjiǔ bù néng wànghuái. Tā shuō tā shàng dàxué de shíhou, měi gè zhōumò dōu huì qù xuéxiào ménkǒu de diànhuàtíng gěi yuǎn zài nóngcūn de fùmǔ dǎ diànhuà. Nà shíhou shǒujī hái méiyǒu pǔjí, dǎ chángtú yào yòng cíkǎ, yī zhāng wǔshí yuán de cíkǎ dàyuē néng tōnghuà shíwǔ fēnzhōng. Wèile zài yǒuxiàn de shíjiān lǐ shuō wán suǒyǒu xiǎng shuō de huà, tā měi cì dǎ diànhuà zhīqián dōu huì zài zhǐ shàng liè yī gè tígāng——xiān huìbào xuéxí chéngjì, zài wèn jiā lǐ de zhuāngjia shōuchéng, zuìhòu yòng shèng xià de liǎng sān fēnzhōng liáo yīxiē wúguān jǐnyào de jiācháng. Tā shuō zhèng shì nà zhǒng shíjiān de jǐnpògǎn, ràng měi yī jù huà dōu biàn de shènzhòng ér zhēnguì. 「Xiànzài Wēixìn liáotiān kěyǐ shuō yī tiān, fǎn'ér shénme dōu méi shuō,」tā xiě dào, 「nà shíhou shíwǔ fēnzhōng yào bǎ quánbù de xiǎngniàn yāsuō jìnqù, měi yī gè zì dōu shì jīngguò shāixuǎn de. Nà zhǒng mìdù de jiāoliú, zài jíshí tōngxùn de shídài yǐjīng bù cúnzài le.」Lìng yī wèi tóugǎo zhě shì gè chūzūchē sījī, tā shuō èrshí nián qián tā dì yī cì cóng chēzhàn chūlái dào yī zuò mòshēng de chéngshì chuǎngdàng, shēn shàng zhǐ yǒu yībǎi kuài qián. Tā zài huǒchēzhàn guǎngchǎng de diànhuàtíng lǐ gěi lǎojiā de péngyǒu dǎ le yī gè diànhuà, nà tōng diànhuà bāng tā zhǎo dào le dì yī fèn gōngzuò. 「Nàge diànhuàtíng shì wǒ zài zhè zuò chéngshì zhā gēn de qǐdiǎn,」tā shuō, 「chāi le tā, jiù xiàng chāi le wǒ rénshēng de dì yī kuài dìjī.」
text_en: The city announced last year it would dismantle the remaining two hundred and thirty public phone booths. A grassroots ‘Phone Booth Guardians Alliance’ launched a preservation campaign, collecting over four thousand personal memories. One submission, from a middle-school Chinese teacher, has stayed with me. In college, every weekend she called her parents from the phone booth outside the school gate. Long-distance calls required magnetic cards—fifty yuan bought about fifteen minutes. To fit everything in, she’d outline a script beforehand: report grades, ask about the harvest, then use the last two or three minutes for small talk. ‘That urgency made every sentence precious,’ she wrote. ‘Now you can chat on WeChat all day and say nothing. Back then you compressed all your longing into fifteen minutes; every word was chosen. That density no longer exists.’ Another contributor, a taxi driver, said twenty years ago he stepped out of the train station into a strange city with a hundred yuan. He called a friend back home from the station-square phone booth; that call got him his first job. ‘That phone booth was the starting point of my putting down roots here. Tearing it down is like tearing out the first foundation stone of my life.’

questions.q_zh: 语文老师打电话前列提纲的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: Why did the Chinese teacher write an outline before making calls?

options.text: 因为她性格内向，不擅长即兴聊天
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā xìnggé nèixiàng, bú shàncháng jíxìng liáotiān
options.text_en: Because she was introverted and not good at impromptu conversation

options.text: 因为磁卡通话时间有限，她需要在十五分钟内说完所有想说的话
options.pinyin: yīnwèi cíkǎ tōnghuà shíjiān yǒuxiàn, tā xūyào zài shíwǔ fēnzhōng nèi shuō wán suǒyǒu xiǎng shuō de huà
options.text_en: Because the card's talk time was limited and she needed to say everything within fifteen minutes

options.text: 因为她的父母听力不好，需要提前准备好说什么
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā de fùmǔ tīnglì bù hǎo, xūyào tíqián zhǔnbèi hǎo shuō shénme
options.text_en: Because her parents had poor hearing and she needed to prepare what to say

questions.explanation: The teacher wrote outlines because her phone card had limited minutes, requiring her to plan what to say within fifteen minutes.

questions.q_zh: 出租车司机为什么反对拆除那个电话亭？
questions.q_en: Why does the taxi driver oppose demolishing that phone booth?

options.text: 他现在还经常使用公共电话
options.pinyin: tā xiànzài hái jīngcháng shǐyòng gōnggòng diànhuà
options.text_en: He still frequently uses public phones

options.text: 那个电话亭是他在这座城市扎根的起点，承载着他人生的重要记忆
options.pinyin: nàge diànhuàtíng shì tā zài zhè zuò chéngshì zhā gēn de qǐdiǎn, chéngzài zhe tā rénshēng de zhòngyào jìyì
options.text_en: That phone booth was the starting point of his life in the city, carrying important memories

options.text: 他觉得拆除电话亭会影响市容
options.pinyin: tā juéde chāichú diànhuàtíng huì yǐngxiǎng shìróng
options.text_en: He thinks demolishing phone booths would affect the cityscape

questions.explanation: The taxi driver's first city call was made from that phone booth; it marks the origin of his urban life and carries irreplaceable personal significance.

title: The Diplomacy of Shared Walls
text_zh: 筒子楼里邻居之间只隔一面薄砖墙，隔音效果约等于用报纸隔开两间房。你的闹钟几点响、晚上几点睡觉——隔壁一清二楚。住户们对此发展出一条不成文的核心条款：哪怕你清清楚楚听见隔壁夫妻吵架的全部细节，第二天碰面也必须表演完全不知情。但共墙外交不只是装聋作哑。冬天夜里你咳嗽不止，隔壁会轻敲三下墙：「你还好吗？」回敲两下：「没事。」不回应的话，十分钟内一定有人来敲门。这套暗号是搬进来第一天隔壁刘大姐教我的。她说：「住在这种房子里，墙不是用来隔开人的，是用来连接人的。」后来刘大姐搬走了。新邻居搬进来的第一天晚上就开了外放的短视频，声音大得像在我床头开了个电影院。我犹豫了很久，最后敲了三下墙。对面安静了。然后回了两下。共墙外交的协议就这样完成了交接。
text_pinyin: Tǒngzilóu lǐ línjū zhījiān zhǐ gé yí miàn báo zhuān qiáng, géyīn xiàoguǒ yuē děngyú yòng bàozhǐ gé kāi liǎng jiān fáng. Nǐ de nàozhōng jǐ diǎn xiǎng, wǎnshang jǐ diǎn shuìjiào — gébì yì qīng èr chǔ. Zhùhù men duì cǐ fāzhǎn chū yì tiáo bù chéng wén de héxīn tiáokuǎn: nǎpà nǐ qīngqīngchǔchǔ tīngjiàn gébì fūqī chǎojià de quánbù xìjié, dì èr tiān pèngmiàn yě bìxū biǎoyǎn wánquán bù zhīqíng. Dàn gòng qiáng wàijiāo bù zhǐ shì zhuāng lóng zuò yǎ. Dōngtiān yè lǐ nǐ késou bù zhǐ, gébì huì qīng qiāo sān xià qiáng: "Nǐ hái hǎo ma?" Huí qiāo liǎng xià: "Méi shì." Bù huíyìng dehuà, shí fēnzhōng nèi yídìng yǒurén lái qiāo mén. Zhè tào ànhào shì bān jìnlái dì yī tiān gébì Liú dàjiě jiāo wǒ de. Tā shuō: "Zhù zài zhè zhǒng fángzi lǐ, qiáng bú shì yòng lái gé kāi rén de, shì yòng lái liánjiē rén de." Hòulái Liú dàjiě bān zǒu le. Xīn línjū bān jìnlái de dì yī tiān wǎnshang jiù kāi le wàifàng de duǎn shìpín, shēngyīn dà de xiàng zài wǒ chuángtóu kāi le ge diànyǐngyuàn. Wǒ yóuyù le hěn jiǔ, zuìhòu qiāo le sān xià qiáng. Duìmiàn ānjìng le. Ránhòu huí le liǎng xià. Gòng qiáng wàijiāo de xiéyì jiù zhèyàng wánchéng le jiāojiē.
text_en: In a tube apartment, neighbors are separated by a thin brick wall with soundproofing roughly equivalent to dividing two rooms with newspaper. What time your alarm rings, when you go to bed—next door knows everything. Residents have developed one unwritten core provision: even if you heard every detail of the couple next door's argument, the next day you must perform complete ignorance. But shared-wall diplomacy isn't just feigned deafness. On winter nights when you can't stop coughing, three gentle knocks come from next door: 'Are you okay?' Knock back twice: 'I'm fine.' No response, and within ten minutes someone's at your door. This code was taught to me by Big Sister Liu next door on my first day. She said: 'In this kind of building, the wall isn't there to separate people—it's there to connect them.' Later Big Sister Liu moved away. The new neighbor, on their very first night, played short videos on speaker so loud it was like having a cinema on my pillow. I hesitated a long time, then knocked the wall three times. It went quiet. Then two knocks came back. The shared-wall diplomacy protocol had been handed over.

questions.q_zh: 筒子楼住户之间「声音的已读不回原则」是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does the 'read but don't reply' principle regarding sound mean among tube-apartment residents?

options.text: 邻居说话时假装没听到，以此保护彼此的隐私和尊严
options.pinyin: línjū shuōhuà shí jiǎzhuāng méi tīng dào, yǐcǐ bǎohù bǐcǐ de yǐnsī hé zūnyán
options.text_en: Pretending not to hear what neighbors say, thereby protecting each other's privacy and dignity

options.text: 邻居之间不允许互相交谈
options.pinyin: línjū zhījiān bù yǔnxǔ hùxiāng jiāotán
options.text_en: Neighbors are not allowed to talk to each other

options.text: 邻居收到消息后不需要回复
options.pinyin: línjū shōu dào xiāoxi hòu bù xūyào huífù
options.text_en: Neighbors don't need to reply after receiving messages

questions.explanation: Residents hearing everything through thin walls but performing complete ignorance the next day, mutually protecting privacy through willful deafness.

questions.q_zh: 刘大姐说「墙不是用来隔开人的，是用来连接人的」，她想表达什么？
questions.q_en: What does Big Sister Liu mean by saying 'the wall isn't there to separate people—it's there to connect them'?

options.text: 薄墙隔音差是一件好事
options.pinyin: báo qiáng géyīn chà shì yī jiàn hǎo shì
options.text_en: Thin walls with poor soundproofing are a good thing

options.text: 通过敲墙暗号等方式，薄墙反而成了邻里间传递关心和善意的媒介
options.pinyin: tōngguò qiāo qiáng ànhào děng fāngshì, báo qiáng fǎn'ér chéng le línjū jiān chuándì guānxīn hé shànyì de méijiè
options.text_en: Through wall-knocking codes and other means, the thin wall becomes a medium for transmitting care and goodwill between neighbors

options.text: 应该把墙拆掉让邻居直接面对面
options.pinyin: yīnggāi bǎ qiáng chāi diào ràng línjū zhíjiē miànduìmiàn
options.text_en: The wall should be torn down so neighbors can interact face to face

questions.explanation: Explains that neighbors develop wall-knocking codes and other signals, transforming the thin wall from a separator into a medium for transmitting care.

title: The Thermodynamics of a Public Library
text_zh: 这座区级图书馆在夏天有一个众所周知的秘密——它是附近三个小区里唯一免费开放空调的公共空间。每年七八月份，阅览室的人数会暴涨三倍，但真正来看书的大约只占一半。剩下的一半是来避暑的——退休老人带着象棋和保温杯、打工的年轻人带着笔记本电脑和充电宝、放暑假的孩子带着零食和漫画书。馆长陈老师对此心知肚明，但她从不驱赶这些「非典型读者」。她说公共图书馆的第一功能不是藏书，而是庇护。「一个城市的文明程度不是看它建了多少图书馆，而是看图书馆允许多少种人走进来。」她唯一的要求是保持安静——不管你来做什么，请把音量控制在不影响别人的范围内。这个简单的规则创造了一种奇妙的共处状态：下棋的老人用手势代替落子的声音，看视频的年轻人戴着耳机默默笑，甚至那些带孩子来的家长也学会了用纸条跟孩子交流。有一个细节特别打动我——图书馆的饮水机旁边放着一个小纸盒，里面装着免费的纸杯，纸盒上写着「献给忘记带杯子的朋友」。陈老师说这是一个常来避暑的老大爷自发准备的，他每周自费买一包纸杯放在那里。「他说他年轻的时候穷，大热天连个喝水的地方都难找。现在他不缺钱了，但他不想让别人经历他当年的窘迫。」陈老师停了停说：「你看，图书馆教人的不只是知识，还有怎么在一个公共空间里做一个体面的人。」
text_pinyin: Zhè zuò qūjí túshūguǎn zài xiàtiān yǒu yī gè zhòngsùozhōuzhī de mìmì——tā shì fùjìn sān gè xiǎoqū lǐ wéiyī miǎnfèi kāifàng kōngtiáo de gōnggòng kōngjiān. Měi nián qī bā yuèfèn, yuèlǎnshì de rénshù huì bàozhǎng sān bèi, dàn zhēnzhèng lái kàn shū de dàyuē zhǐ zhàn yībàn. Shèng xià de yībàn shì lái bìshǔ de——tuìxiū lǎorén dài zhe xiàngqí hé bǎowēnbēi, dǎgōng de niánqīngrén dài zhe bǐjìběn diànnǎo hé chōngdiànbǎo, fàng shǔjià de háizi dài zhe língshí hé mànhuà shū. Guǎnzhǎng Chén lǎoshī duìcǐ xīnzhīdùmíng, dàn tā cóng bù qūgǎn zhèxiē「fēi diǎnxíng dúzhě」. Tā shuō gōnggòng túshūguǎn de dì yī gōngnéng bú shì cángshū, ér shì bìhù. 「Yī gè chéngshì de wénmíng chéngdù bú shì kàn tā jiàn le duōshǎo túshūguǎn, ér shì kàn túshūguǎn yǔnxǔ duōshǎo zhǒng rén zǒu jìnlái.」Tā wéiyī de yāoqiú shì bǎochí ānjìng——bùguǎn nǐ lái zuò shénme, qǐng bǎ yīnliàng kòngzhì zài bù yǐngxiǎng biérén de fànwéi nèi. Zhège jiǎndān de guīzé chuàngzào le yī zhǒng qímiào de gòngchǔ zhuàngtài: xià qí de lǎorén yòng shǒushì dàitì luòzǐ de shēngyīn, kàn shìpín de niánqīngrén dài zhe ěrjī mòmò xiào, shènzhì nàxiē dài háizi lái de jiāzhǎng yě xuéhuì le yòng zhǐtiáo gēn háizi jiāoliú. Yǒu yī gè xìjié tèbié dǎdòng wǒ——túshūguǎn de yǐnshuǐjī pángbiān fàng zhe yī gè xiǎo zhǐhé, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe miǎnfèi de zhǐbēi, zhǐhé shàng xiě zhe「xiàn gěi wàngjì dài bēizi de péngyǒu」. Chén lǎoshī shuō zhè shì yī gè cháng lái bìshǔ de lǎo dàyé zìfā zhǔnbèi de, tā měi zhōu zìfèi mǎi yī bāo zhǐbēi fàng zài nàlǐ. 「Tā shuō tā niánqīng de shíhou qióng, dà rè tiān lián gè hē shuǐ de dìfāng dōu nán zhǎo. Xiànzài tā bù quē qián le, dàn tā bù xiǎng ràng biérén jīnglì tā dāngnián de jiǒngpò.」Chén lǎoshī tíng le tíng shuō: 「Nǐ kàn, túshūguǎn jiào rén de bù zhǐ shì zhīshi, hái yǒu zěnme zài yī gè gōnggòng kōngjiān lǐ zuò yī gè tǐmiàn de rén.」
text_en: This district library has a well-known summer secret—it is the only free air-conditioned public space in three surrounding compounds. Every July and August, the reading room’s headcount triples, but only half are there to read. The rest come to escape the heat—retirees with chess sets, young workers with laptops, children with snacks and comics. Director Chen never evicts these ‘atypical readers.’ She says a library’s primary function is not housing books but sheltering people. ‘A city’s civilization is measured not by how many libraries it builds, but by how many kinds of people its libraries allow in.’ Her only requirement: quiet. Whatever you come to do, keep the volume within a range that doesn’t affect others. This rule creates a wondrous coexistence: chess-playing elders replace the sound of placing pieces with hand gestures, video-watching young people smile silently in headphones, parents communicate with children via notes. One detail moved me—beside the water dispenser sits a box of free paper cups: ‘For friends who forgot to bring a cup.’ Director Chen says an elderly regular provides them, buying a pack weekly with his own money. ‘He says when he was young and poor, on scorching days he could barely find drinking water. He doesn’t want others to feel that embarrassment.’

questions.q_zh: 陈馆长认为公共图书馆的第一功能是什么？
questions.q_en: What does Director Chen consider the primary function of a public library?

options.text: 收藏和保护珍贵书籍
options.pinyin: shōucáng hé bǎohù zhēnguì shūjí
options.text_en: Collecting and preserving precious books

options.text: 庇护——允许各种人走进来
options.pinyin: bìhù——yǔnxǔ gè zhǒng rén zǒu jìnlái
options.text_en: Shelter—allowing all kinds of people to walk in

options.text: 提供安静的学习环境
options.pinyin: tígōng ānjìng de xuéxí huánjìng
options.text_en: Providing a quiet study environment

questions.explanation: Director Chen states a library's primary function is 'shelter—allowing all kinds of people to walk in,' prioritizing access over academic gatekeeping.

questions.q_zh: 老大爷每周自费买纸杯放在饮水机旁边的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: Why does the elderly man buy paper cups with his own money every week?

options.text: 因为图书馆经费不够
options.pinyin: yīnwèi túshūguǎn jīngfèi bú gòu
options.text_en: Because the library doesn't have enough funds

options.text: 因为他年轻时穷得连喝水的地方都难找，不想让别人经历同样的窘迫
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā niánqīng shí qióng de lián hē shuǐ de dìfāng dōu nán zhǎo, bù xiǎng ràng biérén jīnglì tóngyàng de jiǒngpò
options.text_en: Because when he was young he was too poor to find a place to drink water, and he doesn't want others to experience the same embarrassment

options.text: 因为图书馆要求每个读者自备杯子
options.pinyin: yīnwèi túshūguǎn yāoqiú měi gè dúzhě zìbèi bēizi
options.text_en: Because the library requires every reader to bring their own cup

questions.explanation: Explains the man's childhood experience of being too poor to find drinking water motivates him to ensure no one else faces that same embarrassment.

title: The Meteorology of a Neighborhood Committee
text_zh: 居委会主任老方有一套自己的「社区气象预报」系统——他不看天气App，而是观察社区里的各种微妙征兆来预判接下来会发生什么事。「广场舞大妈们突然换歌了，说明她们内部刚完成一次权力交接，」他说，「音响设备归谁管、歌单谁来定、位置怎么排，这些都是政治。」他最准确的一次预判是在去年——他注意到小区里遛狗的人突然增加了三成，而且大部分是中年男人。他判断这不是养宠物的热潮，而是家庭矛盾的信号。「中年男人在家里坐不住，找借口出来散心，最方便的理由就是遛狗。遛狗时间超过四十分钟的，基本上都是在外面躲清静。」果然，接下来两个月，居委会收到的家庭调解申请比平时多了一倍。他还有一套关于垃圾桶的观察理论。「垃圾桶里开始出现大量外卖盒的楼层，说明那一层有住户最近在加班或者独居的人情绪不好——因为正常情况下人们还是会自己做饭的。」最让我佩服的是他处理邻里纠纷的方式——他从不急于调解，而是先让双方各自讲完，然后找一个「共同的不满」作为切入点。「让吵架的两个人一起骂物业，」他笑着说，「他们就会忘记自己为什么吵架了。」老方说他做了十八年居委会工作，最大的心得是：「管理社区不是管理人，是管理人和人之间那些看不见的缝隙。缝隙太大了会漏风，太小了会挤压。我的工作就是把每一条缝隙都调整到刚好让空气流通的宽度。」
text_pinyin: Jūwěihuì zhǔrèn Lǎo Fāng yǒu yī tào zìjǐ de「shèqū qìxiàng yùbào」xìtǒng——tā bú kàn tiānqì App, ér shì guānchá shèqū lǐ de gè zhǒng wēimiào zhēngzhào lái yùpàn jiēxiàlái huì fāshēng shénme shì. 「Guǎngchǎngwǔ dāmāmen tūrán huàn gē le, shuōmíng tāmen nèibù gāng wánchéng yī cì quánlì jiāojiē,」tā shuō, 「yīnxiǎng shèbèi guī shéi guǎn, gēdān shéi lái dìng, wèizhì zěnme pái, zhèxiē dōu shì zhèngzhì.」Tā zuì zhǔnquè de yī cì yùpàn shì zài qùnián——tā zhùyì dào xiǎoqū lǐ liù gǒu de rén tūrán zēngjiā le sān chéng, érqiě dàbùfèn shì zhōngnián nánrén. Tā pànduàn zhè bú shì yǎng chǒngwù de rècháo, ér shì jiātíng máodùn de xìnhào. 「Zhōngnián nánrén zài jiā lǐ zuò bú zhù, zhǎo jièkǒu chūlái sànxīn, zuì fāngbiàn de lǐyóu jiù shì liù gǒu. Liù gǒu shíjiān chāoguò sìshí fēnzhōng de, jīběn shàng dōu shì zài wàimiàn duǒ qīngjìng.」Guǒrán, jiēxiàlái liǎng gè yuè, jūwěihuì shōu dào de jiātíng tiáojiě shēnqǐng bǐ píngshí duō le yī bèi. Tā hái yǒu yī tào guānyú lājītǒng de guānchá lǐlùn. 「Lājītǒng lǐ kāishǐ chūxiàn dàliàng wàimài hé de lóucéng, shuōmíng nà yī céng yǒu zhùhù zuìjìn zài jiābān huòzhě dújū de rén qíngxù bù hǎo——yīnwèi zhèngcháng qíngkuàng xià rénmen háishi huì zìjǐ zuòfàn de.」Zuì ràng wǒ pèifú de shì tā chǔlǐ línlǐ jiūfēn de fāngshì——tā cóng bù jí yú tiáojiě, ér shì xiān ràng shuāngfāng gèzì jiǎng wán, ránhòu zhǎo yī gè「gòngtóng de bùmǎn」zuòwéi qiērùdiǎn. 「Ràng chǎojià de liǎng gè rén yīqǐ mà wùyè,」tā xiào zhe shuō, 「tāmen jiù huì wàngjì zìjǐ wèishéme chǎojià le.」Lǎo Fāng shuō tā zuò le shíbā nián jūwěihuì gōngzuò, zuìdà de xīndé shì: 「Guǎnlǐ shèqū bú shì guǎnlǐ rén, shì guǎnlǐ rén hé rén zhījiān nàxiē kàn bú jiàn de fèngxì. Fèngxì tài dà le huì lòu fēng, tài xiǎo le huì jǐyā. Wǒ de gōngzuò jiù shì bǎ měi yī tiáo fèngxì dōu tiáozhěng dào gānghǎo ràng kōngqì liútōng de kuāndù.」
text_en: Neighborhood committee director Old Fang has his own ‘community weather forecasting’ system—he observes subtle signs to predict what’s coming. ‘When the square-dancing aunties change their song, it means an internal power transition,’ he says. ‘Who controls the sound equipment, who decides the playlist, how positions are arranged—all politics.’ His most accurate prediction: last year he noticed dog-walking in the compound had surged thirty percent, mostly middle-aged men. Not a pet trend—a signal of domestic conflict. ‘Middle-aged men can’t sit still at home and look for excuses to go out. The most convenient is walking the dog. Those whose walks exceed forty minutes are hiding outside for peace.’ Over the next two months, family mediation requests doubled. He also reads trash cans. ‘When a floor’s bins fill with takeout containers, someone on that floor is either working overtime or living alone and in low spirits.’ His method for neighborly disputes: he never rushes to mediate but lets both sides speak fully, then finds a ‘shared grievance’ as entry point. ‘Get the two quarreling people to curse the property management together, and they’ll forget why they were arguing.’ His biggest takeaway from eighteen years: ‘Managing a community isn’t managing people—it’s managing the invisible gaps between people. Too wide and the wind leaks; too tight and there’s pressure. My job is adjusting every gap to the width that lets air circulate.’

questions.q_zh: 老方怎样判断小区里可能会增加家庭矛盾？
questions.q_en: How does Old Fang predict a likely increase in family conflicts?

options.text: 他查看居民投诉记录
options.pinyin: tā chákàn jūmín tóusù jìlù
options.text_en: He reviews resident complaint records

options.text: 遛狗的中年男人突然增多，遛狗时间超过四十分钟
options.pinyin: liù gǒu de zhōngnián nánrén tūrán zēng duō, liù gǒu shíjiān chāoguò sìshí fēnzhōng
options.text_en: The number of middle-aged men walking dogs suddenly increases with walks exceeding forty minutes

options.text: 广场舞大妈们开始吵架
options.pinyin: guǎngchǎngwǔ dāmāmen kāishǐ chǎojià
options.text_en: The square-dancing aunties start quarreling

questions.explanation: Old Fang's predictive sign: 'the number of middle-aged men walking dogs suddenly increases with walks exceeding forty minutes,' indicating domestic tension.

questions.q_zh: 老方说「管理社区是管理人与人之间的缝隙」，他的意思是什么？
questions.q_en: What does Old Fang mean by 'managing a community is managing the gaps between people'?

options.text: 社区管理的核心是维修建筑之间的裂缝
options.pinyin: shèqū guǎnlǐ de héxīn shì wéixiū jiànzhù zhījiān de lièfèng
options.text_en: The core of community management is repairing cracks between buildings

options.text: 关键是调整人际关系中看不见的距离和空间，使之既不疏远也不挤压
options.pinyin: guānjiàn shì tiáozhěng rénjì guānxi zhōng kàn bú jiàn de jùlí hé kōngjiān, shǐ zhī jì bù shūyuǎn yě bù jǐyā
options.text_en: The key is adjusting the invisible distance and space in interpersonal relationships so they are neither too distant nor too compressed

options.text: 居委会应该严格管理每个居民的行为
options.pinyin: jūwěihuì yīnggāi yángé guǎnlǐ měi gè jūmín de xíngwéi
options.text_en: The committee should strictly manage every resident's behavior

questions.explanation: Old Fang's statement means effective community management requires calibrating the interpersonal distance—close enough for support, far enough for autonomy.

title: The Pedagogy of a Shoe Rack
text_zh: 新搬来的邻居在楼道里放了一个鞋架，这在老式居民楼里是一件极其敏感的事情。楼道属于公共空间，但在实际使用中，每户人家都会不自觉地将门口的一小块区域视为自己的延伸领地。鞋架的出现打破了这种模糊的默契，它像是一面旗帜，公然宣示着对公共空间的占领。果然，不到一周，隔壁的李阿姨就在鞋架旁边放了一辆折叠自行车，对门的张叔在另一侧摆了两盆绿萝。短短一个月内，原本宽敞的楼道变成了一个拥挤的杂物展览。物业贴了三次清理通知，每次都只维持了两三天的效果。最后解决这个问题的不是物业，而是五楼的王奶奶。她没有发表任何声明，只是悄悄地把自己门口那块地方打扫得干干净净，并且在地上放了一个很小的花盆，里面种着一株茉莉。花开的时候整个楼道都能闻到香味。渐渐地，邻居们开始效仿——鞋架搬走了，自行车推回了家里，取而代之的是各种小盆栽。到了春天，整个楼道变成了一个微型花园，每户人家门口都有一两盆自己精心照料的植物。王奶奶后来跟我说：「你不能通过命令让人改变习惯，但你可以通过示范让人看到另一种可能。一株茉莉的说服力，比十张告示都大。」
text_pinyin: Xīn bān lái de línjū zài lóudào lǐ fàng le yī gè xiéjià, zhè zài lǎoshì jūmínlóu lǐ shì yī jiàn jíqí mǐngǎn de shìqíng. Lóudào shǔyú gōnggòng kōngjiān, dàn zài shíjì shǐyòng zhōng, měi hù rénjiā dōu huì bù zìjué de jiāng ménkǒu de yī xiǎo kuài qūyù shì wéi zìjǐ de yánshēn lǐngdì. Xiéjià de chūxiàn dǎpò le zhè zhǒng móhu de mòqì, tā xiàng shì yī miàn qízhì, gōngrán xuānshì zhe duì gōnggòng kōngjiān de zhànlǐng. Guǒrán, bú dào yī zhōu, gébì de Lǐ āyí jiù zài xiéjià pángbiān fàng le yī liàng zhédié zìxíngchē, duìmén de Zhāng shū zài lìng yī cè bǎi le liǎng pén lǜluó. Duǎnduǎn yī gè yuè nèi, yuánběn kuānchǎng de lóudào biànchéng le yī gè yōngjǐ de záwù zhǎnlǎn. Wùyè tiē le sān cì qīnglǐ tōngzhī, měi cì dōu zhǐ wéichí le liǎng sān tiān de xiàoguǒ. Zuìhòu jiějué zhège wèntí de bú shì wùyè, ér shì wǔ lóu de Wáng nǎinai. Tā méiyǒu fābiǎo rènhé shēngmíng, zhǐ shì qiāoqiāo de bǎ zìjǐ ménkǒu nà kuài dìfāng dǎsǎo de gāngānjìngjìng, bìngqiě zài dìshàng fàng le yī gè hěn xiǎo de huāpén, lǐmiàn zhòng zhe yī zhū mòlì. Huā kāi de shíhou zhěnggè lóudào dōu néng wén dào xiāngwèi. Jiànjiàn de, línjūmen kāishǐ xiàofǎng——xiéjià bān zǒu le, zìxíngchē tuī huí le jiā lǐ, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì gè zhǒng xiǎo pénzāi. Dào le chūntiān, zhěnggè lóudào biànchéng le yī gè wēixíng huāyuán, měi hù rénjiā ménkǒu dōu yǒu yī liǎng pén zìjǐ jīngxīn zhàoliào de zhíwù. Wáng nǎinai hòulái gēn wǒ shuō: 「Nǐ bù néng tōngguò mìnglìng ràng rén gǎibiàn xíguàn, dàn nǐ kěyǐ tōngguò shìfàn ràng rén kàn dào lìng yī zhǒng kěnéng. Yī zhū mòlì de shuōfúlì, bǐ shí zhāng gàoshì dōu dà.」
text_en: A newly arrived neighbor placed a shoe rack in the hallway, an extremely sensitive act in an old-style apartment building. The hallway is public space, but in practice every household unconsciously treats the small area outside their door as an extension of their territory. The shoe rack's appearance shattered this ambiguous understanding; it was like a flag openly asserting occupation of public space. Sure enough, within a week, Auntie Li next door had placed a folding bicycle beside the shoe rack, and Uncle Zhang across the hall had set two pots of golden pothos on the other side. In barely a month, the once-spacious hallway had become a cramped junk exhibition. The property management posted three cleanup notices, each lasting only two or three days. In the end it was not the property management that solved the problem, but Grandma Wang on the fifth floor. She made no declaration—she simply, quietly, swept the area outside her own door spotlessly clean and placed a tiny flowerpot on the ground containing a jasmine plant. When it bloomed, the entire hallway could smell the fragrance. Gradually, neighbors began to follow her example—the shoe rack was removed, the bicycle wheeled back inside, replaced by various small potted plants. By spring, the entire hallway had become a miniature garden, with every household tending one or two plants of their own at the doorstep. Grandma Wang later told me: 'You can't change people's habits through orders, but you can show them another possibility through example. A single jasmine plant has more persuasive power than ten posted notices.'

questions.q_zh: 鞋架的出现为什么在老式居民楼里引发连锁反应？
questions.q_en: Why did the shoe rack's appearance trigger a chain reaction in the old apartment building?

options.text: 因为鞋架挡住了消防通道
options.pinyin: yīnwèi xiéjià dǎng zhù le xiāofáng tōngdào
options.text_en: Because the shoe rack blocked the fire escape

options.text: 因为它打破了对公共空间使用的默契，引发邻居纷纷占领更多公共空间
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā dǎpò le duì gōnggòng kōngjiān shǐyòng de mòqì, yǐnfā línjū fēnfēn zhànlǐng gèng duō gōnggòng kōngjiān
options.text_en: Because it broke the tacit understanding about public space usage, prompting neighbors to claim more public space

options.text: 因为鞋架太丑影响了楼道美观
options.pinyin: yīnwèi xiéjià tài chǒu yǐngxiǎng le lóudào měiguān
options.text_en: Because the shoe rack was too ugly and affected the hallway's appearance

questions.explanation: The shoe rack as breaking the tacit equilibrium about hallway usage, triggering a chain reaction as other residents claimed public space similarly.

questions.q_zh: 王奶奶认为改变人的习惯最有效的方式是什么？
questions.q_en: What does Grandma Wang believe is the most effective way to change people's habits?

options.text: 张贴告示和罚款
options.pinyin: zhāngtiē gàoshì hé fákuǎn
options.text_en: Posting notices and imposing fines

options.text: 通过示范让人看到另一种可能
options.pinyin: tōngguò shìfàn ràng rén kàn dào lìng yī zhǒng kěnéng
options.text_en: Showing people another possibility through example

options.text: 召开居民大会讨论规则
options.pinyin: zhàokāi jūmín dàhuì tǎolùn guīzé
options.text_en: Holding a residents' meeting to discuss rules

questions.explanation: Grandma Wang believes demonstrating an alternative through example is more effective than verbal persuasion or rules.

title: The Jurisprudence of a Parking Lot
text_zh: 小区的地下停车场只有八十个车位，但小区里有一百二十辆车。这个简单的数学题衍生出了一套复杂得令人叹服的民间治理体系。最早入住的那批业主自然占据了离电梯最近的「黄金车位」，后来的业主只能停在角落或者地上的临时车位。但随着时间推移，一些有趣的潜规则逐渐形成。比如，停车场有一个不成文的「二十四小时法则」——如果一个车位连续空置超过二十四小时，其他车主就可以临时使用，但必须在原车主回来之前让出。判断原车主是否要回来的依据是微信群里的动态——如果有人在群里说「出差回来了」或者晒了一张回程票的照片，占了他车位的人就会心照不宣地把车挪走。最精妙的设计是每月最后一个周日的「车位拍卖日」。那些临时有多余车位的业主可以在微信群里以「请客一顿早茶」的价格出租给需要的人。这不是真正的拍卖，更像是一种社区内部的资源调配仪式——金额不重要，重要的是双方通过这个交易确认了一种互惠的关系。物业经理看着这一切摇头苦笑：「我们制定了一套完整的停车管理制度，厚厚的一本手册，结果业主们用一个微信群就把问题给解决了。有时候最好的制度不是写在纸上的，而是在日常博弈中自发生长出来的。」
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū de dìxià tíngchēchǎng zhǐ yǒu bāshí gè chēwèi, dàn xiǎoqū lǐ yǒu yībǎi èrshí liàng chē. Zhège jiǎndān de shùxué tí yǎnshēng chū le yī tào fùzá de lìng rén tànfú de mínjiān zhìlǐ tǐxì. Zuìzǎo rùzhù de nà pī yèzhǔ zìrán zhànjù le lí diàntī zuìjìn de「huángjīn chēwèi」, hòulái de yèzhǔ zhǐ néng tíng zài jiǎoluò huòzhě dìshàng de línshí chēwèi. Dàn suízhe shíjiān tuīyí, yīxiē yǒuqù de qiánguīzé zhújiàn xíngchéng. Bǐrú, tíngchēchǎng yǒu yī gè bù chéngwén de「èrshísì xiǎoshí fǎzé」——rúguǒ yī gè chēwèi liánxù kōngzhì chāoguò èrshísì xiǎoshí, qítā chēzhǔ jiù kěyǐ línshí shǐyòng, dàn bìxū zài yuán chēzhǔ huílái zhīqián ràng chū. Pànduàn yuán chēzhǔ shìfǒu yào huílái de yījù shì Wēixìn qún lǐ de dòngtài——rúguǒ yǒu rén zài qún lǐ shuō「chūchāi huílái le」huòzhě shài le yī zhāng huíchéng piào de zhàopiàn, zhàn le tā chēwèi de rén jiù huì xīnzhào bú xuān de bǎ chē nuó zǒu. Zuì jīngmiào de shèjì shì měi yuè zuìhòu yī gè zhōurì de「chēwèi pāimài rì」. Nàxiē línshí yǒu duōyú chēwèi de yèzhǔ kěyǐ zài Wēixìn qún lǐ yǐ「qǐng kè yī dùn zǎochá」de jiàgé chūzū gěi xūyào de rén. Zhè bú shì zhēnzhèng de pāimài, gèng xiàng shì yī zhǒng shèqū nèibù de zīyuán diàopèi yíshì——jīn'é bú zhòngyào, zhòngyào de shì shuāngfāng tōngguò zhège jiāoyì quèrèn le yī zhǒng hùhuì de guānxi. Wùyè jīnglǐ kàn zhe zhè yīqiè yáotóu kǔxiào: 「Wǒmen zhìdìng le yī tào wánzhěng de tíngchē guǎnlǐ zhìdù, hòu hòu de yī běn shǒucè, jiéguǒ yèzhǔmen yòng yī gè Wēixìn qún jiù bǎ wèntí gěi jiějué le. Yǒu shíhou zuìhǎo de zhìdù bú shì xiě zài zhǐ shàng de, ér shì zài rìcháng bóyì zhōng zìfā shēngzhǎng chūlái de.」
text_en: The underground parking lot of the residential compound has only eighty spaces, but the compound contains a hundred and twenty cars. This simple math problem has given rise to a complex and admirable grassroots governance system. The earliest residents naturally claimed the 'golden spots' nearest the elevator; latecomers had to park in corners or in temporary ground-level spots. But over time, some interesting unwritten rules gradually took shape. For instance, the parking lot has an unwritten 'twenty-four-hour rule'—if a spot sits empty for more than twenty-four consecutive hours, other drivers may use it temporarily, but must vacate before the original owner returns. The basis for judging whether the original owner is returning is activity in the WeChat group—if someone posts 'back from my business trip' or shares a photo of a return ticket, the person occupying their spot will tacitly move their car. The most ingenious arrangement is 'parking-spot auction day' on the last Sunday of each month. Owners who temporarily have an extra spot can rent it out in the WeChat group at the price of 'treating someone to morning tea.' This isn't a real auction—it's more of an internal resource-allocation ritual. The amount doesn't matter; what matters is that through this transaction both parties confirm a reciprocal relationship. The property manager watches all this and shakes his head with a wry smile: 'We drew up a complete parking management system—a thick manual—and the owners solved the problem with a single WeChat group. Sometimes the best system isn't one written on paper but one that grows organically from daily negotiation.'

questions.q_zh: 小区停车场的「二十四小时法则」是怎么运作的？
questions.q_en: How does the parking lot's 'twenty-four-hour rule' work?

options.text: 物业每二十四小时重新分配一次车位
options.pinyin: wùyè měi èrshísì xiǎoshí chóngxīn fēnpèi yī cì chēwèi

title: The Epistemology of a Security Guard's Logbook
text_zh: 老吴是小区的门卫，他有一个厚厚的登记簿，记录着每天进出小区的访客信息。但这个登记簿真正记录的远不止姓名和时间。老吴在每条记录的旁边都会用铅笔做一些只有他自己能看懂的符号——一个圆圈表示「常客」，一个三角形表示「第一次来」，一个星号表示「需要留意」。他说这套符号系统是他二十年门卫生涯的精华。「我不记名字，我记脸。」他解释说，「一个人走进小区的时候，他的步速、眼神和表情会告诉我很多东西。走路快、目不斜视的人通常是来办正事的，可以放行。走路慢、四处张望的人可能是第一次来，需要指路。走路快但眼神飘忽的人最需要注意——他知道自己要去哪里，但不想被人注意到。」老吴最自豪的事情是他的登记簿曾经帮助破获过一起入室盗窃案。警察来调取监控的时候，他翻出登记簿，指着三天前的一条记录说：「这个人当时说是来找朋友的，但他的鞋子不对——穿着全新的运动鞋，鞋底没有任何磨损，说明他很可能是特意换了鞋来的，防止留下可辨认的鞋印。」警察后来根据这个线索锁定了嫌疑人。老吴说做门卫不是看门，是看人。「每个人走进这扇门的时候，都在无意中展示着自己的一部分。我的工作就是把这些碎片收集起来，拼成一个故事。大多数时候这些故事都很无聊，但偶尔会出现一个让你后背发凉的情节。那个时候，你就知道这本登记簿的重量了。」
text_pinyin: Lǎo Wú shì xiǎoqū de ménwèi, tā yǒu yī gè hòu hòu de dēngjìbù, jìlù zhe měi tiān jìnchū xiǎoqū de fǎngkè xìnxī. Dàn zhège dēngjìbù zhēnzhèng jìlù de yuǎn bù zhǐ xìngmíng hé shíjiān. Lǎo Wú zài měi tiáo jìlù de pángbiān dōu huì yòng qiānbǐ zuò yīxiē zhǐ yǒu tā zìjǐ néng kàn dǒng de fúhào——yī gè yuánquān biǎoshì「chángkè」, yī gè sānjiǎoxíng biǎoshì「dì yī cì lái」, yī gè xīnghào biǎoshì「xūyào liúyì」. Tā shuō zhè tào fúhào xìtǒng shì tā èrshí nián ménwèi shēngyá de jīnghuá. 「Wǒ bú jì míngzi, wǒ jì liǎn.」Tā jiěshì shuō, 「yī gè rén zǒu jìn xiǎoqū de shíhou, tā de bùsù, yǎnshén hé biǎoqíng huì gàosu wǒ hěn duō dōngxi. Zǒulù kuài, mù bù xiéshì de rén tōngcháng shì lái bàn zhèng shì de, kěyǐ fàngxíng. Zǒulù màn, sìchù zhāngwàng de rén kěnéng shì dì yī cì lái, xūyào zhǐlù. Zǒulù kuài dàn yǎnshén piāohū de rén zuì xūyào zhùyì——tā zhīdào zìjǐ yào qù nǎlǐ, dàn bù xiǎng bèi rén zhùyì dào.」Lǎo Wú zuì zìháo de shìqíng shì tā de dēngjìbù céngjīng bāngzhù pòhuò guò yī qǐ rùshì dàoqiè àn. Jǐngchá lái diàoqǔ jiānkòng de shíhou, tā fān chū dēngjìbù, zhǐ zhe sān tiān qián de yī tiáo jìlù shuō: 「Zhège rén dāngshí shuō shì lái zhǎo péngyǒu de, dàn tā de xiézi bú duì——chuān zhe quánxīn de yùndòngxié, xiédǐ méiyǒu rènhé mósǔn, shuōmíng tā hěn kěnéng shì tèyì huàn le xié lái de, fángzhǐ liú xià kě biànrèn de xiéyìn.」Jǐngchá hòulái gēnjù zhège xiànsuǒ suǒdìng le xiányírén. Lǎo Wú shuō zuò ménwèi bú shì kānmén, shì kàn rén. 「Měi gè rén zǒu jìn zhè shàn mén de shíhou, dōu zài wúyì zhōng zhǎnshì zhe zìjǐ de yī bùfèn. Wǒ de gōngzuò jiù shì bǎ zhèxiē suìpiàn shōují qǐlái, pīn chéng yī gè gùshi. Dàduōshù shíhou zhèxiē gùshi dōu hěn wúliáo, dàn ǒu'ěr huì chūxiàn yī gè ràng nǐ hòubèi fā liáng de qíngjié. Nàge shíhou, nǐ jiù zhīdào zhè běn dēngjìbù de zhòngliàng le.」
text_en: Old Wu is the compound’s security guard, keeping a thick logbook of every visitor. But what the logbook truly records goes beyond names and times. Beside each entry he makes pencil annotations in symbols only he can read—a circle means ‘regular,’ a triangle ‘first-time visitor,’ an asterisk ‘keep an eye on.’ This system distills twenty years of guarding. ‘I don’t memorize names—I memorize faces,’ he says. ‘When a person walks in, their pace, gaze, and expression tell me a great deal. Walking briskly, eyes straight ahead—legitimate business, let them through. Walking slowly, looking around—first time, needs directions. Walking quickly with a shifty gaze—needs the most attention. They know where they’re going but don’t want to be noticed.’ His proudest moment: his logbook helped crack a burglary. When police pulled surveillance footage, he pointed to an entry from three days prior: ‘This person said he was visiting a friend, but his shoes were wrong—brand-new sneakers with zero sole wear, suggesting he changed shoes deliberately to avoid identifiable footprints.’ Police used this lead to identify the suspect. Old Wu says guarding isn’t watching the gate—it’s reading people. ‘Every person walking through unwittingly displays a part of themselves. My job is collecting these fragments and piecing them into a story.’

title: The Cartography of Power Outages
text_zh: 每次停电都像是城市的一次微型社会实验。上个月的那次计划外停电持续了六个小时，从下午两点一直到晚上八点。在电力恢复之前，这个小区经历了一场教科书般的社会重组过程。最初的三十分钟是混乱期——电梯停运、手机信号基站因断电而变弱、空调失灵导致室内温度迅速攀升。住户们纷纷走出家门，来到楼下的院子里透气。到了第一个小时，人们开始自发地共享资源——有人从家里搬出一台充满电的便携式充电站，免费给邻居的手机充电；有人把冰箱里即将解冻的冰淇淋分给孩子们吃。第二个小时出现了「信息集市」——由于手机信号不稳定，人们开始面对面交换关于停电原因和恢复时间的各种小道消息。到了傍晚，院子里出现了一个临时的露天社交场——有人搬来了折叠桌，有人贡献了蜡烛，几个大爷围在一起下象棋，几个大妈交换着各自的腌菜秘方。孩子们在没有电子产品的世界里重新发现了跳绳和捉迷藏的乐趣。那天晚上八点电力恢复的时候，人们几乎是不情愿地回到了各自的家里。一个邻居跟我说：「奇怪的是，停电的时候我反而觉得住在这个小区比平时热闹多了。平时大家各自关在门里面，有了电反而把人隔开了。」这句话让我想了很久——也许电力带给我们的不只是光明和便利，还有一种精致的隔离。
text_pinyin: Měi cì tíngdiàn dōu xiàng shì chéngshì de yī cì wēixíng shèhuì shíyàn. Shàng gè yuè de nà cì jìhuà wài tíngdiàn chíxù le liù gè xiǎoshí, cóng xiàwǔ liǎng diǎn yīzhí dào wǎnshàng bā diǎn. Zài diànlì huīfù zhīqián, zhège xiǎoqū jīnglì le yī chǎng jiàokēshū bān de shèhuì chóngzǔ guòchéng. Zuìchū de sānshí fēnzhōng shì hùnluàn qī——diàntī tíng yùn, shǒujī xìnhào jīzhàn yīn duàndiàn ér biàn ruò, kōngtiáo shīlíng dǎozhì shìnèi wēndù xùnsù pānshēng. Zhùhùmen fēnfēn zǒuchū jiāmén, lái dào lóu xià de yuànzi lǐ tòuqì. Dào le dì yī gè xiǎoshí, rénmen kāishǐ zìfā de gòngxiǎng zīyuán——yǒu rén cóng jiā lǐ bān chū yī tái chōng mǎn diàn de biànxiéshì chōngdiàn zhàn, miǎnfèi gěi línjū de shǒujī chōngdiàn; yǒu rén bǎ bīngxiāng lǐ jíjiāng jiědòng de bīngqílín fēn gěi háizimen chī. Dì èr gè xiǎoshí chūxiàn le「xìnxī jíshì」——yóuyú shǒujī xìnhào bù wěndìng, rénmen kāishǐ miànduìmiàn jiāohuàn guānyú tíngdiàn yuányīn hé huīfù shíjiān de gè zhǒng xiǎodào xiāoxi. Dào le bàngwǎn, yuànzi lǐ chūxiàn le yī gè línshí de lùtiān shèjiāo chǎng——yǒu rén bān lái le zhédié zhuō, yǒu rén gòngxiàn le làzhú, jǐ gè dàyé wéi zài yīqǐ xià xiàngqí, jǐ gè dāmā jiāohuàn zhe gèzì de yāncài mìfāng. Háizimen zài méiyǒu diànzǐ chǎnpǐn de shìjiè lǐ chóngxīn fāxiàn le tiào shéng hé zhuō mícáng de lèqù. Nà tiān wǎnshàng bā diǎn diànlì huīfù de shíhou, rénmen jīhū shì bù qíngyuàn de huí dào le gèzì de jiā lǐ. Yī gè línjū gēn wǒ shuō: 「Qíguài de shì, tíngdiàn de shíhou wǒ fǎn'ér juéde zhù zài zhège xiǎoqū bǐ píngshí rènao duō le. Píngshí dàjiā gèzì guān zài mén lǐmiàn, yǒu le diàn fǎn'ér bǎ rén gé kāi le.」Zhè jù huà ràng wǒ xiǎng le hěn jiǔ——yěxǔ diànlì dài gěi wǒmen de bù zhǐ shì guāngmíng hé biànlì, hái yǒu yī zhǒng jīngzhì de gélí.
text_en: Every power outage is like a miniature social experiment in the city. Last month's unplanned outage lasted six hours, from two in the afternoon until eight at night. Before power was restored, this compound underwent a textbook process of social reorganization. The first thirty minutes were a period of chaos—elevators shut down, cell tower signals weakened from the outage, and air-conditioning failure sent indoor temperatures soaring. Residents streamed out of their homes into the courtyard below for air. By the first hour, people began spontaneously sharing resources—someone hauled out a fully charged portable power station from home, offering free phone charging to neighbors; someone else distributed thawing ice cream from their freezer to the children. The second hour saw the emergence of an 'information bazaar'—since cell signals were unstable, people began exchanging rumors about the cause and estimated restoration time face to face. By evening, a temporary open-air social scene had appeared in the courtyard—someone brought out a folding table, someone contributed candles, several old men huddled together over chess, several women swapped pickle recipes. In a world without electronics, children rediscovered the joy of jump rope and hide-and-seek. When power was restored at eight that evening, people returned to their apartments almost reluctantly. A neighbor told me: 'Strangely, during the outage I actually felt the compound was livelier than usual.

title: The Ecology of a Classroom Blackboard
text_zh: 退休前我在这所中学教了三十年的语文，但我最了解的不是课本，而是黑板。三十年来，我看着黑板从木框的水泥面换成了绿色的磁性面板，又从磁性面板升级为电子白板。每一次更换都意味着一种教学生态的微妙转变。水泥黑板的年代，粉笔灰是教室里的空气成分之一。老师们下课后第一件事是拍打衣服上的白色粉末，久而久之每个老教师的手指关节都有一层洗不掉的白色沉积。那个时代的黑板有一种独特的触感——粉笔划过粗糙表面时的阻力恰到好处，既不太滑也不太涩，写出来的字有一种沉稳的份量感。后来换了磁性绿板，书写变得顺滑了，但我总觉得字的「重量」轻了。再后来是电子白板，用手指或触控笔在光滑的屏幕上书写，字体可以随意放大缩小、变色擦除，技术上确实方便了很多，但那种「一笔写下去就无法修改」的庄重感彻底消失了。我最怀念的是传统黑板上那些擦不干净的痕迹——即使用黑板擦反复擦拭，之前写过的字还是会留下一层隐约的灰白色影子。那些残留的笔迹像是教室的记忆叠层，今天的数学公式叠加在昨天的唐诗上，上周的化学方程式模糊地浮现在这周的英语单词底下。一块用了十年的老黑板，它的表面是无数知识的考古遗址。电子白板什么痕迹都不会留下——每一次清除都是彻底的、完美的、没有历史的。在某种意义上，我们用效率换走了厚度。
text_pinyin: Tuìxiū qián wǒ zài zhè suǒ zhōngxué jiào le sānshí nián de yǔwén, dàn wǒ zuì liǎojiě de bú shì kèběn, ér shì hēibǎn. Sānshí nián lái, wǒ kàn zhe hēibǎn cóng mù kuàng de shuǐní miàn huàn chéng le lǜsè de cíxìng miànbǎn, yòu cóng cíxìng miànbǎn shēngjí wéi diànzǐ báibǎn. Měi yī cì gēnghuàn dōu yìwèi zhe yī zhǒng jiàoxué shēngtài de wēimiào zhuǎnbiàn. Shuǐní hēibǎn de niándài, fěnbǐ huī shì jiàoshì lǐ de kōngqì chéngfèn zhī yī. Lǎoshīmen xiàkè hòu dì yī jiàn shì shì pāidǎ yīfu shàng de báisè fěnmò, jiǔ ér jiǔ zhī měi gè lǎo jiàoshī de shǒuzhǐ guānjié dōu yǒu yī céng xǐ bú diào de báisè chénjī. Nàge shídài de hēibǎn yǒu yī zhǒng dútè de chùgǎn——fěnbǐ huà guò cūcāo biǎomiàn shí de zǔlì qiàdàohǎochù, jì bú tài huá yě bú tài sè, xiě chūlái de zì yǒu yī zhǒng chénwěn de fènliàng gǎn. Hòulái huàn le cíxìng lǜbǎn, shūxiě biàn de shùnhuá le, dàn wǒ zǒng juéde zì de「zhòngliàng」qīng le. Zài hòulái shì diànzǐ báibǎn, yòng shǒuzhǐ huò chùkòngbǐ zài guānghuá de píngmù shàng shūxiě, zìtǐ kěyǐ suíyì fàngdà suōxiǎo, biànsè cāchú, jìshù shàng quèshí fāngbiàn le hěn duō, dàn nà zhǒng「yī bǐ xiě xiàqù jiù wúfǎ xiūgǎi」de zhuāngzhòng gǎn chèdǐ xiāoshī le. Wǒ zuì huáiniàn de shì chuántǒng hēibǎn shàng nàxiē cā bù gānjìng de hénjì——jíshǐ yòng hēibǎn cā fǎnfù cāshì, zhīqián xiě guò de zì háishi huì liú xià yī céng yǐnyuē de huībáisè yǐngzi. Nàxiē cánliú de bǐjì xiàng shì jiàoshì de jìyì dié céng, jīntiān de shùxué gōngshì diéjiā zài zuótiān de Tángshī shàng, shàng zhōu de huàxué fāngchéngshì móhu de fúxiàn zài zhè zhōu de Yīngyǔ dāncí dǐxia. Yī kuài yòng le shí nián de lǎo hēibǎn, tā de biǎomiàn shì wúshù zhīshi de kǎogǔ yízhǐ. Diànzǐ báibǎn shénme hénjì dōu bú huì liú xià——měi yī cì qīngchú dōu shì chèdǐ de, wánměi de, méiyǒu lìshǐ de. Zài mǒu zhǒng yìyì shàng, wǒmen yòng xiàolǜ huàn zǒu le hòudù.
text_en: Before retiring I taught Chinese at this middle school for thirty years, but what I knew best was the blackboard. I watched blackboards go from cement surfaces in wooden frames to green magnetic panels to electronic whiteboards. Each replacement shifted the teaching ecology. In the cement era, chalk dust was part of the air. Teachers patted white powder off their clothes after class; veteran teachers’ finger joints bore a permanent white deposit. Those boards had a distinctive feel—the resistance of chalk on rough surface was just right, and characters came out with settled gravity. Magnetic green boards made writing smoother, but the ‘weight’ of characters lightened. Then electronic whiteboards—writing on a smooth screen with finger or stylus, text freely resizable, erasable—technically convenient, but the solemnity of ‘once you put down a stroke, it cannot be altered’ had vanished. What I miss most are the inerasable traces on traditional blackboards—even after repeated wiping, characters left a faint grayish-white shadow. Those residual strokes were memory strata: today’s math formulas over yesterday’s Tang poetry, last week’s chemistry equations beneath this week’s English vocabulary. A blackboard used ten years was an archaeological site of countless lessons. Electronic whiteboards leave no trace—every clearing is thorough, perfect, historyless. We traded depth for efficiency.

title: The Acoustics of a Stairwell
text_zh: 我住的这栋六层老楼没有电梯，所有住户每天都要爬楼梯。正因如此，楼梯间成了整栋楼里最独特的社交空间——它不属于任何一户人家，却被所有人共同使用和塑造。最先引起我注意的是楼梯间的声学特性。由于墙壁和天花板都是光滑的水泥面，声音在其中会产生强烈的混响效果。每个住户回家时钥匙碰撞的叮当声、换鞋的窸窣声、关门的咔哒声，都会沿着楼梯井一层层向上或向下传递，像是一种垂直方向的广播系统。住久了你就会发现，单凭脚步声就能判断是谁在上楼——二楼的张老师步伐稳健匀称，一定是多年太极拳练就的节奏；三楼的小夫妻总是一前一后，中间隔着恰好半层楼的距离，女方的高跟鞋声在前面哒哒哒，男方的运动鞋声在后面嗒嗒嗒；五楼的独居老太太上楼最慢，每上三四级台阶就要停下来歇一歇，呼吸声随着楼层的升高逐渐加重。最温暖的声音出现在每天傍晚——四楼的刘叔会在楼梯间的窗台上放一台小收音机，调到评书频道，音量刚好能让三楼到五楼的邻居听到。没有人要求他这样做，也没有人抱怨过噪音。那个声音在黄昏的楼梯间里缓缓流淌，混合着窗外最后一缕夕阳和楼下飘来的炒菜香味，构成了一种只属于这栋楼的黄昏交响曲。搬走以后我才意识到，我怀念的不是那个房子，而是那个楼梯间里所有声音交织在一起时的那种归属感。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de zhè dòng liù céng lǎo lóu méiyǒu diàntī, suǒyǒu zhùhù měi tiān dōu yào pá lóutī. Zhèng yīncǐ, lóutījiān chéng le zhěng dòng lóu lǐ zuì dútè de shèjiāo kōngjiān——tā bù shǔyú rènhé yī hù rénjiā, què bèi suǒyǒu rén gòngtóng shǐyòng hé sùzào. Zuì xiān yǐnqǐ wǒ zhùyì de shì lóutījiān de shēngxué tèxìng. Yóuyú qiángbì hé tiānhuābǎn dōu shì guānghuá de shuǐní miàn, shēngyīn zài qízhōng huì chǎnshēng qiángliè de hùnxiǎng xiàoguǒ. Měi gè zhùhù huíjiā shí yàoshi pèngzhuàng de dīngdāng shēng, huàn xié de xīsū shēng, guān mén de kādā shēng, dōu huì yán zhe lóutī jǐng yī céng céng xiàng shàng huò xiàng xià chuándì, xiàng shì yī zhǒng chuízhí fāngxiàng de guǎngbō xìtǒng. Zhù jiǔ le nǐ jiù huì fāxiàn, dān píng jiǎobù shēng jiù néng pànduàn shì shéi zài shàng lóu——èr lóu de Zhāng lǎoshī bùfá wěnjiàn yúnchèn, yīdìng shì duō nián tàijíquán liànjiù de jiézòu; sān lóu de xiǎo fūqī zǒng shì yī qián yī hòu, zhōngjiān gé zhe qiàhǎo bàn céng lóu de jùlí, nǚfāng de gāogēn xié shēng zài qiánmiàn dādādā, nánfāng de yùndòng xié shēng zài hòumiàn dādādā; wǔ lóu de dújū lǎotàitai shàng lóu zuì màn, měi shàng sān sì jí táijiē jiù yào tíng xiàlái xiē yīxiē, hūxī shēng suízhe lóucéng de shēnggāo zhújiàn jiāzhòng. Zuì wēnnuǎn de shēngyīn chūxiàn zài měi tiān bàngwǎn——sì lóu de Liú shū huì zài lóutījiān de chuāngtái shàng fàng yī tái xiǎo shōuyīnjī, tiáo dào píngshū píndào, yīnliàng gānghǎo néng ràng sān lóu dào wǔ lóu de línjū tīng dào. Méiyǒu rén yāoqiú tā zhèyàng zuò, yě méiyǒu rén bàoyuàn guò zàoyīn. Nàge shēngyīn zài huánghūn de lóutījiān lǐ huǎnhuǎn liútǎng, hùnhé zhe chuāng wài zuìhòu yī lǚ xīyáng hé lóu xià piāo lái de chǎo cài xiāngwèi, gòuchéng le yī zhǒng zhǐ shǔyú zhè dòng lóu de huánghūn jiāoxiǎngqǔ. Bān zǒu yǐhòu wǒ cái yìshí dào, wǒ huáiniàn de bú shì nàge fángzi, ér shì nàge lóutījiān lǐ suǒyǒu shēngyīn jiāozhī zài yīqǐ shí de nà zhǒng guīshǔ gǎn.
text_en: The six-story building I live in has no elevator; all residents climb stairs daily. The stairwell has become the building’s most distinctive social space—belonging to no household yet shaped by everyone. What first caught my attention was its acoustics. Smooth cement walls produce strong reverberation—each resident’s returning keys, the rustling of shoes, a closing door all travel floor by floor, like a vertical broadcasting system. Live here long enough and you identify climbers by footsteps alone. Teacher Zhang on the second floor: steady, even stride—years of tai chi. The young couple on three always walk single-file, half a flight apart, wife’s heels clicking ahead, husband’s sneakers padding behind. The elderly woman alone on five climbs slowest, resting every few steps, breathing heavier each floor. The warmest sound comes every evening—Uncle Liu on four places a small radio on the stairwell windowsill, tuned to the storytelling channel, volume just audible to neighbors on three through five. No one asked him to; no one has complained. That sound flows through the twilight stairwell, blending with the last sunset through the window and the aroma of stir-frying from below—a dusk symphony belonging only to this building.

questions.q_zh: 作者怎样通过脚步声辨认邻居？
questions.q_en: How does the author identify neighbors by their footsteps?

options.text_en: By the time of day they climb the stairs

questions.explanation: The author identifies neighbors by stride rhythm, speed, and shoe type—each person's footsteps having a distinctive acoustic signature.

questions.q_zh: 作者搬走后最怀念的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author miss most after moving away?

options.pinyin: nà tào fángzi de zhuāngxiū hé géjú
options.text_en: The apartment's decoration and layout

options.text: 楼梯间里所有声音交织在一起时的归属感
options.pinyin: lóutījiān lǐ suǒyǒu shēngyīn jiāozhī zài yīqǐ shí de guīshǔ gǎn

options.text: 不用坐电梯的健康生活方式

title: The Shoes That Shaped My Walk
text_zh: 我的右脚走路时会略微外翻，这个习惯可以追溯到十四岁那年——那年家里经济困难，父亲买了一双大两号的皮鞋给我，说大一点可以多穿两年。为了不让那双过大的鞋在走路时啪啪作响，我不得不调整脚掌着地的角度，久而久之形成了一种独特的步态。后来家境好了，鞋不再是问题，但那个走路的习惯已经刻进了肌肉记忆里，再也改不回来了。有一次体检的时候，骨科医生看着我的X光片说我的右膝关节有轻微的磨损不对称，问我是不是长期穿不合适的鞋。我把这个故事讲给他听，他沉默了一会儿说：「身体是最诚实的记录者。你父亲的经济压力、你的适应策略、以及你们父子之间不需要说出口的体谅——这些全都写在你的骨骼里了。」那句话让我想了很久。我们总以为贫穷只是一个暂时的经济状态，熬过去就没事了。但贫穷留下的痕迹远比我们想象的持久——它改变了一个孩子的步态，而步态改变了骨骼结构，骨骼结构影响了关节健康。一双不合脚的鞋的代价，在三十年后才完全显现。这不是父亲的错，这是贫穷的错。但每当我走路时感觉到右脚那个微妙的外翻，我不会觉得疼，反而会想起父亲在鞋店里犹豫了很久、最终选择了大两号那个下午的表情——那种表情叫作：我给不了你最好的，但我能给你的已经是我的全部了。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de yòu jiǎo zǒulù shí huì lüèwēi wàifān, zhège xíguàn kěyǐ zhuīsù dào shísì suì nà nián——nà nián jiā lǐ jīngjì kùnnan, fùqīn mǎi le yī shuāng dà liǎng hào de píxié gěi wǒ, shuō dà yīdiǎn kěyǐ duō chuān liǎng nián. Wèile bú ràng nà shuāng guò dà de xié zài zǒulù shí pāpā zuòxiǎng, wǒ bùdébù tiáozhěng jiǎozhǎng zhe dì de jiǎodù, jiǔ ér jiǔ zhī xíngchéng le yī zhǒng dútè de bùtài. Hòulái jiājìng hǎo le, xié bú zài shì wèntí, dàn nàge zǒulù de xíguàn yǐjīng kè jìn le jīròu jìyì lǐ, zài yě gǎi bù huílái le. Yǒu yī cì tǐjiǎn de shíhou, gǔkē yīshēng kàn zhe wǒ de X guāng piàn shuō wǒ de yòu xī guānjié yǒu qīngwēi de mósǔn bú duìchèn, wèn wǒ shì bú shì chángqī chuān bù héshì de xié. Wǒ bǎ zhège gùshi jiǎng gěi tā tīng, tā chénmò le yīhuìr shuō: 「Shēntǐ shì zuì chéngshí de jìlùzhě. Nǐ fùqīn de jīngjì yālì, nǐ de shìyìng cèlüè, yǐjí nǐmen fùzǐ zhījiān bù xūyào shuō chūkǒu de tǐliàng——zhèxiē quán dōu xiě zài nǐ de gǔgé lǐ le.」Nà jù huà ràng wǒ xiǎng le hěn jiǔ. Wǒmen zǒng yǐwéi pínqióng zhǐ shì yī gè zànshí de jīngjì zhuàngtài, áo guòqù jiù méi shì le. Dàn pínqióng liú xià de hénjì yuǎn bǐ wǒmen xiǎngxiàng de chíjiǔ——tā gǎibiàn le yī gè háizi de bùtài, ér bùtài gǎibiàn le gǔgé jiégòu, gǔgé jiégòu yǐngxiǎng le guānjié jiànkāng. Yī shuāng bù hé jiǎo de xié de dàijià, zài sānshí nián hòu cái wánquán xiǎnxiàn. Zhè bú shì fùqīn de cuò, zhè shì pínqióng de cuò. Dàn měi dāng wǒ zǒulù shí gǎnjué dào yòu jiǎo nàge wēimiào de wàifān, wǒ bú huì juéde téng, fǎn'ér huì xiǎng qǐ fùqīn zài xiédiàn lǐ yóuyù le hěn jiǔ, zuìzhōng xuǎnzé le dà liǎng hào nàge xiàwǔ de biǎoqíng——nà zhǒng biǎoqíng jiào zuò: wǒ gěi bù liǎo nǐ zuìhǎo de, dàn wǒ néng gěi nǐ de yǐjīng shì wǒ de quánbù le.
text_en: My right foot turns slightly outward when I walk—traceable to age fourteen, a year of financial hardship. My father bought leather shoes two sizes too large, saying bigger ones last two extra years. To keep them from slapping, I adjusted my sole angle, and over time developed a distinctive gait. Later our circumstances improved, but the habit had been carved into muscle memory. During a checkup, an orthopedic doctor noted asymmetric wear on my right knee joint and asked if I’d long worn ill-fitting shoes. I told him the story. He paused, then said: ‘The body is the most honest recorder. Your father’s financial pressure, your adaptation, the unspoken consideration between father and son—all written in your bones.’ We assume poverty is a temporary state—endure it and it’s over. But its traces last longer than we imagine. It changed a child’s gait, the gait changed bone structure, bone structure affected joint health. The cost of one pair of ill-fitting shoes fully manifests thirty years later. This isn’t my father’s fault; it’s poverty’s. But whenever I feel that subtle outward turn, I think of his expression in the shoe store—hesitating a long time before choosing the pair two sizes too big. That expression is called: I can’t give you the best, but what I can give is already everything I have.
