title: The Person in the Photograph
text_zh: 整理手机相册的时候，我发现了一张五年前在某个咖啡馆拍的自拍照。照片里的那个人穿着我不记得自己拥有过的衣服，背景是我完全不记得去过的地方，表情介于微笑和发呆之间，像是在想一件已经被彻底遗忘的事情。我盯着这张照片看了很久，感受到一种奇特的陌生感——那个人确实是我，但又不完全是我。五年的时间改变了太多东西：我的脸稍微胖了一点，发际线后退了一厘米左右，眼角多了两条细纹。但这些物理变化是次要的，真正让我感到陌生的是那个表情——五年前的我有一种我现在已经不具备的松弛感，那是一种尚未被某些人生经历打磨过的、带着微量天真的从容。我忽然意识到，照片最残酷的功能不是记录你的样子，而是在未来的某一天让你意识到，你已经回不到那个样子了。每一张照片都是一个时间胶囊，里面封存的不只是你当时的外貌，还有你当时的心境、你当时还不知道即将失去的东西、以及你当时还天真地以为会永远持续的某种状态。我把那张照片放大，想从那个表情里读出更多的信息，但除了那种无法复制的松弛感之外，什么也读不出来。照片不讲情面。记忆可以美化、可以编辑。照片不行。它不关心你是否准备好了被审视，它只是在那一秒钟忠实地按下了快门，然后把那个你以为转瞬即逝的瞬间，变成了一个永远无法回避的提问：那个人去哪里了？
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ shǒujī xiàngcè de shíhou, wǒ fāxiàn le yī zhāng wǔ nián qián zài mǒu gè kāfēiguǎn pāi de zìpāi zhào. Zhàopiàn lǐ de nàge rén chuān zhe wǒ bú jìde zìjǐ yōngyǒu guò de yīfu, bèijǐng shì wǒ wánquán bú jìde qù guò de dìfāng, biǎoqíng jièyú wēixiào hé fādāi zhījiān, xiàng shì zài xiǎng yī jiàn yǐjīng bèi chèdǐ yíwàng de shìqíng. Wǒ dīng zhe zhè zhāng zhàopiàn kàn le hěn jiǔ, gǎnshòu dào yī zhǒng qítè de mòshēnggǎn——nàge rén quèshí shì wǒ, dàn yòu bù wánquán shì wǒ. Wǔ nián de shíjiān gǎibiàn le tài duō dōngxi: wǒ de liǎn shāowēi pàng le yīdiǎn, fàjìxiàn hòutuì le yī límǐ zuǒyòu, yǎnjiǎo duō le liǎng tiáo xì wén. Dàn zhèxiē wùlǐ biànhuà shì cìyào de, zhēnzhèng ràng wǒ gǎn dào mòshēng de shì nàge biǎoqíng——wǔ nián qián de wǒ yǒu yī zhǒng wǒ xiànzài yǐjīng bù jùbèi de sōngchí gǎn, nà shì yī zhǒng shàng wèi bèi mǒuxiē rénshēng jīnglì dǎmó guò de, dài zhe wēiliàng tiānzhēn de cóngróng. Wǒ hūrán yìshí dào, zhàopiàn zuì cánkù de gōngnéng bú shì jìlù nǐ de yàngzi, ér shì zài wèilái de mǒu yī tiān ràng nǐ yìshí dào, nǐ yǐjīng huí bú dào nàge yàngzi le. Měi yī zhāng zhàopiàn dōu shì yī gè shíjiān jiāonáng, lǐmiàn fēngcún de bù zhǐ shì nǐ dāngshí de wàimào, hái yǒu nǐ dāngshí de xīnjìng, nǐ dāngshí hái bù zhīdào jíjiāng shīqù de dōngxi, yǐjí nǐ dāngshí hái tiānzhēn de yǐwéi huì yǒngyuǎn chíxù de mǒu zhǒng zhuàngtài. Wǒ bǎ nà zhāng zhàopiàn fàngdà, xiǎng cóng nàge biǎoqíng lǐ dú chū gèng duō de xìnxī, dàn chúle nà zhǒng wúfǎ fùzhì de sōngchí gǎn zhīwài, shénme yě dú bù chūlái. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì zhàopiàn hé jìyì de gēnběn qūbié——jìyì shì kěyǐ bèi měihuà hé biānjí de, ér zhàopiàn shì bù jiǎng qíngmiàn de. Tā bù guānxīn nǐ shìfǒu zhǔnbèi hǎo le bèi shěnshì, tā zhǐ shì zài nà yī miǎozhōng zhōngshí de àn xià le kuàimén, ránhòu bǎ nàge nǐ yǐwéi zhuǎnshùn jí shì de shùnjiān, biànchéng le yī gè yǒngyuǎn wúfǎ huíbì de tíwèn: nàge rén qù nǎlǐ le?
text_en: While organizing my phone's photo album, I found a selfie taken in some cafe five years ago. The person in the photo wears clothes I don't remember owning, against a backdrop of a place I have no memory of visiting, with an expression between smiling and daydreaming, as if thinking about something now utterly forgotten. I stared at the photo for a long time and felt an odd sense of estrangement—that person is indeed me, yet not entirely me. Five years changed too many things: my face is slightly fuller, my hairline has retreated about a centimeter, two fine lines have appeared at the corners of my eyes. But these physical changes are secondary; what truly feels foreign is that expression—the me of five years ago possessed a relaxation I no longer have, a composure laced with a trace of naivete that certain life experiences had not yet honed away. A photograph's cruelest function is not recording what you look like, but making you realize on some future day that you can never return to that appearance. Every photograph is a time capsule preserving not just your looks at that moment, but your state of mind, the things you didn't yet know you were about to lose, and a certain condition you still naively believed would last forever. I enlarged the photo, trying to read more information from that expression, but beyond that irreproducible ease I could read nothing. Photographs are merciless. Memory can be embellished and edited. Photographs can’t.

title: The Warmth of Old Teapots
text_zh: 外公的紫砂壶已经用了四十多年，壶身上覆着一层岁月养出的温润包浆，手指抚上去，那种滑腻的触感仿佛在触摸凝固了的时间本身。他从不允许任何人用洗洁精去碰那把壶——「茶养壶，壶养茶，打断了这层关系，壶就只是一块泥了。」每天清晨，外公的第一件事不是洗脸刷牙，而是烧一壶水，等水温降到刚好不烫手的时候，缓缓注入那把紫砂壶里，让壶身慢慢醒来。他说好的茶壶和人一样，需要被温柔地唤醒，不能用滚水猛灌，那样会「伤了壶的脾气」。泡茶的过程在外公手里被赋予了一种近乎仪式性的庄严。他先用热水淋遍壶身，再轻轻拨开茶叶罐的盖子，用竹制的茶匙拨出恰好的分量。茶叶落入壶中的声音极其细微，像是干燥的秋叶落在棉布上。第一泡是洗茶，倒掉不喝；第二泡才是正式的开始，出汤的速度要快，不能闷——他总是说「闷久了茶就苦了，跟人憋着心事一样」。外公去世以后，那把壶传到了我手上。我第一次独自用它泡茶的那个早晨，壶盖揭开的瞬间，一股混合着陈年茶香和外公指纹上烟草气息的味道扑面而来。我忽然意识到，这把壶之所以温热，不仅仅是因为茶水的温度，更是因为四十年来无数次掌心的温度渗透进了每一寸紫砂的毛孔里。
text_pinyin: Wàigōng de zǐshā hú yǐjīng yòng le sìshí duō nián, hú shēn shàng fù zhe yī céng suìyuè yǎng chū de wēnrùn bāojiāng, shǒuzhǐ fǔ shàngqù, nà zhǒng huánì de chùgǎn fǎngfú zài chùmō nínggù le de shíjiān běnshēn. Tā cóng bù yǔnxǔ rènhé rén yòng xǐjiéjīng qù pèng nà bǎ hú——「chá yǎng hú, hú yǎng chá, dǎduàn le zhè céng guānxi, hú jiù zhǐ shì yí kuài ní le.」Měi tiān qīngchén, wàigōng de dì yī jiàn shì bú shì xǐ liǎn shuā yá, ér shì shāo yī hú shuǐ, děng shuǐ wēn jiàng dào gānghǎo bú tàng shǒu de shíhou, huǎnhuǎn zhùrù nà bǎ zǐshā hú lǐ, ràng hú shēn mànmàn xǐng lái. Tā shuō hǎo de cháhú hé rén yíyàng, xūyào bèi wēnróu de huànxǐng, bù néng yòng gǔnshuǐ měng guàn, nàyàng huì「shāng le hú de píqi」. Pào chá de guòchéng zài wàigōng shǒu lǐ bèi fùyǔ le yī zhǒng jìnhū yíshì xìng de zhuāngyán. Tā xiān yòng rè shuǐ lín biàn hú shēn, zài qīngqīng bō kāi cháyè guàn de gàizi, yòng zhúzhì de cháchí bō chū qiàhǎo de fènliàng. Cháyè luòrù hú zhōng de shēngyīn jíqí xìwēi, xiàng shì gānzào de qiūyè luò zài miánbù shàng. Dì yī pào shì xǐ chá, dào diào bù hē; dì èr pào cái shì zhèngshì de kāishǐ, chū tāng de sùdù yào kuài, bù néng mèn——tā zǒngshì shuō「mèn jiǔ le chá jiù kǔ le, gēn rén biē zhe xīnshì yíyàng」. Wàigōng qùshì yǐhòu, nà bǎ hú chuán dào le wǒ shǒu shàng. Wǒ dì yī cì dúzì yòng tā pào chá de nàge zǎochen, hú gài jiē kāi de shùnjiān, yī gǔ hùnhé zhe chénnián cháxiāng hé wàigōng zhǐwén shàng yāncǎo qìxī de wèidào pūmiàn ér lái. Wǒ hūrán yìshí dào, zhè bǎ hú zhīsuǒyǐ wēnrè, bù jǐnjǐn shì yīnwèi cháshuǐ de wēndù, gèng shì yīnwèi sìshí nián lái wúshù cì zhǎngxīn de wēndù shèntòu jìn le měi yī cùn zǐshā de máokǒng lǐ. Tā bú shì yí jiàn qìwù, ér shì yí duàn bèi nítǔ jìzhù de rénshēng.
text_en: Grandfather’s purple clay teapot has been in use over forty years. Its body is covered with a warm patina cultivated by time; your fingers glide over it like touching solidified time. He never allowed dish soap on it—‘Tea nourishes the pot, the pot nourishes the tea; break that relationship and the pot is just clay.’ Every morning, grandfather’s first act was boiling water, waiting until just below scalding, then slowly pouring it into the pot, letting the vessel gently awaken. A good teapot is like a person—rouse it gently, don’t blast it with boiling water. Brewing took on ritualistic solemnity in his hands. First rinse the pot’s body with hot water, then gently pry open the tea canister and use a bamboo scoop for the right amount. Tea leaves falling into the pot—extraordinarily faint, like dry autumn leaves on cotton cloth. The first steep was a rinse, poured away; the second the true beginning, and the pour had to be quick—‘steep too long and it turns bitter, just like a person holding in worries.’ After grandfather passed, the pot came to me. The first morning I brewed tea alone, lifting the lid, a scent washed over me—aged tea fragrance and the tobacco traces embedded in grandfather’s fingerprints. This pot’s warmth came not merely from tea temperature, but from forty years of palms whose warmth had seeped into every pore of the purple clay.

title: Calligraphy Practice at Dawn
text_zh: 父亲写毛笔字只在天亮之前。他说那是一天中最干净的时刻——万物尚未醒来，空气里没有杂念，墨与纸之间的对话才能不被打扰。冬天的凌晨四点半，窗外漆黑一片，父亲书房里的台灯是整条巷子里唯一的光源。他先研墨，不用现成的墨汁，坚持用老墨条在端砚上一圈一圈地慢磨。他说研墨的过程就是静心的过程，「墨磨人，人磨墨」，等到墨汁浓淡恰好的时候，心也就沉下来了。父亲临的是颜真卿的《祭侄文稿》，那是被称为「天下第二行书」的名帖。他临了整整二十年，从来没有觉得自己写得够好。他说颜真卿写那篇文稿的时候满腔悲愤，笔锋里带着对侄子战死沙场的哀痛，那种感情不是技法能模仿的。「你可以临其形，却临不了其心。但你一遍一遍地写，写到某一天忽然经历了类似的悲伤，再提笔的时候，那些枯笔飞白忽然就懂了。」我小时候不理解他为什么要在别人都在睡觉的时候独自起来写字。长大以后才慢慢明白，那段天亮之前的独处时光，是父亲给自己保留的一小片精神领地。在那里，他不是任何人的丈夫、父亲或者同事，他只是一个与笔墨独处的人。窗外第一缕晨光照进来的时候，他会把写好的字铺在地上，远远地端详片刻，然后无论满意与否，都把它们叠好收起。那些字从不示人，仿佛它们存在的意义不在于被欣赏，而在于书写本身那个安静的、与自己对话的过程。
text_pinyin: Fùqīn xiě máobǐ zì zhǐ zài tiān liàng zhīqián. Tā shuō nà shì yī tiān zhōng zuì gānjìng de shíkè——wànwù shàngwèi xǐng lái, kōngqì lǐ méiyǒu zániàn, mò yǔ zhǐ zhījiān de duìhuà cái néng bú bèi dǎrǎo. Dōngtiān de língchén sì diǎn bàn, chuāng wài qīhēi yí piàn, fùqīn shūfáng lǐ de táidēng shì zhěng tiáo xiàngzi lǐ wéiyī de guāngyuán. Tā xiān yán mò, bú yòng xiànchéng de mòzhī, jiānchí yòng lǎo mò tiáo zài duānyàn shàng yī quān yī quān de màn mó. Tā shuō yán mò de guòchéng jiù shì jìngxīn de guòchéng,「mò mó rén, rén mó mò」, děng dào mòzhī nóngdàn qiàhǎo de shíhou, xīn yě jiù chén xiàlái le. Fùqīn lín de shì Yán Zhēnqīng de《Jì Zhí Wéngǎo》, nà shì bèi chēngwéi「tiānxià dì èr xíngshū」de míng tiè. Tā lín le zhěngzhěng èrshí nián, cónglái méiyǒu juéde zìjǐ xiě de gòu hǎo. Tā shuō Yán Zhēnqīng xiě nà piān wéngǎo de shíhou mǎnqiāng bēifèn, bǐfēng lǐ dài zhe duì zhízi zhànsǐ shāchǎng de āitòng, nà zhǒng gǎnqíng bú shì jìfǎ néng mófǎng de.「Nǐ kěyǐ lín qí xíng, què lín bù liǎo qí xīn. Dàn nǐ yí biàn yí biàn de xiě, xiě dào mǒu yī tiān hūrán jīnglì le lèisì de bēishāng, zài tí bǐ de shíhou, nàxiē kūbǐ fēibái hūrán jiù dǒng le.」Wǒ xiǎo shíhou bù lǐjiě tā wèishénme yào zài biéren dōu zài shuìjiào de shíhou dúzì qǐlái xiě zì. Zhǎngdà yǐhòu cái mànmàn míngbai, nà duàn tiān liàng zhīqián de dúchǔ shíguāng, shì fùqīn gěi zìjǐ bǎoliú de yī xiǎo piàn jīngshén lǐngdì. Zài nàlǐ, tā bú shì rènhé rén de zhàngfu, fùqīn huòzhě tóngshì, tā zhǐ shì yí gè yǔ bǐmò dúchǔ de rén. Chuāng wài dì yī lǚ chénguāng zhào jìnlái de shíhou, tā huì bǎ xiě hǎo de zì pū zài dì shàng, yuǎnyuǎn de duānxiáng piànkè, ránhòu wúlùn mǎnyì yǔ fǒu, dōu bǎ tāmen dié hǎo shōu qǐ. Nàxiē zì cóng bú shì rén, fǎngfú tāmen cúnzài de yìyì bú zàiyú bèi xīnshǎng, ér zàiyú shūxiě běnshēn nàge ānjìng de, yǔ zìjǐ duìhuà de guòchéng.
text_en: Father only practiced calligraphy before dawn—the cleanest moment of the day, all things still asleep, so the conversation between ink and paper could proceed undisturbed. Half past four on a winter morning, the world outside pitch black, his desk lamp the only light on the lane. He would first grind ink—never ready-made, insisting on slowly grinding an old ink stick on an end inkstone. Grinding ink was calming the mind: ‘the ink grinds the person, the person grinds the ink.’ By the time the consistency was right, the heart had settled too. Father copied Yan Zhenqing’s ‘Draft of a Requiem to My Nephew,’ ‘the second-greatest running script under heaven.’ Twenty years of copying, never once satisfied. When Yan Zhenqing wrote that draft, he was filled with grief—his brush strokes carrying the anguish of a nephew killed in battle. ‘You can copy its form but not its heart. Yet you write it again and again, and one day when you experience a similar sorrow, those dry strokes and flying whites suddenly make sense.’ As a child I didn’t understand why he rose alone to write while everyone slept. Only later did I grasp that those predawn hours were a territory of spirit he’d reserved for himself—there he was no one’s husband, father, or colleague, just a person alone with brush and ink. When the first morning light entered, he’d spread the characters on the floor, contemplate them briefly, then fold them away, satisfied or not. Those characters were never shown to anyone—their meaning lay not in being admired, but in the quiet act of writing itself.

questions.q_zh: 父亲说「你可以临其形，却临不了其心」是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does the father mean by 'you can copy its form, but you cannot copy its heart'?

options.text: 临帖可以模仿笔画的外形但无法复制书写者当时的情感
options.pinyin: lín tiè kěyǐ mófǎng bǐhuà de wàixíng dàn wúfǎ fùzhì shūxiě zhě dāngshí de gǎnqíng
options.text_en: Copying can imitate the outward form of strokes but cannot replicate the writer's emotions at the time

options.text: 颜真卿的书法技巧太高超无人能学会
options.pinyin: Yán Zhēnqīng de shūfǎ jìqiǎo tài gāochāo wú rén néng xuéhuì
options.text_en: Yan Zhenqing's calligraphy technique is too advanced for anyone to learn

options.text: 毛笔字的核心在于心脏的跳动节奏
options.pinyin: máobǐ zì de héxīn zàiyú xīnzàng de tiàodòng jiézòu
options.text_en: The core of brush calligraphy lies in the rhythm of the heartbeat

questions.explanation: The father explains that copying can replicate brush strokes' external form but cannot reproduce the original calligrapher's emotional state during writing.

questions.q_zh: 父亲写完的字为什么从不示人？
questions.q_en: Why did the father never show his finished calligraphy to anyone?

options.text: 因为他对自己的字不满意觉得丢人
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā duì zìjǐ de zì bù mǎnyì juéde diūrén
options.text_en: Because he was dissatisfied with his work and felt embarrassed

options.text: 因为书写的意义在于那个安静的与自己对话的过程而非被欣赏
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shūxiě de yìyì zàiyú nàge ānjìng de yǔ zìjǐ duìhuà de guòchéng ér fēi bèi xīnshǎng
options.text_en: Because the meaning of writing lies in the quiet process of conversing with oneself, not in being admired

options.text: 因为他的书法老师禁止他把练习作品给别人看
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā de shūfǎ lǎoshī jìnzhǐ tā bǎ liànxí zuòpǐn gěi biéren kàn
options.text_en: Because his calligraphy teacher forbade him from showing practice work to others

questions.explanation: The father never displayed his work because the value was in the meditative process of writing, not in producing something for others to admire.

title: A Grandmother's Sewing Box
text_zh: 奶奶的针线盒是一个月饼铁盒，盒盖上印着褪色的嫦娥奔月图案。打开盒盖，里面的世界比任何一个百宝箱都更让幼时的我着迷。最上面一层是各色丝线，被奶奶按照深浅排列得整整齐齐，像一道微缩的彩虹。丝线下面是一排银针，大小粗细各不相同，插在一个手工缝制的小棉垫上。棉垫里塞的是奶奶攒了多年的头发——她说头发上的油脂能防止银针生锈，这是她母亲教给她的。盒子最底层藏着各种零碎：几粒备用的纽扣、一小卷松紧带、半块裁衣用的划粉、一把只剩一条腿的旧圆规。每一件东西都有它的来历和用途，奶奶如数家珍。那把断腿圆规是爷爷年轻时做木工活用的，爷爷去世后奶奶舍不得扔，就收进了针线盒里，虽然它在缝纫中毫无用处。奶奶补衣服的手艺堪称一绝。她能在一件深色外套上补出一个完全看不出痕迹的补丁——针脚细密到要凑近了用放大镜才看得出来。她说缝补的最高境界不是「看不出补过」，而是「让人知道补过但觉得比原来更好看」。有一次我的校服袖口磨破了，她用一小块碎花布缝了一个精致的贴边，不但遮住了破洞，反而成了一个独一无二的装饰。奶奶去世后，母亲把那个月饼铁盒原封不动地保存了下来。有时候我会打开它，不为找什么东西，只是想闻一闻盒子里那股混合着棉线、铁锈和樟脑丸的气味——那是奶奶双手的味道，是一整个已经消逝的手工年代的味道。
text_pinyin: Nǎinai de zhēnxiàn hé shì yí gè yuèbǐng tiě hé, hé gài shàng yìn zhe tuìsè de Cháng'é bēnyuè tú'àn. Dǎkāi hé gài, lǐmiàn de shìjiè bǐ rènhé yí gè bǎibǎo xiāng dōu gèng ràng yòushí de wǒ zháomí. Zuì shàngmiàn yī céng shì gè sè sīxiàn, bèi nǎinai ànzhào shēnqiǎn páiliè de zhěngzhěngqíqí, xiàng yí dào wēisuō de cǎihóng. Sīxiàn xiàmiàn shì yī pái yínzhēn, dàxiǎo cūxì gè bù xiāngtóng, chā zài yí gè shǒugōng féngzhì de xiǎo miándiàn shàng. Miándiàn lǐ sāi de shì nǎinai zǎn le duō nián de tóufa——tā shuō tóufa shàng de yóuzhī néng fángzhǐ yínzhēn shēngxiù, zhè shì tā mǔqīn jiāo gěi tā de. Hézi zuì dǐcéng cáng zhe gè zhǒng língsuì: jǐ lì bèiyòng de niǔkòu, yī xiǎo juǎn sōngjǐndài, bàn kuài cáiyī yòng de huàfěn, yī bǎ zhǐ shèng yī tiáo tuǐ de jiù yuánguī. Měi yí jiàn dōngxi dōu yǒu tā de láilì hé yòngtú, nǎinai rúshùjiāzhēn. Nà bǎ duàn tuǐ yuánguī shì yéye niánqīng shí zuò mùgōng huó yòng de, yéye qùshì hòu nǎinai shěbude rēng, jiù shōu jìn le zhēnxiàn hé lǐ, suīrán tā zài féngrèn zhōng háo wú yòngchù. Nǎinai bǔ yīfu de shǒuyì kānchēng yī jué. Tā néng zài yí jiàn shēnsè wàitào shàng bǔ chū yí gè wánquán kàn bù chū hénjì de bǔdīng——zhēnjiǎo xìmì dào yào còujìn le yòng fàngdàjìng cái kàn de chūlái. Tā shuō féngbǔ de zuìgāo jìngjiè bú shì「kàn bù chū bǔ guò」, ér shì「ràng rén zhīdào bǔ guò dàn juéde bǐ yuánlái gèng hǎokàn」. Yǒu yī cì wǒ de xiàofú xiùkǒu mó pò le, tā yòng yī xiǎo kuài suìhuā bù féng le yí gè jīngzhì de tiēbiān, búdàn zhēzhù le pòdòng, fǎn'ér chéng le yí gè dúyīwú'èr de zhuāngshì. Nǎinai qùshì hòu, mǔqīn bǎ nàge yuèbǐng tiě hé yuánfēng bú dòng de bǎocún le xiàlái. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì dǎkāi tā, bú wèi zhǎo shénme dōngxi, zhǐ shì xiǎng wén yī wén hézi lǐ nà gǔ hùnhé zhe miánxiàn, tiěxiù hé zhāngnǎo wán de qìwèi——nà shì nǎinai shuāngshǒu de wèidào, shì yí zhěnggè yǐjīng xiāoshì de shǒugōng niándài de wèidào.
text_en: Grandmother’s sewing box was a mooncake tin, its lid printed with a faded Chang’e. Opening it fascinated my childhood self more than any treasure chest. Top layer: threads of every color, arranged dark to light—a miniature rainbow. Beneath, a row of silver needles stuck into a hand-sewn cotton pad. Inside the pad: hair grandmother had saved over years. Hair oil prevents silver needles from rusting—a trick her mother taught her. The bottom hid odds and ends: spare buttons, elastic, half a block of tailor’s chalk, a compass missing one leg. Every item had its origin. The broken compass had been grandfather’s when he did carpentry; after he died, grandmother tucked it in, though it served no purpose in needlework. Her mending was exceptional. She could patch a dark coat so seamlessly you’d need a magnifying glass to detect the stitches. The highest level of mending was not ‘making it look unpatched,’ but ‘letting people see it was patched yet feeling it looks better than before.’ When my school uniform cuff wore through, she sewed on a floral edging that covered the hole and became a one-of-a-kind decoration. After grandmother passed, mother preserved the tin exactly as it was. Sometimes I open it, not looking for anything—just wanting that blend of cotton thread, rust, and camphor. The scent of grandmother’s hands. The scent of an entire vanished age.

questions.q_zh: 奶奶为什么把爷爷的旧圆规收进了针线盒里？
questions.q_en: Why did grandmother keep grandfather's old compass in the sewing box?

options.text: 因为圆规在缝纫中有特殊的用途
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yuánguī zài féngrèn zhōng yǒu tèshū de yòngtú
options.text_en: Because the compass had a special use in sewing

options.text: 因为爷爷去世后奶奶舍不得扔掉它
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yéye qùshì hòu nǎinai shěbude rēngdiào tā
options.text_en: Because grandmother could not bear to throw it away after grandfather died

options.text: 因为圆规可以用来量布料的尺寸
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yuánguī kěyǐ yòng lái liáng bùliào de chǐcun
options.text_en: Because the compass could be used to measure fabric dimensions

questions.explanation: Grandmother could not bear to discard grandfather's compass after his death, keeping it in her sewing box as a memento.

questions.q_zh: 奶奶认为缝补的最高境界是什么？
questions.q_en: What did grandmother consider the highest level of mending?

options.text: 让人完全看不出补过的痕迹
options.pinyin: ràng rén wánquán kàn bù chū bǔ guò de hénjì
options.text_en: Making it completely undetectable that it was mended

options.text: 让人知道补过但觉得比原来更好看
options.pinyin: ràng rén zhīdào bǔ guò dàn juéde bǐ yuánlái gèng hǎokàn
options.text_en: Letting people see it was mended yet feeling it looks even better than before

options.text: 用最短的时间完成缝补工作
options.pinyin: yòng zuì duǎn de shíjiān wánchéng féngbǔ gōngzuò
options.text_en: Completing the mending in the shortest possible time

questions.explanation: Grandmother considered the highest mending to be work visible as repaired yet perceived as looking even better than the original.

title: The Sound of Bamboo in Wind
text_zh: 后院那片竹林是祖父亲手栽下的，算起来已经有六十余年的光景了。竹子这种植物有一种令人敬畏的生命力——地面上看到的每一根竹竿，地下都连着一张错综复杂的根系网络，彼此相通、互为依存。祖父生前常说，竹子是最懂得「群居」的植物：一根竹子成不了林，但只要根系扎稳了，一夜之间就能冒出几十根新笋。风起的时候，竹林会发出一种极其独特的声响。那不是树木被风吹动时的沙沙声，而是一种更为清越的、近乎金属质感的碰撞声——竹竿与竹竿之间轻轻触碰，发出「叮」「咚」的细响，像是有人在用一种失传的乐器演奏一支即兴的曲子。风小的时候声音稀疏，只有几声零星的轻叩；风大的时候整片竹林都在摇曳，那种声音就汇成了一片密集的、此起彼伏的合奏，仿佛竹林自身就是一架巨大的风铃。我最喜欢在夏天的傍晚搬一把藤椅坐在竹林边上，闭着眼睛只听风竹之声。暮色渐浓，蝉鸣渐弱，竹声便从白天的喧嚣中慢慢浮出水面，变成黄昏里最后的独奏者。那种声音有一种奇妙的安抚力量——它不像白噪音那样均匀单调，而是有起伏、有间歇、有意外，但每一个音符都是轻柔的、非攻击性的。它提醒你世界还在运转，风还在吹，生命还在生长，但这一切都不需要你的参与，你只需要坐在那里，安静地做一个倾听者。祖父去世后的第一个春天，竹林深处冒出了异常多的新笋，密密麻麻地挤在一起，像是大地在用自己的方式纪念一个栽竹的人。
text_pinyin: Hòuyuàn nà piàn zhúlín shì zǔfù qīnshǒu zāi xià de, suàn qǐlái yǐjīng yǒu liùshí yú nián de guāngjǐng le. Zhúzi zhè zhǒng zhíwù yǒu yī zhǒng lìng rén jìngwèi de shēngmìnglì——dìmiàn shàng kàn dào de měi yī gēn zhúgān, dìxià dōu lián zhe yī zhāng cuòzōng fùzá de gēnxì wǎngluò, bǐcǐ xiāngtōng, hùwéi yīcún. Zǔfù shēngqián cháng shuō, zhúzi shì zuì dǒngde「qúnjū」de zhíwù: yī gēn zhúzi chéng bù liǎo lín, dàn zhǐyào gēnxì zhā wěn le, yí yè zhījiān jiù néng mào chū jǐshí gēn xīn sǔn. Fēng qǐ de shíhou, zhúlín huì fāchū yī zhǒng jíqí dútè de shēngxiǎng. Nà bú shì shùmù bèi fēng chuī dòng shí de shāshā shēng, ér shì yī zhǒng gèngwéi qīngyuè de, jìnhū jīnshǔ zhìgǎn de pèngzhuàng shēng——zhúgān yǔ zhúgān zhījiān qīngqīng chùpèng, fāchū「dīng」「dōng」de xì xiǎng, xiàng shì yǒu rén zài yòng yī zhǒng shīchuán de yuèqì yǎnzòu yī zhī jíxìng de qǔzi. Fēng xiǎo de shíhou shēngyīn xīshū, zhǐ yǒu jǐ shēng língxīng de qīng kòu; fēng dà de shíhou zhěng piàn zhúlín dōu zài yáoyè, nà zhǒng shēngyīn jiù huì chéng le yí piàn mìjí de, cǐqǐbǐfú de hézòu, fǎngfú zhúlín zìshēn jiù shì yí jià jùdà de fēnglíng. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan zài xiàtiān de bàngwǎn bān yī bǎ téngyǐ zuò zài zhúlín biān shàng, bì zhe yǎnjīng zhǐ tīng fēng zhú zhī shēng. Mùsè jiàn nóng, chánmíng jiàn ruò, zhú shēng biàn cóng báitiān de xuānxiāo zhōng mànmàn fú chū shuǐmiàn, biàn chéng huánghūn lǐ zuìhòu de dúzòu zhě. Nà zhǒng shēngyīn yǒu yī zhǒng qímiào de ānfǔ lìliàng——tā bú xiàng bái zàoyīn nàyàng jūnyún dāndiào, ér shì yǒu qǐfú, yǒu jiānxiē, yǒu yìwài, dàn měi yí gè yīnfú dōu shì qīngróu de, fēi gōngjī xìng de. Tā tíxǐng nǐ shìjiè hái zài yùnzhuǎn, fēng hái zài chuī, shēngmìng hái zài shēngzhǎng, dàn zhè yīqiè dōu bù xūyào nǐ de cānyù, nǐ zhǐ xūyào zuò zài nàlǐ, ānjìng de zuò yí gè qīngtīng zhě. Zǔfù qùshì hòu de dì yī gè chūntiān, zhúlín shēnchù mào chū le yìcháng duō de xīn sǔn, mìmìmámá de jǐ zài yīqǐ, xiàng shì dàdì zài yòng zìjǐ de fāngshì jìniàn yí gè zāi zhú de rén.
text_en: The backyard bamboo grove was planted by grandfather some sixty years ago. Bamboo has an awe-inspiring vitality—every culm above ground connects to an intricate root network below, all interconnected. Grandfather said bamboo best understands ‘communal living’: a single bamboo can’t make a grove, but once roots take hold, dozens of shoots can emerge overnight. When wind rises, the grove produces a unique sound—not the rustling of swayed trees, but something more crystalline, almost metallic. Culms touching one another make delicate ‘ding’ and ‘dong’ sounds, as if someone played an impromptu piece on a lost instrument. In gentle breezes, scattered light taps; in strong winds, the sounds converge into a dense, undulating ensemble—the grove itself an enormous wind chime. My favorite thing: carrying a rattan chair to the grove’s edge on summer evenings, closing my eyes, listening. As dusk deepened and cicadas faded, the bamboo sound surfaced from daytime noise, becoming the last soloist of twilight. That sound had a wondrous soothing power—not uniform like white noise, but with rises and falls, pauses and surprises, yet every note gentle. It reminded you the world was still turning, wind still blowing, life still growing, but none required your participation—you need only sit and listen. The first spring after grandfather’s passing, an extraordinary number of new shoots emerged, crowded densely, as if the earth were commemorating the man who planted them.

questions.q_zh: 竹林发出的声音为什么与普通树木不同？
questions.q_en: Why is the sound the bamboo grove makes different from ordinary trees?

options.text: 因为竹竿之间的碰撞发出近乎金属质感的清越声响
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhúgān zhījiān de pèngzhuàng fāchū jìnhū jīnshǔ zhìgǎn de qīngyuè shēngxiǎng
options.text_en: Because the collisions between bamboo culms produce a crystalline sound with an almost metallic quality

options.text: 因为竹子的叶片比其他树木更厚更重
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhúzi de yèpiàn bǐ qítā shùmù gèng hòu gèng zhòng
options.text_en: Because bamboo leaves are thicker and heavier than those of other trees

options.text: 因为竹林里住着很多鸟会一起鸣叫
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhúlín lǐ zhù zhe hěn duō niǎo huì yīqǐ míngjiào
options.text_en: Because many birds live in the bamboo grove and sing together

questions.explanation: Bamboo culms colliding produce 'a crystalline sound with an almost metallic quality,' distinct from the soft rustling of leafy trees.

questions.q_zh: 祖父去世后的第一个春天，竹林发生了什么？
questions.q_en: What happened in the bamboo grove the first spring after grandfather's death?

options.text: 竹林因为无人照料而渐渐枯萎了
options.pinyin: zhúlín yīnwèi wú rén zhàoliào ér jiànjiàn kūwěi le
options.text_en: The bamboo grove gradually withered without anyone to tend it

options.pinyin: zhúlín shēnchù mào chū le yìcháng duō de xīn sǔn
options.text_en: An extraordinary number of new shoots emerged from deep within the grove

options.text: 家人把竹林砍掉改种了果树

options.text_en: The family cut down the bamboo and planted fruit trees instead

questions.explanation: An extraordinary number of new shoots emerged the first spring after grandfather's death, as though the grove were responding to his absence.

title: Autumn Persimmons Drying
text_zh: 霜降过后，村子里家家户户的屋檐下就挂满了一串串橘红色的柿子，像是给灰扑扑的土墙系上了一排排小灯笼。晾柿饼是一件需要耐心和天时配合的事情。首先要选果——太硬的不行，涩味重；太软的也不行，晾到一半就会烂掉。外婆总是亲自去果园里一个一个地捏，用拇指和食指轻轻按压柿子的腰部，凭借几十年的手感判断每一颗的成熟度。选好的柿子要先削皮，削下来的皮也不扔，铺在竹匾上晒干，留着以后泡水喝——外婆说柿子皮泡的水有一股淡淡的甜香，冬天喝了暖胃。削好皮的柿子用棉线系在竹竿上，一根竿子挂十几个，再把竹竿架在屋檐下通风处。接下来就是等待。柿子需要在秋天干燥的风中慢慢脱水，白天晒太阳，晚上吹凉风，如此反复大约三周。在这个过程中，柿子的颜色会从鲜亮的橘红渐渐变深，表面开始出现细密的皱纹，糖分慢慢渗出结晶，形成一层薄薄的白霜。外婆说这层白霜叫「柿霜」，是整颗柿饼最珍贵的部分——入口即化，甜而不腻。晾柿饼最令人焦虑的是天气。如果连续下了几天雨，柿子来不及脱水就会发霉，一季的辛苦就前功尽弃了。所以每到晾柿子的季节，外婆总是格外关注天气预报，遇上阴天就提前把竹竿收进屋里。那些年的秋天，空气中总是弥漫着一股柿子特有的清甜气息，混合着稻草和泥土的味道。现在想起来，那或许就是故乡秋天应该有的味道。
text_pinyin: Shuāngjiàng guòhòu, cūnzi lǐ jiājiāhùhù de wūyán xià jiù guà mǎn le yī chuàn chuàn júhóngsè de shìzi, xiàng shì gěi huīpūpū de tǔqiáng jì shàng le yī pái pái xiǎo dēnglóng. Liàng shìbǐng shì yí jiàn xūyào nàixīn hé tiānshí pèihé de shìqíng. Shǒuxiān yào xuǎn guǒ——tài yìng de bù xíng, sèwèi zhòng; tài ruǎn de yě bù xíng, liàng dào yíbàn jiù huì làn diào. Wàipó zǒngshì qīnzì qù guǒyuán lǐ yí gè yí gè de niē, yòng mǔzhǐ hé shízhǐ qīngqīng ànyā shìzi de yāobù, píngjiè jǐshí nián de shǒugǎn pànduàn měi yī kē de chéngshúdù. Xuǎn hǎo de shìzi yào xiān xuē pí, xuē xiàlái de pí yě bù rēng, pū zài zhúbiǎn shàng shài gān, liú zhe yǐhòu pào shuǐ hē——wàipó shuō shìzi pí pào de shuǐ yǒu yī gǔ dàndàn de tiánxiāng, dōngtiān hē le nuǎn wèi. Xuē hǎo pí de shìzi yòng miánxiàn jì zài zhúgān shàng, yī gēn gānzi guà shí jǐ gè, zài bǎ zhúgān jià zài wūyán xià tōngfēng chù. Jiēxiàlái jiù shì děngdài. Shìzi xūyào zài qiūtiān gānzào de fēng zhōng mànmàn tuōshuǐ, báitiān shài tàiyáng, wǎnshàng chuī liángfēng, rúcǐ fǎnfù dàyuē sān zhōu. Zài zhège guòchéng zhōng, shìzi de yánsè huì cóng xiānliàng de júhóng jiànjiàn biàn shēn, biǎomiàn kāishǐ chūxiàn xìmì de zhòuwén, tángfèn mànmàn shèn chū jiéjīng, xíngchéng yī céng báobáo de báishuāng. Wàipó shuō zhè céng báishuāng jiào「shìshuāng」, shì zhěng kē shìbǐng zuì zhēnguì de bùfen——rùkǒu jí huà, tián ér bú nì. Liàng shìbǐng zuì lìng rén jiāolǜ de shì tiānqì. Rúguǒ liánxù xià le jǐ tiān yǔ, shìzi láibují tuōshuǐ jiù huì fāméi, yī jì de xīnkǔ jiù qiángōngjìnqì le. Suǒyǐ měi dào liàng shìzi de jìjié, wàipó zǒngshì géwài guānzhù tiānqì yùbào, yù shàng yīntiān jiù tíqián bǎ zhúgān shōu jìn wū lǐ. Nàxiē nián de qiūtiān, kōngqì zhōng zǒngshì mímàn zhe yī gǔ shìzi tèyǒu de qīngtián qìxī, hùnhé zhe dàocǎo hé nítǔ de wèidào. Xiànzài xiǎng qǐlái, nà huòxǔ jiù shì gùxiāng qiūtiān yīnggāi yǒu de wèidào.
text_en: After the Frost Descent solar term, strings of orange-red persimmons hang beneath every village eave, like small lanterns tied to dusty earthen walls. Drying persimmon cakes requires patience and weather’s cooperation. First: selecting fruit—too firm and the astringency is strong, too soft and they rot mid-drying. Grandmother went to the orchard herself, pressing each persimmon’s waist between thumb and forefinger, judging ripeness through decades of touch. Selected persimmons are peeled—even the peels kept, spread on bamboo trays to dry for steeping later. Persimmon-peel water has a faint sweetness and warms the stomach in winter. Peeled persimmons are tied with cotton string to bamboo poles, a dozen per pole, propped beneath the eaves. Then the waiting. They dehydrate slowly in dry autumn wind—sun by day, breeze by night—for roughly three weeks. Color deepens from bright orange-red, fine wrinkles appear, sugar seeps out and crystallizes into a thin white frost. Grandmother called this ‘persimmon bloom’—the most precious part. It dissolves on the tongue, sweet but never cloying. The anxiety of drying persimmons is weather. Days of rain mean mold, and the season’s labor is wasted. So grandmother watched forecasts closely, bringing poles inside at the first overcast sky. In those autumns, the air was suffused with the persimmon’s clean sweetness, mingled with rice straw and earth. That was what autumn in one’s hometown smelled like.

questions.q_zh: 外婆说的「柿霜」是怎么形成的？
questions.q_en: How is the 'persimmon bloom' that grandmother described formed?

options.text: 人工在柿饼表面撒上一层糖粉
options.pinyin: réngōng zài shìbǐng biǎomiàn sā shàng yī céng tángfěn
options.text_en: Sugar powder is manually sprinkled on the persimmon's surface

options.text: 柿子在脱水过程中糖分慢慢渗出结晶形成的
options.pinyin: shìzi zài tuōshuǐ guòchéng zhōng tángfèn mànmàn shèn chū jiéjīng xíngchéng de
options.text_en: Sugar slowly seeps out and crystallizes during the dehydration process

options.text: 冬天的霜冻凝结在柿子表面形成的
options.pinyin: dōngtiān de shuāngdòng níngjié zài shìzi biǎomiàn xíngchéng de
options.text_en: Winter frost condenses on the persimmon's surface

questions.explanation: The white 'bloom' forms as sugar gradually seeps out of the fruit and crystallizes on the surface during dehydration.

questions.q_zh: 晾柿饼最令人焦虑的因素是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the most anxiety-inducing factor when drying persimmons?

options.text: 选不到成熟度合适的柿子
options.pinyin: xuǎn bú dào chéngshúdù héshì de shìzi
options.text_en: Not being able to find persimmons of the right ripeness

options.text: 连续下雨导致柿子发霉前功尽弃
options.pinyin: liánxù xiàyǔ dǎozhì shìzi fāméi qiángōngjìnqì
options.text_en: Consecutive rain causing the persimmons to mold and ruining all the work

options.text: 邻居家的柿饼做得比自己家的好

options.text_en: The neighbors' dried persimmons turning out better than one's own

questions.explanation: Identifies consecutive rain as the greatest threat, causing mold that can ruin an entire batch of persimmons already hung to dry.

title: The Night Watchman's Lantern
text_zh: 小镇上最后一个打更的人叫老陈，他在我童年的记忆里始终是一个半明半暗的轮廓——提着一盏昏黄的纸灯笼，敲着竹梆子，沿着青石板路慢慢地走。「天干物燥，小心火烛——」他的声音不高不低，刚好能穿透薄薄的窗纸，飘进每一户人家的梦里。打更这个职业在那时已经是一种近乎荒诞的存在了。镇上早就通了电，家家都有闹钟，没有人真的需要靠打更来知道时辰。但老陈依然每晚准时出门，风雨无阻，仿佛他守的不是时间，而是某种更古老、更深沉的秩序。母亲告诉我，老陈年轻时是镇上小学的语文老师，退休以后闲不住，就主动接了打更的活。他自己做了那盏灯笼——竹篾为骨，糊上桃花纸，底部搁一截蜡烛。灯笼的光是暖色的，不像电灯那样锋利，而是柔柔的、带着一圈橘黄的晕染，走到哪里就把哪里的黑暗温柔地推开一小块。冬天的夜晚最难熬，老陈会在棉袄外面再裹一件油布斗篷，灯笼挂在一根弯头的竹竿上，一手提竿一手敲梆。有时候雪下得大了，他走过之后留下一串浅浅的脚印，到天亮时又被新雪覆盖，仿佛他从未来过。老陈去世的那一年，镇上安静了许多。夜里再也没有那声悠长的「小心火烛」从巷子深处飘来。人们这才意识到，老陈打更的意义从来不在于报时，而在于他那盏灯笼给了无数个深夜一种「有人在看顾」的安心感——哪怕你从未醒来看过那盏灯，但你知道它在那里，这就够了。
text_pinyin: Xiǎo zhèn shàng zuìhòu yí gè dǎ gēng de rén jiào Lǎo Chén, tā zài wǒ tóngnián de jìyì lǐ shǐzhōng shì yí gè bànmíng bàn'àn de lúnkuò——tí zhe yī zhǎn hūnhuáng de zhǐ dēnglóng, qiāo zhe zhú bāngzi, yánzhe qīngshíbǎn lù mànmàn de zǒu.「Tiān gān wù zào, xiǎoxīn huǒzhú——」tā de shēngyīn bù gāo bú dī, gānghǎo néng chuāntòu báobáo de chuāngzhǐ, piāo jìn měi yī hù rénjiā de mèng lǐ. Dǎ gēng zhège zhíyè zài nà shí yǐjīng shì yī zhǒng jìnhū huāngdàn de cúnzài le. Zhèn shàng zǎo jiù tōng le diàn, jiājiā dōu yǒu nàozhōng, méiyǒu rén zhēn de xūyào kào dǎ gēng lái zhīdào shíchén. Dàn Lǎo Chén yīrán měi wǎn zhǔnshí chūmén, fēngyǔ wúzǔ, fǎngfú tā shǒu de bú shì shíjiān, ér shì mǒu zhǒng gèng gǔlǎo, gèng shēnchén de zhìxù. Mǔqīn gàosu wǒ, Lǎo Chén niánqīng shí shì zhèn shàng xiǎoxué de yǔwén lǎoshī, tuìxiū yǐhòu xiánbuzhù, jiù zhǔdòng jiē le dǎ gēng de huó. Tā zìjǐ zuò le nà zhǎn dēnglóng——zhúmiè wéi gǔ, hù shàng táohuā zhǐ, dǐbù gē yī jié làzhú. Dēnglóng de guāng shì nuǎnsè de, bú xiàng diàndēng nàyàng fēnglì, ér shì róuróu de, dài zhe yī quān júhuáng de yūnrǎn, zǒu dào nǎlǐ jiù bǎ nǎlǐ de hēi'àn wēnróu de tuī kāi yī xiǎo kuài. Dōngtiān de yèwǎn zuì nán'áo, Lǎo Chén huì zài mián'ǎo wàimiàn zài guǒ yī jiàn yóubù dǒupéng, dēnglóng guà zài yī gēn wāntóu de zhúgān shàng, yī shǒu tí gān yī shǒu qiāo bāng. Yǒu shíhou xuě xià de dà le, tā zǒu guò zhīhòu liú xià yī chuàn qiǎnqiǎn de jiǎoyìn, dào tiān liàng shí yòu bèi xīn xuě fùgài, fǎngfú tā cóng wèi lái guò. Lǎo Chén qùshì de nà yī nián, zhèn shàng ānjìng le xǔduō. Yè lǐ zài yě méiyǒu nà shēng yōucháng de「xiǎoxīn huǒzhú」cóng xiàngzi shēnchù piāo lái. Rénmen zhè cái yìshí dào, Lǎo Chén dǎ gēng de yìyì cónglái bú zàiyú bàoshí, ér zàiyú tā nà zhǎn dēnglóng gěi le wúshù gè shēnyè yī zhǒng「yǒu rén zài kāngù」de ānxīn gǎn——nǎpà nǐ cóng wèi xǐng lái kàn guò nà zhǎn dēng, dàn nǐ zhīdào tā zài nàlǐ, zhè jiù gòu le.
text_en: The last night watchman in the small town was called Old Chen. In childhood memory he remains a half-lit, half-shadowed silhouette—carrying a dim yellow paper lantern, tapping a bamboo clapper, walking slowly along the bluestone lane. ‘The air is dry and things brittle—mind your candles—’ His voice pitched just enough to penetrate thin window paper and drift into dreams. The profession was already nearly absurd by then. The town had electricity, every family an alarm clock. Yet Old Chen went out punctually every evening, undeterred by wind or rain, as if guarding not time but some older order. Mother told me he’d been the town’s primary school Chinese teacher; unable to sit idle after retirement, he took on the watchman’s duties. He made the lantern himself—bamboo-strip frame, peach-blossom paper, candle stub in the base. Its light was warm-toned, not sharp like electric light but soft, ringed with an orange-yellow halo, pushing back a small piece of darkness. Winter nights were hardest. He’d wrap an oilcloth cloak over his padded jacket, hang the lantern on a curved bamboo pole. When snow fell heavily, he left shallow footprints, covered by fresh snow by dawn—as if he’d never been. The year Old Chen died, the town grew quieter. Never again did that drawn-out ‘mind your candles’ drift from the alley’s depths. Only then did people realize the meaning of his rounds had never been telling time, but the reassurance his lantern gave countless late nights—the feeling that someone was watching over things.

options.text: 帮助失眠的人知道现在是几点

options.text_en: Helping insomniacs know what time it was

questions.q_zh: 老陈的灯笼光与电灯光有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How was Old Chen's lantern light different from electric light?

options.text: 灯笼光更亮能照得更远
options.pinyin: dēnglóng guāng gèng liàng néng zhào de gèng yuǎn
options.text_en: The lantern light was brighter and could illuminate further

options.text: 灯笼光是柔和的暖色带着橘黄的晕染温柔地推开黑暗
options.pinyin: dēnglóng guāng shì róuhé de nuǎnsè dài zhe júhuáng de yūnrǎn wēnróu de tuī kāi hēi'àn

title: Ink Stones and Patience
text_zh: 那方端砚是曾祖父传下来的，石质细腻如婴儿的皮肤，砚堂中央因为百余年的研磨而形成了一个浅浅的凹陷，那是无数次墨条旋转留下的弧形轨迹。父亲说，一方好砚的价值不在于它的雕工或者石品，而在于它是否被认真地使用过——就像一个人的价值不在于他的外貌或出身，而在于他是否被岁月认真地打磨过。研墨是一门被现代人遗忘的功课。如今的书法爱好者大多使用瓶装墨汁，方便快捷，倒出来就能写。但父亲坚持认为，省略了研墨的过程，就等于省略了书法最重要的准备仪式。他把研墨比作冥想的前奏：「你还没有动笔就已经开始写了。手上那个匀速的、反复的圆圈动作，会把你从外面纷乱的世界里一点一点地拉回来，拉回到当下这一刻，拉回到你和这块石头之间。」我试过几次，发现研墨确实需要一种特殊的耐心。太快了墨汁粗糙不匀；太慢了墨浓不够。力度也有讲究——太重会伤砚面，太轻则磨不出墨来。它要求你进入一种不急不缓的状态，既不能走神也不能用力过猛，恰恰是这种状态最难维持。现代生活教会我们的都是如何更快地完成事情，而研墨教会的恰恰相反——它教你如何慢下来，如何在一个看似无意义的重复动作中找到内心的锚点。父亲年纪大了以后手会微微颤抖，但他研墨的动作依然稳定。我问他为什么，他说：「因为这个动作我做了五十年，它已经不需要经过大脑了。手自己知道该怎么转，就像你走路不需要思考先迈哪只脚一样。」
text_pinyin: Nà fāng duānyàn shì zēngzǔfù chuán xiàlái de, shízhì xìnì rú yīng'ér de pífū, yàntáng zhōngyāng yīnwèi bǎi yú nián de yánmó ér xíngchéng le yí gè qiǎnqiǎn de āoxiàn, nà shì wúshù cì mòtiáo xuánzhuǎn liú xià de húxíng guǐjì. Fùqīn shuō, yī fāng hǎo yàn de jiàzhí bú zàiyú tā de diāogōng huòzhě shípǐn, ér zàiyú tā shìfǒu bèi rènzhēn de shǐyòng guò——jiù xiàng yí gè rén de jiàzhí bú zàiyú tā de wàimào huò chūshēn, ér zàiyú tā shìfǒu bèi suìyuè rènzhēn de dǎmó guò. Yán mò shì yī mén bèi xiàndài rén yíwàng de gōngkè. Rújīn de shūfǎ àihào zhě dàduō shǐyòng píngzhuāng mòzhī, fāngbiàn kuàijié, dào chūlái jiù néng xiě. Dàn fùqīn jiānchí rènwéi, shěnglüè le yán mò de guòchéng, jiù děngyú shěnglüè le shūfǎ zuì zhòngyào de zhǔnbèi yíshì. Tā bǎ yán mò bǐ zuò míngxiǎng de qiánzòu:「Nǐ hái méiyǒu dòng bǐ jiù yǐjīng kāishǐ xiě le. Shǒu shàng nàge yúnsù de, fǎnfù de yuánquān dòngzuò, huì bǎ nǐ cóng wàimiàn fēnluàn de shìjiè lǐ yīdiǎn yīdiǎn de lā huílái, lā huí dào dāngxià zhè yī kè, lā huí dào nǐ hé zhè kuài shítou zhījiān.」Wǒ shì guò jǐ cì, fāxiàn yán mò quèshí xūyào yī zhǒng tèshū de nàixīn. Tài kuài le mòzhī cūcāo bù yún; tài màn le mò nóng búgòu. Lìdù yě yǒu jiǎngjiu——tài zhòng huì shāng yàn miàn, tài qīng zé mó bù chū mò lái. Tā yāoqiú nǐ jìnrù yī zhǒng bú jí bù huǎn de zhuàngtài, jì bù néng zǒushén yě bù néng yònglì guòměng, qiàqià shì zhè zhǒng zhuàngtài zuì nán wéichí. Xiàndài shēnghuó jiàohuì wǒmen de dōu shì rúhé gèng kuài de wánchéng shìqíng, ér yán mò jiàohuì de qiàqià xiāngfǎn——tā jiào nǐ rúhé màn xiàlái, rúhé zài yí gè kàn sì wú yìyì de chóngfù dòngzuò zhōng zhǎodào nèixīn de máodiǎn. Fùqīn niánjì dà le yǐhòu shǒu huì wēiwēi chàndǒu, dàn tā yán mò de dòngzuò yīrán wěndìng. Wǒ wèn tā wèishénme, tā shuō:「Yīnwèi zhège dòngzuò wǒ zuò le wǔshí nián, tā yǐjīng bù xūyào jīngguò dànǎo le. Shǒu zìjǐ zhīdào gāi zěnme zhuǎn, jiù xiàng nǐ zǒulù bù xūyào sīkǎo xiān mài nǎ zhī jiǎo yíyàng.」
text_en: That Duan inkstone was passed down from my great-grandfather. Its stone is fine as an infant’s skin, and at the center of the grinding surface a shallow depression has formed from over a hundred years of grinding—arc-shaped traces of countless ink-stick rotations. Father said a good inkstone’s value lies not in carving or stone quality, but in whether it has been earnestly used—as a person’s value lies not in appearance but in whether they’ve been polished by the years. Grinding ink is a forgotten discipline. Most calligraphers today use bottled ink—pour and write. But father insisted skipping the grinding was skipping calligraphy’s most important ritual. He compared it to meditation’s prelude: ‘You’ve begun writing before picking up the brush. That steady circular motion pulls you back from the chaotic outside world to this present moment, to the space between you and this stone.’ I tried it. Grinding ink demands a special patience. Too fast and the ink is coarse; too slow and it won’t reach the right density. Pressure matters—too heavy damages the surface, too light produces no ink. It demands a state neither hurried nor sluggish, neither drifting nor pressing—precisely the state hardest to maintain. Modern life teaches finishing faster; ink grinding teaches the opposite—how to slow down, how to find an inner anchor in seemingly meaningless repetition. As father aged, his hands trembled slightly, yet his grinding remained steady. I asked why. ‘Fifty years of this motion. It no longer passes through the brain. The hand knows how to turn on its own, just as you don’t think about which foot to step with when you walk.’

questions.q_zh: 父亲为什么把研墨比作冥想的前奏？
questions.q_en: Why does the father compare ink grinding to a prelude to meditation?

options.text: 因为研墨时需要闭上眼睛集中精神
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yán mò shí xūyào bì shàng yǎnjīng jízhōng jīngshén
options.text_en: Because you need to close your eyes and concentrate while grinding ink

options.text: 因为匀速重复的动作能把人从纷乱中拉回到当下
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yúnsù chóngfù de dòngzuò néng bǎ rén cóng fēnluàn zhōng lā huí dào dāngxià
options.text_en: Because the steady, repetitive motion pulls a person back from chaos to the present moment

options.text: 因为墨的香味有助于放松和入睡
options.pinyin: yīnwèi mò de xiāngwèi yǒuzhùyú fàngsōng hé rùshuì
options.text_en: Because the fragrance of ink helps with relaxation and falling asleep

questions.explanation: Ink grinding's steady, repetitive motion 'pulls a person back from chaos to the present moment,' functioning as a meditative preparation.

questions.q_zh: 父亲年纪大了手会颤抖但研墨动作依然稳定，他怎么解释？
questions.q_en: Father's hands trembled with age yet his ink grinding remained steady—how did he explain this?

options.text: 他吃了治疗手抖的药物
options.pinyin: tā chī le zhìliáo shǒu dǒu de yàowù
options.text_en: He took medication for hand tremors

options.text: 这个动作做了五十年已经不需要经过大脑手自己知道该怎么转
options.pinyin: zhège dòngzuò zuò le wǔshí nián yǐjīng bù xūyào jīngguò dànǎo shǒu zìjǐ zhīdào gāi zěnme zhuǎn
options.text_en: He had done this motion for fifty years and it no longer needed the brain—the hand knew how to turn on its own

options.text: 研墨的时候他会用另一只手扶住手腕
options.pinyin: yán mò de shíhou tā huì yòng lìng yī zhī shǒu fú zhù shǒuwàn
options.text_en: He would steady his wrist with the other hand while grinding ink

questions.explanation: The father explains that fifty years of repetition transferred the knowledge from conscious control to the hand itself, making trembling irrelevant.

title: The Last Ferry of the Evening
text_zh: 镇上的渡船每天最后一班是傍晚六点。到了秋冬时节，六点钟天色已经暗下来了，江面上只剩下一片深沉的灰蓝色，远处的山峦变成了墨色的剪影。渡船是一条老旧的木船，船身漆着斑驳的红漆，船头挂着一盏马灯，在暮色中晃晃悠悠地画着不规则的光圈。撑船的老周是个寡言的人，一年四季都穿着同一件洗得发白的蓝布褂子，戴一顶竹笠。他撑船不用桨而用篙——一根长约三米的竹竿，一端包着铁皮。他把篙插进河底的淤泥里，双臂用力一撑，船就无声地向前滑出一段。这个动作反复循环，形成了一种平稳的、催眠般的节奏。末班渡船上的乘客大多是从对岸集市归来的村民，挑着空了的箩筐，脸上带着一天劳作后的倦意。没有人说话，只有水声和篙声交替响着。偶尔有一只白鹭从芦苇丛中惊起，拍着翅膀掠过水面，在暮色中划出一道白色的弧线。船到对岸，老周用篙把船头顶住岸边的石阶，等所有人都下了船，才把马灯拎到船尾，点上一支烟，坐在船板上独自抽完。那支烟的火光在江面的暗色里格外醒目，像是黄昏最后的一个标点。我后来离开了那个小镇，去了城市生活。有一年回去，听说渡口已经停运了——上游修了桥，没有人再需要坐渡船过江。老周也早已不在了。但每到黄昏时分，我总会不由自主地想起那条暮色中缓缓移动的老木船，想起那盏在江风里摇曳的马灯。那种画面有一种无法被任何现代交通工具替代的诗意——它属于一个更慢的年代，一个人们还愿意等一条船的年代。
text_pinyin: Zhèn shàng de dùchuán měi tiān zuìhòu yī bān shì bàngwǎn liù diǎn. Dào le qiūdōng shíjié, liù diǎn zhōng tiānsè yǐjīng àn xiàlái le, jiāngmiàn shàng zhǐ shèng xià yí piàn shēnchén de huīlán sè, yuǎnchù de shānluán biàn chéng le mòsè de jiǎnyǐng. Dùchuán shì yī tiáo lǎojiù de mùchuán, chuánshēn qī zhe bānbó de hóngqī, chuántóu guà zhe yī zhǎn mǎdēng, zài mùsè zhōng huànghuangyōuyōu de huà zhe bù guīzé de guāngquān. Chēngchuán de Lǎo Zhōu shì gè guǎyán de rén, yī nián sìjì dōu chuān zhe tóngyī jiàn xǐ de fābái de lánbù guàzi, dài yī dǐng zhúlì. Tā chēngchuán bú yòng jiǎng ér yòng gāo——yī gēn cháng yuē sān mǐ de zhúgān, yī duān bāo zhe tiěpí. Tā bǎ gāo chā jìn hédǐ de yūní lǐ, shuāng bì yònglì yī chēng, chuán jiù wúshēng de xiàng qián huá chū yí duàn. Zhège dòngzuò fǎnfù xúnhuán, xíngchéng le yī zhǒng píngwěn de, cuīmián bān de jiézòu. Mòbān dùchuán shàng de chéngkè dàduō shì cóng duì'àn jíshì guīlái de cūnmín, tiāo zhe kōng le de luókuāng, liǎn shàng dài zhe yī tiān láozuò hòu de juànyì. Méiyǒu rén shuōhuà, zhǐ yǒu shuǐ shēng hé gāo shēng jiāotì xiǎng zhe. Ǒu'ěr yǒu yī zhī báilù cóng lúwěi cóng zhōng jīng qǐ, pāi zhe chìbǎng lüè guò shuǐmiàn, zài mùsè zhōng huá chū yí dào báisè de húxiàn. Chuán dào duì'àn, Lǎo Zhōu yòng gāo bǎ chuántóu dǐng zhù àn biān de shíjiē, děng suǒyǒu rén dōu xià le chuán, cái bǎ mǎdēng līn dào chuánwěi, diǎn shàng yī zhī yān, zuò zài chuánbǎn shàng dúzì chōu wán. Nà zhī yān de huǒguāng zài jiāngmiàn de ànsè lǐ géwài xǐngmù, xiàng shì huánghūn zuìhòu de yí gè biāodiǎn. Wǒ hòulái líkāi le nàge xiǎo zhèn, qù le chéngshì shēnghuó. Yǒu yī nián huíqù, tīngshuō dùkǒu yǐjīng tíngyùn le——shàngyóu xiū le qiáo, méiyǒu rén zài xūyào zuò dùchuán guò jiāng. Lǎo Zhōu yě zǎoyǐ búzài le. Dàn měi dào huánghūn shífēn, wǒ zǒng huì bùyóuzìzhǔ de xiǎng qǐ nà tiáo mùsè zhōng huǎnhuǎn yídòng de lǎo mùchuán, xiǎng qǐ nà zhǎn zài jiāngfēng lǐ yáoyè de mǎdēng. Nà zhǒng huàmiàn yǒu yī zhǒng wúfǎ bèi rènhé xiàndài jiāotōng gōngjù tìdài de shīyì——tā shǔyú yí gè gèng màn de niándài, yí gè rénmen hái yuànyì děng yī tiáo chuán de niándài.
text_en: The last ferry departed at six in the evening. By autumn, the sky had darkened—only deep gray-blue on the river’s surface, distant mountains reduced to ink silhouettes. The ferry was an old wooden boat, hull in mottled red lacquer, a hurricane lamp at the bow swaying and tracing circles of light. Old Zhou the boatman wore the same faded blue cotton jacket through all seasons, topped with a bamboo hat. He propelled the boat with a punt pole—bamboo, about three meters, iron-tipped. He’d plunge it into the riverbed mud, push with both arms, and the boat glided forward silently. This motion repeated in a steady, hypnotic rhythm. Passengers on the last ferry were mostly villagers returning from market—empty baskets on shoulder poles, faces marked with a day’s weariness. No one spoke; only water and punt pole alternated. Occasionally an egret started from the reeds, tracing a white arc through the dusk. At the far bank, Old Zhou pressed the bow against stone steps with his pole, waited until everyone disembarked, then carried the lamp to the stern, lit a cigarette, and sat on the deck alone. The glow was especially vivid against the river’s dark tones—the last punctuation mark of dusk. Years later I returned and learned the crossing had shut down. A bridge upstream; no one needed the ferry. Old Zhou was long gone. But every evening at dusk, I think of that boat moving through twilight, that lamp swaying in the river wind. That image holds a poetry no modern transport can replace—it belongs to a slower era, when people were still willing to wait for a boat.

questions.q_zh: 老周每天末班渡船结束后会做什么？
questions.q_en: What did Old Zhou do after the last ferry run each day?

options.text: 立刻回家吃晚饭
options.pinyin: lìkè huí jiā chī wǎnfàn
options.text_en: Immediately went home for dinner

options.text: 把马灯拎到船尾独自坐着抽一支烟
options.pinyin: bǎ mǎdēng līn dào chuánwěi dúzì zuò zhe chōu yī zhī yān
options.text_en: Carried the hurricane lamp to the stern and sat alone smoking a cigarette

options.text: 把船拉上岸仔细检查船底
options.pinyin: bǎ chuán lā shàng àn zǐxì jiǎnchá chuándǐ
options.text_en: Pulled the boat ashore and carefully inspected the hull

questions.explanation: Old Zhou carried the hurricane lamp to the stern and sat alone smoking a cigarette after completing the last crossing.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为那条渡船的画面有一种什么样的特质？
questions.q_en: What quality does the author believe that image of the ferry possesses?

options.text: 一种令人恐惧的孤独感
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng lìng rén kǒngjù de gūdú gǎn
options.text_en: A frightening sense of loneliness

options.text: 一种无法被现代交通工具替代的诗意
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng wúfǎ bèi xiàndài jiāotōng gōngjù tìdài de shīyì
options.text_en: A poetry that no modern form of transportation can replace

options.text: 一种对落后交通方式的不满和批判
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng duì luòhòu jiāotōng fāngshì de bùmǎn hé pīpàn
options.text_en: A dissatisfaction and criticism of backward transportation

questions.explanation: The ferry scene possesses a poetic quality—lamplight on water, wooden hull, evening silence—that no modern transport can replicate.
